body:
# What is this?
This discussion is for companies looking for people with Next.js experience. Both permanent positions and contract work are welcome.

# Companies
Please use the following format followed by a brief description of the role:

> COMPANY_NAME | ROLE | LOCATION | Full-time | $100-150k base | LINK

Please only post if you personally are part of the hiring company—no recruiting firms or job boards. Only one post per company.

Good luck with recruiting!

## Sample Job Post

Here's a good example job post.

> Vercel | Senior Full-Stack Engineer | Remote (US, Canada, Europe, or Asia) | Full-time | $100-225k base | [Link](https://vercel.com/careers/senior-full-stack-engineer-amer)
>
> Vercel’s hiring across the board on various engineering teams and positions, but the above link is for our full-stack engineering position. Vercel is innovating on and for the web and at the edge. We build tools and workflows developers love and deliver outstanding performance for end users.
>
> I've been at the company for 2 years and still find plenty of opportunity for impact, and love the teammates I'm working with. Feel free to apply using the link above or reach out to me directly.

*If you're looking for last month's discussion, see #32093. At least one company has [hired someone](https://twitter.com/Nutlope/status/1475929984174895105) as a result of it!* 

If you're a developer looking to post about themselves, use #32938.

title: Next.js execution model
body:
👋  - we've started Next.js in production at the company I work at and I've been trying to understand the guts of how things work. Our setup in particular has us using:

 - A custom next.js server mounted on Express
 - Next.js API routes
 - Pages w/ getServerSideProps
 - The app is compiled with `target: server`

To make sure we're able to properly operationalize this all, I've been trying to understand what Next.js execution model is. How the server is compiled/bundled and how it runs. I was wondering if there's a doc describing that somewhere[0]? I haven't been able to find anything like that online.

[0] In lieu of a doc, I was planning to read the code itself but was hoping for some way of bootstrapping my mental model to make that more productive.

-------

As an example of why I care about this. We recently ran into an issue w/ knex (https://github.com/knex/knex) being bundled using webpack. This led to the following thread: knex/knex#1128, which had a potentially great solution (tell webpack the modules that knex would need are external). It was unclear to me if that would be okay in the Next execution model.

title: Image optimization URL doesn't work when images introduced to file system after nextJS server started
body:
### What version of Next.js are you using?

12.0.7

### What version of Node.js are you using?

16.3.0

### What browser are you using?

all

### What operating system are you using?

linux

### How are you deploying your application?

yarn dev / yarn build && yarn start

### Describe the Bug

NextJS response with optimized images that existed on the file system BEFORE I started the server. If I upload an image and place it in the `public/uploads/foobar.jpg` path when I attempt to display that image on the page it just hangs and doesn't render anything. If I restart the server and access the URL the image is rendered as expected.

I noticed when I try to access an image that doesn't exist the connection hangs indefinitely.

I also noticed that when if I'm running the build version (not dev) of nextjs and I introduce a file after the server is running and attempt to access it I get this error in the console:

```
TypeError: res.hasHeader is not a function
    at setRevalidateHeaders (/path/to/app/node_modules/next/dist/server/send-payload.js:18:37)
    at Object.sendRenderResult (/path/to/app/node_modules/next/dist/server/send-payload.js:51:9)
```


### Expected Behavior

Expect images to be rendered that get uploaded after the next server is already running. Also when an image is missing to respond with 404Expect images to be rendered that get uploaded after the next server is already running. Also when an image is missing to respond with 404

### To Reproduce

1. start next server
2. Upload image into public/uploads/abc.jpg
3. access an image optimize URL. eg. http://localhost:3000/_next/image?url=/uploads/abc.jpg&w=640&q=75
4. restart the server and the image will render as expected

title: Cache: Purge cache manually
body:
### Problem
If you export an async function called **getStaticProps** from a page, Next.js will pre-render this page at build time using the props returned by **getStaticProps**. If you would like to update the cache, next provide **revalidate** property that is an optional amount in seconds after which a page re-generation can occur. That is not so good because:

1. The getStaticProps function is called unnecessarily and the same page is regenerate and store in the cache.
2. When the data(content of blog for example) that depend page change, the page don’t change directly and some users can continue to read outdated information.
3. It is sometimes difficult to choose a number of seconds for re-validation. If you make a blog for example, some articles will need to be updated faster than others.

### Proposition
Next.js can provide boolean(default false) property **purge** in getStaticProps payload. 
When some URL like https://www.website.com/article/slug-1 is call with DELETE method and purge property is true in payload of getStaticProps in articles/[slugs].js, Next.js delete the cache. 

If routes is called with PUT method, and purge is true Next.js delete the cache and regenerate the page.  

### Alternative
I have write an article that explain how we can purge the cache of Next.js specific route. 
https://medium.com/@briceguekam/next-js-cache-optimization-7f14d2113f74#8995-5d7d7acfede9

title: Storybook and Next/Image: Impossible to have an Image without width and height in Storybook
body:
### Run `next info` (available from version 12.0.8 and up)

_No response_

### What version of Next.js are you using?

12.0.7

### What version of Node.js are you using?

16.13.1

### What browser are you using?

Chrome

### What operating system are you using?

macOS

### How are you deploying your application?

next start

### Describe the Bug

When I add a local image with the next/image component without specify width and height according to [https://nextjs.org/docs/basic-features/image-optimization#local-images](url) I always have the error:
`Image with src "static/media/public/images/x.png" must use "width" and "height" properties or "layout='fill'" property.` on Storybook.

I don't have any problem outside Storybook.

### Expected Behavior

I would like not to add, just for Storybook, width and height for local image.

### To Reproduce

- Use the example `with-storybook` [https://github.com/vercel/next.js/tree/canary/examples/with-storybook](url)
- In `public` directory, add an image 
- In `index.js`, import this image and use the `next/image` component with only `src`, according to [https://nextjs.org/docs/basic-features/image-optimization#local-images](url)
- In `.storybook/main.js`, add `staticDirs: ["../public"]`
- In `.storybook/preview.js`, add
```
import * as NextImage from "next/image";

const OriginalNextImage = NextImage.default;

Object.defineProperty(NextImage, "default", {
  configurable: true,
  value: (props) => <OriginalNextImage {...props} unoptimized />,
});
```
- Launch Storybook

title: [Feature Request] Add an programmatic API to share context between prerender workers during `getStaticPaths` and `getStaticProps`
body:
### Describe the feature you'd like to request

RT.

Context: https://github.com/vercel/next.js/discussions/32981

In short, I am using a node library at local as my backend CMS. Unlike requesting a remote GraphQL endpoint or querying a database (which is damn slow), it is *FAAAAASSSSST enough* that I need just one instance. With the Next.js multi-thread feature when prerendering the static page, starting multiple instances of the library actually makes the build slower.

**Related context**

- The content of Next.js worker where every component is individual: https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/canary/packages/next/export/worker.ts
- Where Next.js starts multiple instances of the pretender worker:
https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/cfa8ab9cbfb8818408654e223aa9253be619879e/packages/next/build/index.ts#L780-L791

### Describe the solution you'd like

Allow `getStaticProps` and `getStaticPaths` to have a shared context where I can put the local node library CMS in.

### Describe alternatives you've considered

An option to disable the multi-thread feature completely.

module.exports = {

            'cdn-static.hesapkurdu.com',
            'qm1n93v26l.mncdn.com',
            'cdn.hesapkurdu.com',
            'localhost',
            'localhost:3000'
        ],
    },
    poweredByHeader: false,
    swcMinify: true,

title: Javascript randomly not executed on production builds
body:
We're seeing an issue occur more frequently with our Next.js production builds.  All is well when we're working in dev mode, but when we go to do a production build, some pages will, randomly, fail to execute any of the Javascript on the page, making any interactive features non-functional.  We have yet to figure out what's causing this and would appreciate any suggestions on how to debug the issue!

Often we can isolate the issue to a specific component or provider, and removing that component solves the issue for the particular page (not ideal)...however that exact same component works fine on other pages within the application.  My suspicion is that there's something about the way that Next.js is running Webpack and/or using Babel to transpile the code into the various chunks loaded on the production builds...but I'm not sure how to debug that!

Another hypothesis is that there's something about the way Next.js is doing server side rendering (SSR).  In the particular case I was debugging yesterday, when I wrapped the main page component in a `dynamic` call, the page started responding to clicks, etc. again. 

Some version details:
React: 16.14.0
Next.js: 10.2.3
Node: 16.6.1
NPM: 7.20.3
webpack: 5.37.1
@babel/core: 7.6.2

Any help is greatly appreciated!

title: Looking for help with ES Module
body:
I'm the main contributor to a Next.js open-source package called [`next-multilingual`](https://github.com/Avansai/next-multilingual). Recently, we updated to Next.js 12+ and I noticed that the localized URLs were no longer working in the SSR markup.

I managed to pinpoint the issue to `11.1.3-canary.69` and more specifically to this [commit](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/pull/29878) which enables `experimental: { esmExternals: true }` by default. Since this change, our Webpack config that allows SSR to work has started to silently fail and I have been looking for a solution for a few days now which is why I am asking for help.

One of the ideas I had was to add ESM support to our package but I've been running into issue after issue which is why I am ready to pay an expert to help us solve this problem.

Please let me know if you are interested, or have ideas on how to unblock me in any form or shape :)

body:
### Run `next info` (available from version 12.0.8 and up)

_No response_

### What version of Next.js are you using?

none

### What operating system are you using?

FreeBSD 12.2-RELEASE-p5

npm run build (next build)

### Describe the Bug

After migrating from version 11.1.2 to 12.0.7, I've started getting permission error when building next app. That's true, I don't have permission to /usr/home, because it's hosting server. I actually have permission to /usr/home/USER.

I've reproduced this issue on new, clean project created using `yarn create next-app --typescript` command. One thing I had to do was to disable SWC (probably not having impact on the issue).

Trying again on version 11.1.2 passed without issues.

```
> next build

warn  - No ESLint configuration detected. Run next lint to begin setup

info  - Creating an optimized production build ...<w> [webpack.cache.PackFileCacheStrategy] Caching failed for pack: Error: Unable to snapshot resolve dependencies
info  - Creating an optimized production build  
info  - Compiled successfully

> Build error occurred
[Error: EACCES: permission denied, readlink '/usr/home'] {
  errno: -13,
  code: 'EACCES',
  syscall: 'readlink',
  path: '/usr/home'
}
info  - Collecting page data .npm ERR! code ELIFECYCLE

npm ERR! 
npm ERR! Failed at the test_app@ build script.

npm ERR! A complete log of this run can be found in:

```



### Expected Behavior

Building project does not require any parent directory access permissions.

### To Reproduce

Probably restricting access to parent directories (/usr/home) and trying to build a project.

title: How is getServerSideProps going to work with React 18 concurrency, streaming and suspense?
body:
I was recently dissapointed to discover that getServerSideProps is synchronous and blocks rendering of components before it completes its internal fetches. Although documented as such there appears to be no way to return a promise to a component in place of data which may take time to retrieve on the server. It doesnt appear to offer any granularity on the partial rendering that Suspense offers.

Do the new React 18 concurrency features make getServerSideProps obsolete? I cant see a use for a blocking initialisation once use of suspense and dynamic imports becomes the norm.

Or does the new Suspense component offer an opportunity to reinvent getServerSideProps to work with Suspense and stream data to its component?

Thanks

author: sebsonjura

title: Backward compatible ESM support
body:
### Describe the feature you'd like to request

Since Next.js `11.1.3-canary.69`, ESM externals has been turned on by default using the `experimental: { esmExternals: true }` in this [commit](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/pull/29878).

One of the challenges is that the module resolutions between CommonJS and ESM is different. From Node [documentation](https://nodejs.org/api/esm.html):

> The current specifier resolution does not support all default behavior of the CommonJS loader. One of the behavior differences is automatic resolution of file extensions and the ability to import directories that have an index file.

By having this, it means that you could end up with different import paths depending on if you use CommonJS or ESM which will be a really bad developer experience. On top of this, to provide the correct support for Next.js, modules would have to be backward compatible since not all packages support ESM today.

### Describe the solution you'd like

It looks like the way to do would be to support `--experimental-specifier-resolution=node` but from looking at previous issues ([here](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/issues/9607) and [here](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/issues/9487)), it does not seem to work with Next.js (not sure why).

I was not able to get it to work myself, so maybe we could have this as a new experimental option that is supported by Next.js?

### Describe alternatives you've considered

I tried to build a backward compatible package using this [article](https://www.sensedeep.com/blog/posts/2021/how-to-create-single-source-npm-module.html) but ended up with clunky paths which degraded the developer experience.

What I mean by clunky paths is:

- For example, if we have an import that points to `next-multilingual/messages` we would now need to use `next-multilingual/messages.js`
- To get `next-multilingual/messages.js` to work we would need to remove the file in `src/messages/index.js` and move it to `src/messages.js` which then prevents us from using directories to organize files which will make the project messier
- Or we could use `next-multilingual/messages/messages.js` to keep the directory but this makes the import even harder to read and less intuitive

I believe the experimental flag would help resolve some of these issues and is worth trying.

title: An SWC enabled solution for Inline SVGs
body:
Turning this into a separate discussion from: https://github.com/vercel/next.js/discussions/30174#discussioncomment-1900852

Seeing lots of upvotes, but I'm not sure it gets in front of people who could help.

> I feel like I am missing something very obvious, but I can't find out what, so please excuse me asking:
> We're down to inline-react-svg being our last babel plugin. Inlining SVGs is necessary to modify the SVGs with CSS/props as far as I know. It's very important for us to use SVGs for icons/interface components, and to be able to pretty much just copy them from Figma.
> Now I only see a few people in [here](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/discussions/30174) using this plugin. I don't see any mention on the official Next.js docs how to use SVGs, other than `<Image src="/icon.svg" />`.
It's also not mentioned in the [original post](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/discussions/30174) as something being on the roadmap for the swc based compiler.
> That leaves me wondering, whether we're doing something completely outdated. How are you dealing with icons, if not SVGs? Is this really not covered by Next.js (without babel/webpack) somewhere?
How would we make this work with swc?
> The only option I see right now is converting the svgs to jsx and then inlining them into the source code. Is that really the way to go?
> Helpful for any hint. We really want to move to swc 🤞

title: Add support for middleware rewrite proxy headers
body:
### Describe the feature you'd like to request

I would like to be able to specify headers to be sent by the proxy that is running behind the `NextResponse.rewrite` middleware response.

I understand it's possible to set headers in the `NextResponse.rewrite` response, but these headers are sent to the user visiting the next.js page, what I would like is to set headers that the destination server will receive.

Upon digging into the next.js internals, I understand we'd need to provide such headers in the `Proxy` instance within `proxyRequest` inside `/packages/next/server/base-server.ts`

### Describe the solution you'd like

I would like to be able to, perhaps, provide a config object as second parameter of `NextResponse.rewrite` which could accept something like `forwardHeaders` that accepts a Headers object and this would then be merged with the headers that the proxy may already need to send.

### Describe alternatives you've considered

Currently, my alternative solution is preform this rewrite using nginx and provide the headers there. If these headers can be forwarded within nextjs, I believe this feature will be even more powerful.

author: glenngijsberts

title: RFC: Change default JS/CSS output to target modern browsers
body:
**Edit:** This shipped in Next.js 13! Learn more: https://nextjs.org/blog/next-13

---


## Background

Next.js currently targets [ES5](https://www.w3schools.com/js/js_es5.asp) for JS output and autoprefixes CSS in order to support legacy browsers. This results in much larger JS and CSS output because newer features need to be down-leveled.

For example, ES5 doesn’t include many of the JavaScript syntax features we use today such as [async/await](https://babeljs.io/repl/#?browsers=defaults%2C%20not%20ie%2011%2C%20not%20ie_mob%2011&build=&builtIns=false&corejs=3.6&spec=false&loose=false&code_lz=IYZwngdgxgBAZgV2gFwJYHsIwBYFMA2-6AFAJQwDeAUDDFJiOvrgHREDmxA5Hoel6QDcVAL5A&debug=false&forceAllTransforms=false&shippedProposals=false&circleciRepo=&evaluate=false&fileSize=false&timeTravel=false&sourceType=script&lineWrap=true&presets=env%2Creact&prettier=false&targets=Node-6.10&version=7.16.7&externalPlugins=&assumptions=%7B%7D), [Object rest/spread](https://babeljs.io/repl/#?browsers=defaults%2C%20not%20ie%2011%2C%20not%20ie_mob%2011&build=&builtIns=false&corejs=3.6&spec=false&loose=false&code_lz=MYewdgzgLgBMA24CmMC8MDeMB0uCGATgOYQwC-AUKJCPEtokQBQLICUA3EA&debug=false&forceAllTransforms=false&shippedProposals=false&circleciRepo=&evaluate=false&fileSize=false&timeTravel=false&sourceType=script&lineWrap=true&presets=env%2Creact&prettier=false&targets=Node-4&version=7.16.7&externalPlugins=&assumptions=%7B%7D), [for-of loop](https://babeljs.io/repl/#?browsers=defaults%2C%20not%20ie%2011%2C%20not%20ie_mob%2011&build=&builtIns=false&corejs=3.6&spec=false&loose=false&code_lz=G4QwTgBOkLwQ2gcgPYDsCmiA0FEBcB3ZRAXQCgyAzZSACgBt08IBLPdAWwmUqjDACUEAN5kIEAMZoAzskYA6esgDmtNpwFkAvkA&debug=false&forceAllTransforms=false&shippedProposals=false&circleciRepo=&evaluate=false&fileSize=false&timeTravel=false&sourceType=script&lineWrap=true&presets=env%2Creact&prettier=false&targets=Node-4&version=7.16.7&externalPlugins=&assumptions=%7B%7D), [optional chaining](https://babeljs.io/repl/#?browsers=defaults%2C%20not%20ie%2011%2C%20not%20ie_mob%2011&build=&builtIns=false&corejs=3.6&spec=false&loose=false&code_lz=G4QwTgBA9gRgVhAvBA3gKAhAdiAtgUwC4IByAMyihLQF80BjKLAZygBt8A6NqAcwApYcAPyccBAJQBuIA&debug=false&forceAllTransforms=false&shippedProposals=false&circleciRepo=&evaluate=false&fileSize=false&timeTravel=false&sourceType=script&lineWrap=true&presets=env%2Creact&prettier=false&targets=Node-4&version=7.16.7&externalPlugins=&assumptions=%7B%7D), [nullish coalescing](https://babeljs.io/repl/#?browsers=defaults%2C%20not%20ie%2011%2C%20not%20ie_mob%2011&build=&builtIns=false&corejs=3.6&spec=false&loose=false&code_lz=G4QwTgBAdiC2CmEC8EDkAzA9p1AoAxplAM6YA28AdGZgOYAUMCEA_C2qgJQDcQA&debug=false&forceAllTransforms=false&shippedProposals=false&circleciRepo=&evaluate=false&fileSize=false&timeTravel=false&sourceType=script&lineWrap=true&presets=env%2Creact&prettier=false&targets=Node-4&version=7.16.7&externalPlugins=&assumptions=%7B%7D), etc.

In addition to syntax down-leveling, Next.js also [injects polyfills](https://nextjs.org/docs/basic-features/supported-browsers-features#polyfills) such as `Object.assign()`, `URL`, etc. However, those are only included in browsers that require them (using the [`nomodule`](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTML/Element/script#attr-nomodule) attribute).

## Proposal

We propose changing the default Next.js output to target modern browsers that support ES Modules, which accounts for [93%](https://caniuse.com/es6-module) of global usage:

- Edge 16+
- Firefox 60+
- Chrome 61+
- Safari 11+
- Opera 48+

This change would reduce JS/CSS bundle size for all applications that use the default Next.js configuration. Next.js will continue to autoprefix CSS for the supported browsers above when necessary.

Changing the default output target would align Next.js with [Vite](https://vitejs.dev/guide/build.html#browser-compatibility) and SvelteKit. In addition, most other tools don’t support ES5 such as [esbuild](https://esbuild.github.io/content-types/#es5) and [Angular](https://github.com/angular/angular/issues/41840).

The notable browser missing here is Internet Explorer which will soon reach [End Of Life](https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/lifecycle/faq/internet-explorer-microsoft-edge).

This would also fix obscure bugs caused by down-leveling to ES5 such as https://github.com/vercel/next.js/issues/30892.

*Note: This proposal is not suggesting changing the output to ESM, but rather using that feature as a way to determine which browsers we consider “modern”.*

## Backwards Compatibility

Changing default configuration is considered semver major and will not ship until Next.js 13.

However, Next.js will still maintain backwards compatibility for developers who wish to upgrade to Next.js 13 and continue supporting legacy browsers, such as IE 11. There are a few options:

1. **Opt-out flag:** Developers can provide `legacyBrowsers: true` in `next.config.js` to emit the same JS/CSS output as today. This could work exactly the same as today. Alternatively, it could create two builds: one modern `<script type=module>` and one legacy `<script nomodule>` , therefore increasing build times.
2. **Browserlist support:** Add built-in support for [browserslist](https://github.com/browserslist/browserslist). We already load `.browserslistrc` and `browserslist` from `package.json` for PostCSS, but don’t pass it to the JavaScript transformations (yet). Browserslist is already supported in SWC, so we’d only have to pass the browserslist loading result as an option. ([related discussion](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/discussions/12826))

## Release Timeline

Changing default configuration is considered semver major and will not ship until Next.js 13.

However, users may wish to adopt this change today, so we could provide a flag to enable the new behavior with Next.js 12. Similar to the options above but reversed:

1. Opt-in flag `legacyBrowsers: false` in `next.config.js`
2. Modify `.browserslistrc` or `browserslist` in `package.json`

## Feedback

We’d love to hear your feedback on which solution would work better for your team and why.

title: How to make this setup with NextJS?
body:
I have a requirement for a new website starting from scratch and I'm currently evaluating if NextJS will take away a lot of the bootstrapping and maintenance headache. I used to be very active with NextJS around versions v5 and v6 but I've dropped out of it quite some time ago. Now I see it has evolved a lot and I want to consider it for the next project.

There are several requirements that need to be fulfilled and if anyone can chime in with ideas or pointers if NextJS is a way to go, that would be amazing and highly appreciated.

The website will be split into two parts: public facing (www.domain.com) and user/admin panel (app.domain.com).

General requirements:
- Hosted in the cloud with easy maintenance and deployments (Vercel hosting maybe?)
- Database is Firebase Firestore (I have a lot of experience with it and it's in the cloud so scaling will not cause headache; it allows for realtime communication via subscription mechanism but that's not required for this project so regular CRUD will do the job)
- If possible it would be good to structure it under the single code base / single repo so that NextJS serves both parts of the website but differently as per requirements below

Public facing website requirements:
- Apart from typical static pages (about us, privacy policy, terms and conditions etc.) it will contain profile pages for each user where data comes from the database - profile pages are the core of the business model so this has to work flawlessly
- Blazingly fast content serving
- SEO-friendly pages

User/admin panel website requirements:
- Typical user/admin panel as a React app (it can be SPA but not a requirement)
- User login/signup
- User profile editing (data changes should be quickly propagated to the public facing website without the need to redeploy everything)
- No need for SEO

I was looking into SSR and SSG as features of the NextJS and I'm not quite sure what's the best approach to make use of NextJS for such project.

title: NextJS 12 results in less standalone setup for serverless environments than NextJS 11
body:
### Run `next info` (available from version 12.0.8 and up)

    Operating System:
      Platform: darwin
      Arch: x64
      Version: Darwin Kernel Version 19.6.0: Thu May  6 00:48:39 PDT 2021; root:xnu-6153.141.33~1/RELEASE_X86_64
    Binaries:
      Node: 14.17.5
      npm: 6.14.14
      Yarn: N/A
      pnpm: N/A
    Relevant packages:
      next: 12.0.8
      react: 17.0.2
      react-dom: 17.0.2


### What version of Next.js are you using?

12.0.8

### What version of Node.js are you using?

14.17.5

### What browser are you using?

Chrome

### What operating system are you using?

MacOS

### How are you deploying your application?

Other platform

### Describe the Bug

## Version 11

NextJS 11 had the `serverless` target which, despite some more dependencies than advertised, was an attempt at making  serverless functions from each dynamic page. The idea was that the serverless page could be deployed like a render function only without any resources. This was in fact almost right, even though some dependencies actually existed, which was reported here: https://github.com/vercel/next.js/issues/26297. Nonetheless, despite these extra resources, you could actually copy the render function along with about five or so dependency chunks and it all worked; you didn't even have to run a server to render the response.

Note well that NextJS adds some middleware to both API requests and API responses:

* https://nextjs.org/docs/api-routes/api-middlewares
* https://nextjs.org/docs/api-routes/response-helpers

An arbitrary API page written for NextJS will assume that these exist. In NextJS version 11, one of the dependency chunks that I talked about, that resides inside the `.next/serverless/pages/chunks` folder, enhances the `req` and `res` objects with this in function `apiResolver`.

To sum up, in version 11, all you needed was the following file system to serve a given page:

    .next
        serverless
            chunks
                 ... (about 5 chunks)
            pages                 
                api
                    ... (api pages)
                ... (regular pages)
    index.js (some file that reads an incoming message and then calls resources in `.next`)

## Version 12
Version 12 has ditched the `serverless` target at the benefit of [outputting a file for each page containing information about the dependencies](https://nextjs.org/docs/advanced-features/output-file-tracing). This way we will clearly see the needed dependencies and may copy them to the location where they're needed. There is also the option to let NextJS do this for us, whereby a `standalone` folder with `node_modules` and the whole shebang is included. The problem now, no matter if we use the standalone option or not, the `apiResolver` method where the extra middleware is added has been moved to `node_modules/next/server/api-utils.js` which means we MUST include `node_modules` folder to get the expected NextJS equipped `req` and `res` objects in our standalone pages. It also seems that the `apiResolver` method, which was in version 11 called from the standalone api function itself, is now called from the `base-server` in the server package, indicating that we need to start a server to ornament the `req` and `res` objects with the necessary api middleware / helpers.

## Conundrum
It seems to me that from a general serverless perspective, NextJS just took a step backwards and we are with version 12 required to both keep a `node_modules` folder and also start a Node / Next server to be able to properly handle api requests whereas we could do the same in version 11 without both. Starting a server is more time consuming than just rendering the response from a function and keeping the `node_modules` folder around takes up more space than necessary as well. Perhaps given some specific serverless platform, this is a step in the right direction but if so, then it is a shame that it should be at the cost of a general usability in serverless contexts.

Please enlighten me if I have misunderstood something or if I'm wrong in any way, I would be really glad if I am :)

### Expected Behavior

Improved architecture from version 11, perhaps with the rendering functions actually being standalone as advertised instead of depending on the `.next/serverless/chunks` folder.

### To Reproduce

1. Start a new Next project with `npx create-next-app`
2. Run `next build`
3. Copy the contents of `.next` folder to a new folder `serverless`
4. Verify the dependencies for default api `hello` endpoint in `.next/server/pages/api/hello.js.nft.json` to be only the main `package.json` and `.next/server/webpack-api-runtime.js`
5. Delete all files in the `serverless` folder except `.next/server/webpack-api-runtime.js` and `.next/server/pages/api/hello.js`
6. At the root, create a serverless entrypoint `index.js` with content

        const renderer = require("./serverless/server/pages/api/hello")
        const http = require("http")

        const run = () => {
            const req = new http.IncomingMessage(null)
            const res = new http.ServerResponse(req)
            return renderer.default(req, res)
        }

        run()

7. Run `node index.js`
8. Verify error `res.status is not a function`

The default api endpoint makes use of NextJS specific `res.status()` ad `res.json()` which doesn't work since the `req` and `res` objects have not been ornamented with these extra capabilities through the `apiResolver` function.

Adding 

    module.exports = {
        experimental: {
            outputStandalone: true,
        }
    }

to `next.config.js` and using the entire `.next/standalone` in place of the above `serverless` folder does not change the results, indicating that we must indeed spawn a server (perhaps the included `server.js`) to get the embellished `req` and `res` objects that are expected, which in turn also suggests that we need to keep a `node_modules` folder around.

title: eslint rule for no unwanted polyfill.io does not fully cover the issue
body:
https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/34dcede6902fc1bc100a3a9d8874851c4bcd9977/packages/eslint-plugin-next/lib/rules/no-unwanted-polyfillio.js

Polyfill.io polyfills can have dependencies on features you are trying to warn users about.
Those would not be detected, giving a false sense of everything being ok.

If you want to help users correctly polyfill their code you can instead promote something build exactly for this issue : https://github.com/mrhenry/core-web :D

author: nandorojo

title: Process of converting Next.js examples to TypeScript?
body:
Reference: https://github.com/vercel/next.js/pull/33245#issuecomment-1012094076

Personally, I'm *not* in favor of converting examples to Typescript. Unless there are some extraordinary benefits to new Next.js projects (which I wager often start as prototytpes), speed of development is the most important metric and Typescript definitely slows this down. 

In addition, Next.js was created with the philosophy of *incredmental adoptability* – including Typescript goes against this philosophy. The framework's name is also `Next.js`, not `Next.ts`. I am in favor of the existing pattern of having Typescript examples alongisde the JavaScript ones (e.g. [blog-starter-typescript](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/tree/canary/examples/blog-starter-typescript)).

What does everyone else think?

author: TonyIngall

title: Why is router.isReady only intended to be used within useEffect?
body:
The docs for the router object returned by the `useRouter` hook mention the following about the isReady field:

> isReady: boolean - Whether the router fields are updated client-side and ready for use. Should only be used inside of useEffect methods and not for conditionally rendering on the server.

I imagine that this is to avoid rehydration issues, but I would also guess that `router.isReady` would always be false on the server and the initial value of `isReady` is also always false. Given that, it seems like it would be fine to use `isReady` outside of a useEffect method to conditionally render something. Can someone explain what I'm missing? Thanks!

        teams.tsx

title: Styled Components being reset on page refresh
body:
### Run `next info` (available from version 12.0.8 and up)

 Operating System:
      Platform: win32
      Arch: x64
      Version: Windows 11 Pro
    Binaries:
      Node: 16.13.1
      npm: 8.1.2
      Yarn: 1.22.17
      pnpm: 6.25.1
    Relevant packages:
      next: 12.0.7
      react: 17.0.2
      react-dom: 17.0.2

### What version of Next.js are you using?

12.0.7

### What version of Node.js are you using?

16.13.1

### What browser are you using?

Firefox, Chromium, Edge

### What operating system are you using?

Windows 11

### How are you deploying your application?

next dev

### Describe the Bug

I'm using Grommet (with Styled Components) to attempt to center an element. I'm actually copying over some old code that I had from plain React, which featured a centered input form. But for some reason when I attempt to center it, it works on saving the source, but whenever I refresh the page, the styles immediately revert back to before I applied the styles. The styles are then permanently not applied on the component.

### Box Component with centered items
```js
<Box flex align='center' width='large'>
   { props.children }
</Box>
```

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/43776043/149503101-f16ac30f-28d2-4cda-bcd9-3819f779b23e.png)

Putting `alignSelf` and `alignContent` as well as `justify` produces the same result of being centered, then going back to not having the styles applied. It keeps going back to the top left corner of the screen on page refresh.

As mentioned above I have gotten this to work in plain React as I've written a much older version of this app over there.

### Expected Behavior

I was thinking it would center the form permanently, not just upon saving the source, then reverting after a refresh. Here's the plain React version:

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/43776043/149504854-7b085c3d-9085-4d85-9959-fae91af156f0.png)


### To Reproduce

- By using Styled Components, align the component to the center, then save the changes. 
- Then refresh the page and it will return to the top left corner of the page.

### _app.js
```js
function MyApp({ Component, pageProps }) {
  return (
    <Provider>
      <Grommet full theme={ yellowJacket }>
        <DashboardTemplate>
          <Component {...pageProps} />
        </DashboardTemplate>
      </Grommet>
    </Provider>
  )
}

export default MyApp
```

### Template.jsx
```js
export default function DashboardTemplate(props) {
    //const [currentUser] = useAtom(userAtom)

    return ( 
        <Box height='100%' fill background='light-1'>
            {/*
                currentUser ?
                <Topbar /> :
                false
            */}
            {/* <Topbar /> */}
            <Box
                flex='grow'
                direction='row'
                overflow={{
                    horizontal: 'hidden'
                }}
            >
                {/*
                    currentUser ?
                    <Sidebar /> :
                    false
                */}
                {/* <Sidebar /> */}
                <Box flex align='center' justify='center' width='large'>
                    { props.children }
                </Box>
            </Box>
        </Box>
    )
}
```

### index.js (login form)
```js
export async function getServerSideProps(context) {
  return {
    props: {
      currentUser: null,
      sidebarToggle: true
    }
  }
}

export default function Home({ currentUser, sidebarToggle }) {
  useHydrateAtoms([
    [userAtom, currentUser],
    [sidebarAtom, sidebarToggle]
  ])

  return (
    <Box align='center'>
      <Avatar background='brand' margin={{ bottom: 'medium' }}>
        <Book />
      </Avatar>
      <Heading level='2' margin={{ bottom: 'medium' }}>
        Login Form :D
      </Heading>
      <Box background='neutral' align='center'>
        <Form onSubmit={ () => console.log("submission taking place yeah!") }>
          <FormField margin={{ top: 'small' }}>
            <TextInput
              name='username'
              placeholder='Username'
              type='text'
              required
            />
          </FormField>
          <FormField margin={{ top: 'medium' }}>
            <TextInput
              name='password'
              placeholder='Password'

title: window.gtag is not function
body:
As per the official docs, attempting to report web vitals to GA using the example found [here](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/tree/canary/examples/with-google-analytics) results in the error "window.gtag is not a function."

This has been a topic of discussion [in this repo](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/discussions/26778) and on [Stack Overflow](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/62791299/typeerror-window-gtag-is-not-a-function). There have been some workarounds such as...

```
if (window && window.gtag) {
        window.gtag('event', name, {
            event_category:
            label === 'web-vital' ? 'Web Vitals' : 'Next.js custom metric',
            value: Math.round(name === 'CLS' ? value * 1000 : value), // values must be integers
            event_label: id, // id unique to current page load
            non_interaction: true, // avoids affecting bounce rate.
        })
}
```

... but no satisfactory answers. If the example from the official docs has a problem it stands to reason that either the docs or the example should be altered. I would like to know more about the nature of this issue, so we don't have to use the workaround. It's clear that `reportWebVitals()` is executing before window.gtag is defined.

author: ZmonGitHub

title: Mocking and stubbing dependencies in server-side code during e2e tests
body:
While it is not officially documented on the [testing doc](https://nextjs.org/docs/testing), the [with-msw](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/tree/canary/examples/with-msw) example features how to mock http requests. This covers the case when you are simply using next.js to fetch and render data from a CMS like WordPress, but I failed to find any guides or discussions covering the topics of mocking generic dependencies in e2e tests.

Here are some of the potential use cases where you might want to mock something other than an http request in an e2e test:

1. Your next.js app interacts with a DBMS directly.
It is not an uncommon use case. It is also one mentioned in the documentation. While it can be argued that e2e tests need to cover everything from user click to persistence layer, mocking and stubbing database abstraction layer is a common practice since you don't really care about testing code that isn't yours, especially if it's not something between user and your app.
2. Your next.js app interacts with file system.
While not a common use case, [it is featured in official next.js learn course](https://nextjs.org/learn/basics/dynamic-routes/render-markdown). In a typical node application I would stub the relevant methods from `fs` and/or the library that does uses `fs` under the hood and call it a day.
3. Your next.js app sends http requests using some third party library.
This can be anything from an aws s3 sdk to stripe. These libraries send normal http requests and you can mock them with msw, but that would mean that you're essentially testing those libraries and not your own code. Additionally, if you update that library to a new major version and the javascript api changes, but under the hood it is still sending the same request, your test will pass, but in production you would get a runtime error. In all likelihood, if you're using typescript, it would fail to compile, but if we turn it the other way around (the api changes, but the library itself works), the tests would fail, but the production would code work flawlessly. So you need to mock the actual library.
4. Your next.js app sends emails or does any other non-http communication.
Assuming your test sends an actual email over smtp, the only logical way to verify that it was delivered would be to wait one minute, login to the recipients mailbox with a nodejs imap client and verify that the message was delivered. I don't think anyone actually does that. You need to mock your smtp library.

Possible workarounds:
* Use actual test database, maybe replace it with an in-memory one to make things faster.
This would require you to provide credentials for the test database in `.env.test` and to make sure your test runner passes `NODE_ENV=test` to build and start commands. You will also need to care about resetting the database after every test, if your tests update any records or documents. This workaround allows you to adjust the database state before each test (to test the cases when there is data and there is no data), if you are willing to initialise your database client during tests.
* Move all the non-http logic to a separate micro-service and mock your communication with it via msw.
This is probably the best solution for me so far as it allows you to have the highest confidence in your software system (assuming you are testing that micro-service too). This comes at a cost of development time and, if you are unable to make the client-side code to send requests directly to that micro-service, a delay in user interactions introduced by the next.js app relaying the requests.
* Add dummy data directly to the production code.
Assuming you have a function `getProducts` that fetches data from a database or parses json or a csv file, you can add an if statement that would check `APP_ENV === 'test'` and return some fake data.
This doesn't allow you customise the returned value on per-test basis, but does allow you tweak it depending on parameters that are passed to the function.

Just for reference, these are the usual tools for mocking dependencies, would be great if there was a way to use these or a similar library with next.js:
* [Sinon.JS](https://sinonjs.org/releases/v12.0.1/) - supports mocks, spies, stubs and fakes.
* [proxyquire](https://www.npmjs.com/package/proxyquire) - not as versatile as SinonJS, but gets the job done.

Thanks.

title: How do I set the path of the file generated by the shell script to the root of the site when hosting on Vercel?
body:
When running on localhost, if you set the output destination to public/, you can access the file from the root of the site.
```
exec(ffmpeg -i https://foo.com/input.mp4 -c copy public/output.mp4);
```
```
http://localhost:3000/output.mp4
```
However, when you build and deploy, the public folder will not be created, so you will not be able to generate files, in the site root.
```
exec(ffmpeg -i https://foo.com/input.mp4 -c copy public/output.mp4);
// public/output.mp4 No such file or directory
```
```
https://mysite/output.mp4
// Because the file has not been generated, of course, it cannot be accessed.
```
Could you please let me know if there is any way to solve this problem?  
Thank you.

title: Secret leak prevention out of the box
body:
Hi guys, do you think it would make sense to include a secret leak check out-of-the-box in Next.js? 

I've tried Gitleaks (https://github.com/VulcanJS/vulcan-next/issues/143), I am not yet 100% confident in it. I wonder if smth similar could be applied specifically to Next.js projects? Or maybe simply an official documentation on the subject? 
The difficulty also stems for the dependency to either Go, or Docker to avoid the install, so it takes a few second to run just because of the container.

It seems too easy to me to accidentally use a .env.local with some sensible information, eg for temporary testing, accidentaly destroying .gitignore or accidentally rename it, and push it to the repo, or even accidentally adding variables for some reason. In my scenario, I temporarily copied a file with env variables in order to get the exact name of a single value, but forgot to remove the pasted code, which also contained a sensitive value... 

I see there as been discussion about this recently but focused on a weird use case (https://github.com/vercel/next.js/discussions/32874), here I am talking about actual developer error, that could be gracefully caught by the framework. 

One related scenario I also dread is the accidental leak of getServerSideProps into the client bundle, which could also lead to env variables leaking but at runtime instead. I know there are unit tests and solid code to handle this, but I'd sleep better with a second layer of security.

I know this isn't specific to Next.js, but maybe your Rust superpowers could help designing a nice solution :)

### Describe the Bug

Starting with a vanilla `create-next-app` project (reproduced using `--typescript` and regular javascript) and following the documented integration guide [here](https://nextjs.org/docs/basic-features/eslint#prettier) running `next lint` reports no issues when an obvious formatting error is present in any source code.

npx create-next-app my-app

Chrome

### What operating system are you using?

macOS

### How are you deploying your application?

other

### Describe the Bug

When installing a production install using `yarn install --frozen-lockfile --production`

The size of Next.js is 77M, 
```
3.2M	node_modules/caniuse-lite
 33M	node_modules/next
 44M	node_modules/@next
```

when `@next` size is basically due to `swc`.
```
 24K	node_modules/@next/env
 60K	node_modules/@next/react-refresh-utils
 44M	node_modules/@next/swc-darwin-x64
```

Why it got installed in production installation?

### Expected Behavior

`swc` should not be installed in production installation.

### To Reproduce

make an empty folder with the following `package.json`:
```json
{
  "name": "next-swc-install",
  "version": "1.0.0",
  "main": "index.js",
  "author": "felixmosh",
  "license": "MIT",
  "dependencies": {
    "next": "^12.0.8"
  }
}
```

And run `yarn install --frozen-lockfile --production`

body:
I have a function which returns a bunch of meta tags as one large string. This needs to be dynamically because of the (private) Design System I use (don't want to lookup and update on each version bump).
These tags I want to add to the `Head` in `_document.js`.

```HTML
// Example of string
<meta name=theme-color content=#FFFFFF>
<meta name=apple-mobile-web-app-capable content=yes>
<meta name=apple-mobile-web-app-status-bar-style content=white>
<meta name=apple-mobile-web-app-title content="Title">
<link rel=manifest href=https://cdn.....webmanifest>
```

```TSX
<Head>
//...
{getMetaTagsAndIconLinks()}
//...
```

Already tried with `<head dangerouslySetInnerHTML`, but then the linter complaints, that I should use the `Head` component of next.js.
`<Head dangerouslySetInnerHTML` doesn't work, because I also set some children. Multiple `Heads` are not supported.

Any suggestions?

body:

      next: 12.0.8
      react: 17.0.2
      react-dom: 17.0.2

### What version of Next.js are you using?

12.0.8

### What version of Node.js are you using?

17.2.0

### What browser are you using?

Chrome

### What operating system are you using?

macOS

### How are you deploying your application?

Other

### Describe the Bug

I've written some middleware using the new API to rewrite urls for fully translated routing within my app. I had it all working with a custom express server using version `12.0.5-canary.11`.

I then subsequently upgraded to `12.0.7` and now get this error:

```
Error: To use middleware you must provide a `hostname` and `port` to the Next.js Server
```

This check was introduced in [`12.0.5-canary.18` ](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/pull/31935/commits/a29a1fd624f17e92943c00b787fae2d1eaae52ce)

This problem also occurs with the most basic Middleware implementation:

```tsx
import type { NextRequest, NextFetchEvent } from 'next/server';
import { NextResponse } from 'next/server';

export function middleware(req: NextRequest, _ev: NextFetchEvent) {
  return NextResponse.next();
}
```



### Expected Behavior

Should be able to use middleware with custom server, with no error

### To Reproduce

Use a the express custom server with the basic middleware implementation

author: abdukhashimov

title: Feature Request: `next build` should log all TSC errors, not just the first one
body:
### Describe the feature you'd like to request

Currently, `next build` fails on the first TSC error and prints only that error. For instance, if you have `N` TypeScript errors in your project that will prevent `next build` from succeeding, you will run `next build` `N` times, revealing them one at a time, unless you manually run `tsc --noEmit` or something.

Next Server should collect all of the errors emitted by TSC and log all of them, not just throw on the first one and log it.

### Describe the solution you'd like

Implement change above. Collect all errors from TSC and log them all, rather than just the first one.

### Describe alternatives you've considered

Run `tsc --noEmit`, resolve errors, and then run `next build`.

### Describe the feature you'd like to request

Use case:
Im using getStaticPaths to generate the most popular pages, and the rest can be generated on the fly with fallback blocking

I've added some tasks that take some time to run (about 5-6s)  in getStaticProps (generated blurDataUrl for remote images etc). This is fine for the pre-generated pages returned by getStaticPaths however for the other pages it takes a long time for the user.

### Describe the solution you'd like

A boolean isFallbackBlocking in GetStaticPropsContext would be the solution, which would allow to skip the time-consuming tasks on fallback.

### Describe alternatives you've considered

Current workaround is just to not do time-consuming tasks in getStaticProps, even if they are pre-rendered

body:

I have gotten the same error when trying to set this up with another tutorial based on this starter, and no other versions have worked either, e.g. using an Apollo client.
Both projects are running locally - WP through XAMPP in Windows and Next in WSL (because it is super slow in Windows for me).

I had to install the WP GraphQL PLugin by searching in the plugin directory instead of using the zip-file. The zip could be installed but there was a fatal error when activating (I tried 2 different zips, latest and slightly older version).
`fatal error: uncaught typeerror: call_user_func_array(): argument #1 ($callback) must be a valid callback, function "graphql_activation_callback" not found or invalid function name`

My api url works fine in Postman, so does the url for just the rest api without graphql.

I'm trying out plain React and WP REST api tomorrow but I'm curious about the issue here, any ideas?

title: Goes back to new spot when you return to page
body:
### Run `next info` (available from version 12.0.8 and up)

im using version 12.0.4

### What version of Next.js are you using?

12.0.4

### What version of Node.js are you using?

16.9.0

### What browser are you using?

Google Chrome

### What operating system are you using?

Windows 10

### How are you deploying your application?

Vercel

### Describe the Bug

Lets say i am in the homepage at the top of the page, then i choose to click a button link that takes me to the Products Listing page, now that i am in the PLP i scroll down a little, when i click to go back to the homepage, instaead of taking me to the top of the homepage, it just takes me to a different spot. It happens in many pages of the site, that when you go back it just takes you to a different spot on the page than the one you were before. Sometimes i can be in the products listing page, and when i click to go back to the homepage, it takes me to the homepage but down to the footer level. It affecting us in production. Please let me know if you need more details.

### Expected Behavior

The expected behaviour would be that, if i am in the homepage and i am at the TOP of the page, when i click a link that takes me to the products listing page and then cloose to go back, it will take me back to the homepage and point to the TOP of the page, not to a different spot like its doing now.

### To Reproduce

I am not allowed to provide code, But maybe to reproduce it, just navigate trough a big next.js project, try to go from the homepage, at the top of the page, to a different page, like the products one or the contact us one, and then when you click to go back check if it actually takes you to the top of the homepage

title: How to make PhpStorm/WebStorm aware of Next.js webpack config?
body:
I use a custom webpack config as detailed in the [Next.js docs _Custom Webpack Config_ page](https://nextjs.org/docs/api-reference/next.config.js/custom-webpack-config). PhpStorm has [built-in support for handling webpack](https://www.jetbrains.com/help/phpstorm/using-webpack.html), however, since there is no actual webpack config file to point to, how does one integrate their IDE with a custom Next.js webpack config?

One issue I'm running into is PhpStorm complaining (rightfully) that the SVG I'm trying to import it not a module. However, this works because I'm using the `@svgr/webpack` loader. I'm assuming if I can get PhpStorm to "see" my webpack config, it won't complain about such things.

Having a command to generate a webpack config from `next.config.js`, for example, might be a viable workaround. Another is having a reference to a file that is dynamically resolved to the same config that the Next.js config file would use; [this is the approach taken by Vue](https://cli.vuejs.org/guide/webpack.html#using-resolved-config-as-a-file).
author: chipit24

title: Allow setting middleware rewrite destination on an existing NextResponse
body:
### Describe the feature you'd like to request

TL;DR: Please create a helper function which attaches the x-middleware-rewrite header to an existing NextResponse that we previously created with NextResponse.next()

We use [Next.js middleware](https://nextjs.org/docs/middleware) for A/B testing - we send users to one variation of a page or another.

To determine which page (A or B) to serve to a user:
1) We check if a browserId is stored in a cookie called browserId. If not, we generate a GUID with no expiration and set it as the value of the browserId cookie for subsequent visits.
2) Once we know the browserId, we send this semi-permanent browserId to an internal A/B testing endpoint that decides whether to place this user on the A side or B side of an experiment.
3) Rewrite the location to go to either A.tsx or B.tsx page.

Notice that in step 1 we do not know if we are sending the user to page A or B. Also, in this step 1 we sometimes need to **set** a cookie with the browserId. To do that, we need call NextResponse.next() to get an empty response, then we call response.cookie('browserId', ...) on it. 

In steps 2 and 3 we determine where to send the user and at this point we need to call [rewrite](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/d7062dddcc4f7e679cf52214e2a9fc0d87e4f068/packages/next/server/web/spec-extension/response.ts#L89) with the correct path. However, since we started with an empty response that we want to modify, we cannot call NextResponse.rewrite(...) anymore because that would generate a separate new response. What we do instead is a bit of a hack, we manually set the _x-middleware-rewrite_ header on the response we created for step 1.

Since this header name _x-middleware-rewrite_ can presumably change in the future, I propose having an official helper function.

### Describe the solution you'd like

Please create a public helper function which attaches the x-middleware-rewrite header to an existing NextResponse that we previously created with NextResponse.next(). Then have the rewrite function internally call this same helper function.

### Describe alternatives you've considered

We can postpone setting the cookie described in step 1 after we performed steps 2 and 3 but this would be ugly and it would not follow normal logic.

title: Static pages and A/B testing with middleware - HOW?
body:
Our goal: Have two **static** pages index.tsx and index2.tsx used in an A/B experiment where we use middleware rewrite() to switch between them.

I want to mention that we host Next.js in Azure on our own, and we do not use Vercel.

The problem we are having is that for static pages Next.js emits a cache control header that makes our CDN cache one page or another. And we cannot override cache control headers (to set it to no-cache) for static pages in next.config.js. Here's what the [docs](https://nextjs.org/docs/going-to-production#caching) say: _Cache-Control headers set in next.config.js will be overwritten in production to ensure that static assets can be cached effectively._ 

It's probably something very simple that I am missing, but I don't understand how we can have two static pages (built at Next.js build time with GetStaticPageProps) involved in an A/B test directed by middleware, and also use a CDN in front?

Add shallow shallow routing with a param. ` [div onClick={() => { router.push(`${router.asPath}?list=1`, undefined, { shallow: true }) }}] items [/div] ` 2. Add a secondary param, by using Note: The "asPath" here, at this point, is: "/product/234?list=1" - The reason I use an "object" is that at this point, we "might" have another param. ` const addItem = () => { router.push({ pathname: router.asPath, query: { addItem: 123 }}, undefined, { shallow: true }) }; ` I'd expect next.js to handle this and change the url to: ` /product/234?list=1&addItem=123 ` Instead, I get: ` /project/3%3Flist=1?addItem=123 ` Is this expected behavior? **Note**: I know I could just check for a "?" in the asPath and append accordingly, but my sense is that Next.js should handle it in their routing, no? ### Expected Behavior I'd expect next.js to handle this and update the url to: ` /product/234?list=1&addItem=123 ` ### To Reproduce [https://codesandbox.io/s/bad-param-for-bug-report-zlhx7?file=/pages/index.js.](url)

author: angelovdev

title: Support static page generation on demand at runtime (for SSG/ISR)
body:
### Describe the feature you'd like to request

Add support for generating static pages (without slug) on demand = at runtime (not only at build time).

Currently NextJS support generation on demand only for pages which implements `getStaticPaths` (page with slug). But static page without slug (like "pages/index.js" for homepage, "404.js", etc.) cannot implement `getStaticPaths`, so they need to be generated at build time. But in many cases this is not enough. For example when you need to support multiple instances of NexJS application and each instance has its own endpoints for data, cms etc. In this case you cannot fetch data in `getStaticProps` at build time, it's necessary to pre-generate pages at runtime in each instance.

### Describe the Bug

I have a lambda function handler like this, and I want to bundle it to serve the bundled version over the wire:

```javascript
const server = require('restana')();
const app = require('next')({ dev: false });
const files = require('serve-static');
const path = require('path');
const bodyParser = require('body-parser');

const nextRequestHandler = app.getRequestHandler();

server.use(files(path.join(__dirname, 'build')));
server.use(files(path.join(__dirname, 'public')));

server.all('/api/*', bodyParser.json(), (req, res) => nextRequestHandler(req, res));

server.all('*', (req, res) => nextRequestHandler(req, res));

module.exports.handler = require('serverless-http')(server);
```

I am bundling this with webpack:

```javascript
const ZipPlugin = require("zip-webpack-plugin")
const path = require("path")

const config = {
  entry: {
    handler: "./_lambda.js",
  },
  output: {
    filename: "[name]/index.js",
    path: path.resolve(__dirname, "dist/"),
    libraryTarget: "umd",
  },
  target: "node",
  mode: "production",
  optimization: { minimize: false },
}
//finally zip the output directory, ready to deploy
const pluginConfig = {
  plugins: Object.keys(config.entry).map(entryName => {
    return new ZipPlugin({
      path: path.resolve(__dirname, "dist/"),
      filename: entryName,
      extension: "zip",
      include: [entryName],
    })
  }),
}

const webpackConfig = Object.assign(config, pluginConfig)
module.exports = webpackConfig
```

I expect no error from the bundle. Right now I am getting error from `next/dist/server`

author: winston0410

title: ISR Content Reverting on Cloud Run & Scaling
body:
I have a website that is using the SSG and ISR capabilities of the framework. It's deployed to Cloud Run on GCP with the following settings:

```
--platform: managed
--cpu: 2
--memory: 8Gi 
--ingress: internal-and-cloud-load-balancing
--port: 3000
--min-instances: 1
--max-instances: 1
--region: northamerica-northeast1
```

It's connected to Contentful for the headless CMS, and set to revalidate every 4 minutes. However, I'm finding that a day or two later my changes that were updated via ISR will revert to the version of the page on original build time (not even the previous version, but the original version). Any ideas on how to fix this issue? Is it because the Cloud Run CPU idles?

Also, I'm trying to scale but replicating a pod will mean the replication will have the same content as the original build. How do I specify a shared volume for the cache?

Thank you!
author: JordanTheriault

title: Custom server: Can't conditionally serve different Next directories
body:
### Run `next info` (available from version 12.0.8 and up)

    Operating System:
      Platform: darwin
      Arch: x64
      Version: Darwin Kernel Version 20.5.0: Sat May  8 05:10:33 PDT 2021; root:xnu-7195.121.3~9/RELEASE_X86_64
    Binaries:
      Node: 16.13.2
      npm: 8.1.2
      Yarn: 1.22.17
      pnpm: N/A
    Relevant packages:
      next: 12.0.8
      react: 17.0.2
      react-dom: 17.0.2

### What version of Next.js are you using?

12.0.8

### What version of Node.js are you using?

16.13.2

### What browser are you using?

Safari

### What operating system are you using?

macOS

### How are you deploying your application?

node server.js

### Describe the Bug

I prepared a minimal repro and issue description here: [Next.js no conditional server issue (minimal repro)](https://github.com/just-learning-1/minimal-repros/tree/master/nextjs-no-conditional-server-issue)

### Goal

I want to conditionally serve different Next directories to different sessions within a custom Express server.

### Issue Summary

After Next app server serves one directory (`next({ dir: 'a' })`) in a server runtime, it seems impossible to later serve a different directory (`next({ dir: 'b' })`), even to an entirely different session or machine.

### Expected Behavior

### Expected behavior

(See code & repro) I expected to get served `dir: 'a'` when storing cookie value `'a'`, and to get served `dir: 'b'` when storing cookie value `'b'`.

Instead, after getting served either `'a'` or `'b'` once, all future attempts to conditionally serve will serve that first directory.

### To Reproduce

## Repro

1.  Clone the [minimal repro](https://github.com/just-learning-1/minimal-repros/tree/master/nextjs-no-conditional-server-issue)
2.  Run `yarn serve`
3.  Visit `localhost:4000` in a private browser window
4.  Read: `Visit /a or /b to continue`
5.  Visit `localhost:4000/a` (for this example)
6.  Read: `Your choice is a. Now, return to /`
7.  Visit `localhost:4000`
8.  Read: `Welcome to a`
9.  Now, visit `localhost:4000` in a different private browser window (or different machine)) and read instruction
10. Visit `localhost:4000/b` and read message
11. Finally, visit `localhost:4000`

**Expected:** Read: `Welcome to b`

**Actual:** Read: `Welcome to a`

*Note:* Visiting `/b` first will indeed persist `Welcome to b` instead.

## Code

Here's the code in `server.js`, minimally changed from the official [Custom Express Server example](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/tree/canary/examples/custom-server-express):

```js
// server.js
const cookieParser = require('cookie-parser')
const express = require('express')
const next = require('next')

const port = parseInt(process.env.PORT, 10) || 4000
const dev = process.env.NODE_ENV !== 'production'

// Want different next apps & handlers to conditionally source different directories
const appA = next({ dev, dir: 'a' })
const handleA = appA.getRequestHandler()
const appB = next({ dev, dir: 'b' })
const handleB = appB.getRequestHandler()

const server = express()

server.use(cookieParser())

// Visit /a to receive cookie with choice=a
server.get('/a', (req, res) => {
  res.cookie('choice', 'a')
  res.status(200).send('Your choice is a. Now, return to /')
})

// Visit /b to receive cookie with choice=b
server.get('/b', (req, res) => {
  res.cookie('choice', 'b')
  res.status(200).send('Your choice is b. Now, return to /')
})

server.all('*', (req, res, goNext) => {
  const cookieChoice = req.cookies['choice'] || ''
  const chooseMessage = 'Visit /a or /b to continue'

  if (!cookieChoice) {
    // When no cookie, send message
    res.send(chooseMessage)
  } else {
    let app
    let handle
    switch (cookieChoice) {
      case 'a':
        // Set app & handler to serve directory 'a/'
        app = appA
        handle = handleA
        break
      case 'b':
        // Set app & handler to serve directory 'b/'
        app = appB
        handle = handleB
        break
      default:
        res.send(chooseMessage)
    }

    // Want to conditionally serve 'a/' or 'b/' here
    app.prepare()
      .then(() => {
        handle(req, res) 
      })

    goNext()
  }
})

server.listen(port, (err) => {
  if (err) throw err
  console.log(`> Ready on http://localhost:${port}`)
})
```

title: Should next.js handle the distribution of license information for open source dependencies?
body:
### Describe the feature you'd like to request

After playing around with my create-next-app, I notice there is no builtin way to create and distribute the license files which are required to be available when using certain OSS. 

Generating such licenses is pretty easy with https://github.com/xz64/license-webpack-plugin (which NCC already uses). Given that information, I wonder if it makes sense to expose a "known route" to retrieve this information to make it easy to add a link to the notices.

In my opinion (and happy to discuss further) OSS compliance is exactly the "type" of web dev work that Next.js is equipped to totally handle and abstract away.

### Describe the solution you'd like

Perhaps use LicenseWebpackPlugin and expose the notice generated in some known way to be able to include it in the next.js documentation. Happy to help brainstorm further.

### Describe alternatives you've considered

Alternatives require users manually creating and exposing license information, but this goes against "zero config" ideals.

The more common alternative is for users to just ignore these licenses and not provide any mechanism to attribute the OSS they are using. (I should note that I was also unable to find this information for vercel.com)

title: "Prop `href` did not match.", when using next/link from external package.
body: ### Run `next info` (available from version 12.0.8 and up) I don't think that works on codesandbox: `> No such directory exists as the project root: /sandbox/info`, sorry 😢 ### What version of Next.js are you using? 12.0.9 ### What version of Node.js are you using? 14.18.1 ### What browser are you using? Chrome ### What operating system are you using? macOS ### How are you deploying your application? any development mode / codesandbox ### Describe the Bug In short: I get a rehydration warning like this, when using next/link through any external package and use locales: ``` Warning: Prop `href` did not match. Server: "/my-page" Client: "/fr/my-page" ``` Where I think the problem is: When a i18n enabled next app uses `next/link` through an external package (that somehow enriches its behaviour), the environment variable `process.env.__NEXT_I18N_SUPPORT` is not defined (only on the server) within the `addLocale` function used by the link. Thus the link has no locale added on the server. On the client the locale attribute is present as expected. When react rehydrates the html it stumbles upon the a tag with mismatched href attribute. When not using dev mode the warning is suppressed but I have a hunch that the mismatch is still there. Also see the discussion here: #22130 (esp. some of my strange logging adventures [here](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/issues/22130#issuecomment-833610774)) ### Expected Behavior `process.env.__NEXT_I18N_SUPPORT` should be defined within `next/link` no matter if server or client and no matter if imported directly or via an external package. Otherwise it would be impossible to write packages abstracting link related things. In other words using `next/link` through external packages should not cause a warning and should have correct html generated on the server. ### To Reproduce I have created a reproduction on **[Codesandbox](https://codesandbox.io/s/nextjs-prop-href-did-not-match-9ob4o?file=/pages/index.js)**. Just check `pages/index.js` and the console. I published a package that does almost nothing but using `next/link` to create this reproduction (it only has `next` and `react` as peerDeps). Check the source here: https://unpkg.com/next-link-30d@1.0.0/index.js I would love if you could help me out. This bug has been haunting me for half a year now and I finally managed to reproduce it reliably. ❤️

title: Build fails when `exactOptionalPropertyTypes` TS config option is enabled
body:
### Run `next info` (available from version 12.0.8 and up)

```
Operating System:
      Platform: linux
      Arch: x64
      Version: #1 SMP PREEMPT Wed, 22 Dec 2021 09:23:54 +0000
    Binaries:
      Node: 17.3.0
      npm: N/A
      Yarn: 3.1.1
      pnpm: N/A
    Relevant packages:
      next: 12.0.8
      react: 17.0.2
      react-dom: 17.0.2
```

### What version of Next.js are you using?

12.0.8

### What version of Node.js are you using?

17.3.0

### What browser are you using?

Chrome (but irrelevant)

### What operating system are you using?

Arch Linux

### How are you deploying your application?

next build

### Describe the Bug

When the TypeScript `compilerOptions` option `exactOptionalPropertyTypes` is enabled, `next build` fails with the following error:
```
Failed to compile.

../../node_modules/next/dist/server/dev/next-dev-server.d.ts:63:5
Type error: Property 'generateRoutes' in type 'DevServer' is not assignable to the same property in base type 'NextNodeServer'.
```

My understanding is that `DevServer` has an "explicitly" optional type containing `{generateRoutes: Route | undefined}` while `NextNodeServer` has an "implicit" optional type containing `{generateRoutes?: Route}`. `exactOptionalPropertyTypes` disallows explicitly assigning `undefined` to optional types.

### Expected Behavior

I'm unsure if I _expect_ this to pass. But it would probably be good to be compatible with this option.

This bug report might be more of an improvement suggestion.

### To Reproduce

1. Set up a Typescript Next.js project normally (i.e. via the example or otherwise).
2. Modify `tsconfig.json` to have the following (along with what was there to begin with):
```json
{
  "compilerOptions": {
    "exactOptionalPropertyTypes": false,
  }
}
```
3. Run `next build` (`next dev` works fine), and watch it fail on the "Checking validity of types" step.
