title: SWC + styled-components: element classes & style classes are not synched
body:
### Verify canary release

- [X] I verified that the issue exists in Next.js canary release

### Provide environment information

Operating System:
      Platform: win32
      Arch: x64
      Version: Windows 10 Pro
    Binaries:
      Node: 16.14.1
      npm: N/A
      Yarn: N/A
      pnpm: N/A
    Relevant packages:
      next: 12.1.5-canary.4
      react: 17.0.2
      react-dom: 17.0.2
      styled-components: 5.3.5

### What browser are you using? (if relevant)

Chrome 100.0.4896.75

### How are you deploying your application? (if relevant)

_No response_

### Describe the Bug

Sometimes when [passing props](https://styled-components.com/docs/basics#passed-props) to styled-components component, the generated CSS class is not synched with the styles.  Classes work normally when adding the basic .babelrc configuration for styled-components so I assume the problem is with SWC. 

### Expected Behavior

So far there has been 2 cases when classes are wrong:

1. When starting the app, initial class is wrong but works after dynamically updating props. In this case the initial class should work without dynamic changes.
2. After dynamically changing props the class changes on the element but CSS styles are scoped for the old class and if the props are changed to the original state, a third class is generated (kind like x -> y -> z -> y -> z). in this case the styles should be updated to the new class. 



### To Reproduce

I created a CodeSandbox version: https://codesandbox.io/s/next-styled-components-class-names-issue-bz5p0z?file=/pages/index.js

title: Inject external API result or payload into NextAuth session
body:
Hi, I am using NextAuth Credential Provider for login to the site. Everything is working fine for Login process. 
Our site's user data is stored on external db which communicate thru API. Due to the mechanism of the External API, the registration process require the site to create session once user submit the form. The result or payload returned by the external API will consist of user information including the token to communicate with the external API. 
I see the NextAuth document  using authorize() method to create a session.  My question is, can I create a session without running the authorized() method? I am thinking to inject the payload result from the external API into the session. I need to create a session because I need to get the token from the session that will be used for subsequence pages once the form is submitted.  

For short , my question is, may I know if it is possible to inject or add a payload or result returned by external API into the session using NextAuth?
author: archercoder

title: Error Handling For SSR Suspense
body:
A stable React 18  version has been released and Next.js now supports Suspense for Serverside Rendering (SSR).

If an error occurs inside the Suspense, Next.js creates this error:
```
Unhandled Runtime Error
Error: The server could not finish this Suspense boundary, likely due to an error during server rendering. Switched to client rendering.
```

How can I handle errors that happen inside Suspense? I am using an Error Boundary already but it only works for client rendering.

author: neeraj3029

title: Nextpress: A Wordpress-like CMS based entirely on Next.js?
body:
I think one of the most attractive features of Next.js is being able to work on both the Frontend + Backend and then seamlessly deploying it anywhere.

WordPress CMS still occupies a major space in publishing but it's heavily outdated in terms of modern technology. One of the reasons WP is still so active is that it's a one-codebase ecosystem which means the developers can modify the frontend and backend seamlessly (something similar to what Next.js did to React.js+Node.js backend). I think investing resources into **a modern alternative for WordPress entirely based on Next.js that can be deployed onto Vercel or a custom server with a click**.

The modern approach has been to use a 3rd party CMS like Sanity, Strapi, etc. but there still is a lot of potential for a combined CMS + Next.js solutions. Ever since the dynamic shift towards JAMStack, Next.js has taken over the competition quick strong. But there is still room to build a nice WP-like ecosystem that would further ease JAMStack CMS's problems and enhance its development. Currently, every JAMStack developer starts from scratch on a Next.js project. But we do we manage to build an ecosystem like WP, we can combine all the developer's efforts of JAMStack projects and further cut down developer times and efficiency on CMS development.

It would just be WordPress with templates, plugins, etc the entire ecosystem is built entirely based on the Next.js framework. The vision is to be able to deploy simple to a complex CMS website onto Vercel.com with no hassle just like WordPress with a single click!

Would Vercel be interested in investing in such an idea? @timneutkens  :D I have a group of buddies who would love to work on this project. Just wanted to get an opinion for the founders of Next.js on what they think!

Any feedback is welcome! 👍

title: What is the best approach for a subdomain mirror?
body:
Hi,

I have a mini website build with nextjs, that is created from a CMS, with the below architecture:

- webTV
  - [webTvId].js
  - [webTvId]
    - section
      - [slug].js
    - group
      - [slug].js
    - [WebTvContentId].js 

This website in on a subdomain (player.domain.com) and shared among all users. This is working as expected.

Now, there is possibility for that website to have a custom subdomain, for example userjohn.domain.com. In that case i would need the below architecture:

- section
  - [slug].js
- group
  - [slug].js
- [WebTvContentId].js 
- index.js (equal to old [webTvId].js)

The subdomain is a unique string that can replace the webTVId. 

I would need to keep the both routings working. For example, this page player.domain.com/webtv/12345/section/section-1 should be a "mirror" of userjohn.domain.com/section/section-1

I could just duplicate all my folders and files (from the page directory) but that would not be very smart I guess.

Any idea on how i could be smart on this? :)

Thanks.

author: Badyl1g

title: Merging partial static exports?
body:
Hi,

We're building an ecommerce site where we want the output to be a completely static site on S3/Cloudfront using the static export functionality of Next.js. The thing we're trying to solve is the build time, as we have a lot of pages for our products and our product listing pages, and that will be updated fairly frequently as we re-rank products in our listing pages every few hours, causing a rebuild.

On-demand ISR would be perfect for this if we we're running with the Next.js server or Vercel, but unfortunately we will not go that route at this time. Multi-zone is also not quite what we're looking for, since we'll have 10k+ instances of the same page.

My question is if it's doable to merge static exports with the same build id? So for page `/product/[id]`, one node can build `/product/a` and `product/b`, while another node can build `product/c`, using different inputs to `getStaticPaths`. Then finally merge both out directories. My initial testing indicates that this is possible, but we want to see if there are any obvious pitfalls we're missing.

Any other supported options to make this work would also be appreciated, since it feels a bit like we're on thin ice here.

Thanks!

author: hemeanandh

title: Early Hints
body:
### Describe the feature you'd like to request

Some CDNs like [Cloudflare](https://developers.cloudflare.com/cache/about/early-hints/) support the [Early Hints](https://httpwg.org/specs/rfc8297.html) web standard.

This can dramatically improve the page loading speed as the CDN can hint to the browser the render-blocking resources (CSS, Fonts, etc.) that need to be downloaded.

### Describe the solution you'd like

Next.js could add the appropriate [Link](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTTP/Headers/Link) headers to any HTML response with [preload](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTML/Link_types/preload) for any stylesheets that Next.js is going to add the `<head>`. Doing so will allow CDNs to cache these headers. On the next request, they can return the headers early while the CDN waits for the complete response.

### Describe alternatives you've considered

I've considered implementing this ourselves within Next.js, but it is difficult (if not impossible?) to know the URLs to the CSS files from _within_ Next.js itself.

body:
Hi,

I'm evaluating using Next.js for a work project. I'm having trouble calling an API running locally from a component.

So I get: `http://localhost:3000/localhost:12000/api/auth/token`
Instead of: `http://localhost:12000/api/auth/token`

If I use https it works like I'd expect. Our local development environment for this API doesn't use https though.

I've tried setting `localhost:12000/` as a:
- local variable
- .env.local variable
- property in .next.config.js

I've tried making the call with:
- JavaScript's fetch API
- Axios
- An SDK we have of helper methods that make REST requests

I've tried doing the calls from:
- the "Home" component
- a non page component

None of this worked though. I still get the mangled URL. 

I could get it to work doing a call in `getServerSideProps`. So it looks like it's an issue on the client.

Anyone else come across this? Any ideas?

1. Next.js will not erase comments from your `tsconfig.json`.
2. Next.js will not override options you have set without prompting you first. You can answer "No" and edit them manually.
3. Next.js will still create a default (but overridable) `tsconfig.json` for the user if not present.

      react: 18.0.0
      react-dom: 18.0.0

### What browser are you using? (if relevant)

Chrome 100.0.4896.75

### How are you deploying your application? (if relevant)

Vercel

### Describe the Bug

According to NextJS's documentation around measuring performance (https://nextjs.org/docs/advanced-features/measuring-performance) its indicated that the `reportWebVitals` function leverages `web-vitals.js` under the hood. The `web-vitals.js` offers support for `value` and `delta` however NextJS only exposes the `value` and not `delta` - is there any reason why this was left out? 

Im busy adding some functionality to a site and we need to get the `delta` for certain Web Vitals. 

### Expected Behavior

```
// ⛔  Current Function Definition
export function reportWebVitals({ id, name, label, value }) {}
```

```
// ✅  Expected Function Definition
export function reportWebVitals({ id, name, label, value, delta }) {
```

### To Reproduce

1. Setup a new NextJS project
2. Create a custom `_app.js` 
3. Add the `export function reportWebVitals(metric) {}`
4. No value available for `delta`

title: Using NextJs on Vercel with a separate self-hosted server
body:
My idea is to build an app that acts as a middleman so that when users trade on another platform they'll be credited on my website. Therefore, I need a server that will continuously perform tasks to track trades via an api key and update the clients via WebSockets.

Since NextJs on Vercel is Serverless, one possible solution is to create a self-hosted server to handle those needs. I would like, however, to still use NextJs on Vercel to host my front-end application because of SSR, performance, SEO, etc.

My application would then have a front-end on Vercel and a separate backend (hosted on AWS, for example). 

With this setup, I have two possibilities: 
- I can use Vercel for the front-end only and create all api routes on the self-hosted server, which would increase its workload.
- I can use Serverless Functions (and I would love to) for my api and delegate only the job of tracking trades and updating clients to the self-hosted server.

I’m not sure yet what approach to take, but in both of them I would need to have some type of domain “routing”. 

If I were to go with the first one, for example, all requests to `https://mydomain.com/api/*` would need to go to the self-hosted server and all other requests to `https://mydomain.com/*` would go to Vercel. How would I do that? Would I need to have two separate domains and then use a `_middleware.ts` to redirect all `/api/*` calls to the self-hosted server domain?

Would it be possible to use `subdomains` so that `https://api.mydomain.com/*` goes to the self-hosted server and `https://mydomain.com/*` goes to Vercel?

I also don't want to hard-code the server's location in an environment variable.  that's just not very portable and seems wrong... I've read through the dialogues of https://github.com/vercel/next.js/issues/10266, https://github.com/vercel/next.js/issues/10272 and https://github.com/vercel/next.js/issues/9524 but it isn't clear to me what the solution is

my question: what is the preferred way in NextJs to formulate the served page's location?

TIA - ekkis

p.s. I cannot do this client-side (i.e. `window.location`) because the QR code is rendered by a React component so the value needs to be known at the time it's rendered

```
23:27:09.807 | at DataTable (/vercel/path0/.next/server/pages/reports.js:749:37)
23:27:09.807 | at Hc (/vercel/path0/node_modules/react-dom/cjs/react-dom-server.browser.production.min.js:64:191)
23:27:09.807 | at Kc (/vercel/path0/node_modules/react-dom/cjs/react-dom-server.browser.production.min.js:66:253)
23:27:09.807 | at Z (/vercel/path0/node_modules/react-dom/cjs/react-dom-server.browser.production.min.js:71:89)

```

I can't figure out why this would be the case. The code on that line should not be getting executed at all. And I don't get why `yarn build` works on my machine but not on Vercel.

I tracked it down to the fact that my local environment is using a different version of Node. Node 16+ supports the `performance` API but Node 14 doesn't. This is probably what causes things to break in Vercel. Is there any way to get around this? Can I force Vercel to use Node 16?

I also logged this issue with `react-table` [here](https://github.com/TanStack/react-table/issues/3830#issuecomment-1101012448).

author: kristofferso

title: Opt out or removal of whatwg-fetch from getOptimizedAliases
body:
### Describe the feature you'd like to request

I would like to be able to polyfill `fetch` via `whatwg-fetch` for a [Web Worker](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/Worker).

Next's [webpack config](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/d895a50abbc8f91726daa2d7ebc22c58f58aabbb/packages/next/build/webpack-config.ts#L102-L107) replaces the `whatwg-fetch` import with an "optimized" variant, under the assumption that nextjs has already polyfilled `fetch`.

Nextjs's polyfills don't impact workers because they run in their own scope, so this prevents polyfilling workers with specific polyfills.

### Describe the solution you'd like

An opt out or removal of getOptimizedAliases. Linting seems sufficient for preventing inclusion of unnecessary polyfills (identical to the polyfill.io lint rules already in place), though these wouldn't prevent any dependencies from including unnecessary polyfills.

### Describe alternatives you've considered

Using alternative polyfills (eg another fetch polyfill).

author: Rodeveer

title: Don't run page middleware on requests served from /public folder
body:
### Describe the feature you'd like to request

We use the same next.js app for both our marketing site and for our authenticated webapp.

We host our marketing site at `blah.com` & our app at `app.blah.com`.

We have all of our "app" pages in `pages/app`. We also have root `_middleware.ts` that rewrites all requests from `app.blah.com`=>`blah.com/app`.

The setup is lovely, the only wrinkle is that we share public assets between these different sections of our app.  We have stuff like:
`public/font.css`. From the `app.blah.com` subdomain the request for these resources fail because the middleware runs and looks for the resources at `public/app/font.css`.

### Describe the solution you'd like

I'd love it if any resource requests that are filled from the `/public` directory would not pass through the page middleware. They aren't page requests, so why do they go through the page middleware?



### Describe alternatives you've considered

If I duplicate all of my resources from `/public`=>`/public/app` it avoids the 404s because once the middleware has rewritten the path it can still find the resources. This is suboptimal because it requires duplicating files.

I can put all resources in a sub folder like `/public/resources` & then in the root page middleware I can avoid rewriting requests prefixed by `/resources`. This is suboptimal because we have files that are expected to be in specific locations on our root path (i.e. `favicon.ico`, `/.well-known/appple-app-site-association`).


I can handle each file/folder present in `/public` in my `/pages/_middleware.ts` & avoid rewriting - this is suboptimal because it requires a lot of extra code & is error prone because if anything in that folder changes or if a root level file is added it won't be accessible.

Any other options?

author: ericmatthys

title: Allow runtime environment variables in middleware
body:
### Describe the feature you'd like to request

We'd like the ability to use environment variables at runtime in middleware.

It is a common application practice to build & bundle an app once and then configure it based on runtime environment variables. This allows us to adhere to being a 12 factor app as much as possible. We can do this with the rest of nextjs, but not middleware.

### Describe the solution you'd like

I'm unsure what the mechanism should be because you currently replace `process.env.FOO` at build time, but there should be some ability to read it. Perhaps make `serverRuntimeConfig` specially available in middleware? Perhaps make certain env vars available if they're prefixed with `MIDDLEWARE_` like the established pattern for prefixing `PUBLIC_` vars ?

### Describe alternatives you've considered

We previously were able to use optional chaining to get around this and `process.env?.FOO` worked at runtime in middleware, but as there were changes at some point between nextjs versions this ability has disappeared and there appears to be no way to access any environment variables at run time.

The only other solution we can think of is mentioned here to perhaps load a JSON file at deployment time with config that would be included in the app, but it is somewhat awkward to use and requires changes to the way env vars are set by deployment systems and generally doesn't feel right. It'd be better if environment variables functioned as they do in other parts of the application.

title: Emotion SWC plugin label options don’t work for css prop, [filename] segment erroneously includes file extension
body:
### Verify canary release - [X] I verified that the issue exists in Next.js canary release ### Provide environment information Operating System: Platform: darwin Arch: x64 Version: Darwin Kernel Version 21.4.0: Fri Mar 18 00:45:05 PDT 2022; root:xnu-8020.101.4~15/RELEASE_X86_64 Binaries: Node: 16.6.2 npm: 7.20.3 Yarn: 1.22.11 pnpm: N/A Relevant packages: next: 12.1.6-canary.5 react: 17.0.2 react-dom: 17.0.2 ### What browser are you using? (if relevant) _No response_ ### How are you deploying your application? (if relevant) _No response_ ### Describe the Bug The Emotion SWC plugin label generation doesn’t match `@emotion/babel-plugin` in two ways: 1. `className`s added via the `css` prop don’t include a generated label in the format specified by `labelFormat` when the `autoLabel` option is `always` or `dev-only`. They _will_ include labels manually added with the Emotion’s custom `label` property, but won’t label the style based on the variable name like `@emotion/babel-plugin`. 2. Labels including the `[filename]` segment include the file extension, breaking selectors. e.g. if a style is defined in a file called `styles.js`, it will generate a className including `styles.js`, which breaks the CSS selector since it includes an illegal `.` character. ### Expected Behavior 1. `className`s add via the `css` prop should be labelled the same way as `className`s generated via the `@emotion/styled` `styled` function. 2. Labels including the `[filename]` segment should exclude the filename’s extension. ### To Reproduce Reproduction here: https://github.com/GrantASL19/nextjs-swc-emotion-labels-regression First, start as-is with `npm run dev`. Given the Home component: ```tsx const Home = () => (
Cool Styles With :hover. Let's bounce.

Paragraph styled with css prop

Paragraph styled with css prop including label declaration

) ``` The Babel output is: ```html
Cool Styles
With :hover.
Let's bounce.

Paragraph styled with css prop

Paragraph styled with css prop including label declaration

``` Next delete or rename `.babelrc` to opt out of Babel and enable the SWC transform, then restart the dev server with `npm run dev`. The SWC output is: ```html
Cool Styles
With :hover.
Let's bounce.

Paragraph styled with css prop

Paragraph styled with css prop including label declaration

``` Note that the styles from `styles.js` will no longer work because of the invalid generated className, and the labels for the elements styled with the `css` prop won’t match the `@emotion/babel-plugin` output (e.g. `customPrefix` is gone, and only the element with `label: paragraphWithLabel;` has `paragraphWithLabel` included in its `className`). --- FWIW I’ve never liked how verbose the `@emotion/babel-plugin` generated `className`s are, but we do heavily rely on the auto-labelling to identify which component and style variable is applied to an element in the browser devtools. So e.g. an output like `css-m78a9o-customPrefix-pages_paragraph` would be totally fine, but `css-1g4xn9m-Home` is a regression because we can’t identify that the style comes from the `paragraph` variable. Also we exclusively use the `css` prop (not `styled`), so I can’t say if the `styled` behaviour is expected or not.

author: noidar

title: How does next/image actually work behind the scenes and a possible improvement
body:
So I've recently created a website using next.js for a client and everything has gone fairly smooth.  There has been some slight issue with images taking longer than 1 second to load due to the initial cold startup and multiple cache "MISS".  After a couple of days of the site running, things started to smooth out with a few images sometimes taking around 400ms to load due to a cache "STALE".  Through testing I believe I have an idea as to how next/image is handled in the background. I've looked through the code and I think I have some idea as to how it works, but I'm hoping someone is able to confirm this.  

From what I believe is happening, every request with a "src", "q", "w" are handled as a single image request meaning that the origin image is fetched every single time when the transformed image is not present in the cache.  I assume that the fetch request only happens for absolute images an not relative images (relative should be read using "fs" I assume?).  For examples

src=/example.png&w=32&q=50 -> get transform cache -> MISS -> fetch origin image -> set transform cache -> response
src=/example.png&w=64&q=50 -> get transform cache -> MISS -> fetch origin image -> set transform cache -> response
src=/example.png&w=32&q=50 -> get transform cache -> HIT -> response

If I assume that this is how next/image handles images where a fetch request has to be made for the same image when only the width has changed, would performance be improved by also caching the origin image on the server?  That way the examples would look more like this.

src=/example.png&w=32&q=50 -> get transform cache -> MISS -> get origin cache -> MISS -> fetch origin image -> set origin cache -> set transform cache -> response
src=/example.png&w=64&q=50 -> get transform cache -> MISS -> get origin cache -> set transform cache -> response
src=/example.png&w=32&q=50 -> get transform cache -> HIT -> response

From testing locally with express + sharp, I found that the average "MISS" response for a large image was around 1s to 5s with a "STALE" response being around 200ms to 400ms with the old method.  Using the method I described above, the average "MISS" response was reduced to around 200ms to 400ms which was almost exactly the same as the "STALE" response! (Keeping in mind that the very first request for the image would still take around 1s to 5s, but all requests with changed width/quality would only take as long as the toBuffer() method on the sharp object)  

Because of my findings, I started to believe that next/image uses the first method and not the second one, but I would love to know whether or not I've made a mistake.  If not, when it comes to the second method where we cache the origin image, I believe the best method for handling this would be to use the same logic currently being used for caching, with cache-control being set on the cached origin image, and transformed images having their own cache control being set in a similar fashion whenever requested.

author: patilharsh03

title: Batch requests in ISR
body:
### Describe the feature you'd like to request

Hey!
So, by now we've to return pages slugs from `getStaticPaths`. e.g `[ { params: { id: 1 } }, { params: { id: 2 } } ]` 
And that means `getStaticProps` will do a requests 1 by 1 to some `/endpoint/[id]`
But in my case I've over `500` pages and I don't wanna DDOS `3rd party API` at build time
Therefore such `APIs` usually have batching endpoints like `getMultipleIds`, so in this case we can already grab all data inside of `getStaticPaths` and don't request it 1 by 1 in each `getStaticProps`. 

### Describe the solution you'd like

`getStaticPaths` exports not only `slugs`, but also a `data` for these `slugs`. e.g `[ { params: { id: 1, data: [] } } ]`

### Describe alternatives you've considered

By now I've to create own decorator, that will wait for all `ids` to come in

title: infinite renders / history.replaceState calls on error in dev (when running as non-standard spa)
body: ### Verify canary release - [X] I verified that the issue exists in Next.js canary release ### Provide environment information ``` yarn run v1.22.10 Operating System: Platform: darwin Arch: arm64 Version: Darwin Kernel Version 20.6.0: Wed Jan 12 22:22:45 PST 2022; root:xnu-7195.141.19~2/RELEASE_ARM64_T8101 Binaries: Node: 16.13.0 npm: 8.1.0 Yarn: 1.22.10 pnpm: N/A Relevant packages: next: 12.1.6-canary.6 react: 17.0.1 react-dom: 17.0.1 ✨ Done in 1.05s. ``` ### What browser are you using? (if relevant) chrome 100.0.4896.88 (Official Build) (arm64) | safari Version 15.4 (16613.1.17.1.13, 16613) ### How are you deploying your application? (if relevant) not deploying, running `next dev` ### Describe the Bug disclaimer: i'm trying to run a react-router spa inside the nextjs framework according to the [this gist](https://gist.github.com/tannerlinsley/65ac1f0175d79d19762cf06650707830) and [this article](https://colinhacks.com/essays/building-a-spa-with-nextjs). the goal is to migrate a large create-react-app-style spa one page at a time. the app appears to infinitely render when there's a client-side runtime error on dev while using rewrites in `next.config.js`. i've captured some screenshots. | browser | screenshot | notes | |-|-|-| | chrome | | kinda hard to see, but in the console badges there's been 12000+ errors. the tab eventually crashes. | | safari | | the last error message from safari is more helpful indicating it has blocked `history.replaceState` | in `next/dist/client/index.js`, i can seemingly prevent the error in `Container#componentDidMount` by only calling `router.replace` if it looks like we're gonna change the path. idk if that's actually helpful. i'm mostly fumbling at the keyboard looking for a root cause. but still, here's a screenshot of that "fix" (manually editing node_modules) and a link to where i applied the fix on github: | screenshot | github link | | - | - | | | [link](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/v12.1.6-canary.6/packages/next/client/index.tsx#L122) | ### Expected Behavior should show the development error page from `ReactDevOverlay`. i'm certainly aware the spa setup isn't really supported. be nice if it worked for migrating. be nice if the error overlay worked. will totally understand if this gets closed because it's not worth fixing / supporting. ### To Reproduce sample app: https://github.com/gkemmey/nextjs-react-router. i forked the example in the linked article above, updated next, react, and react-router. the app triggers a superficial runtime error by calling `console.foo()` in render of `pages/index.js`, and it has a superficial rewrite (to a path never used). as is, it should do the infinite rendering running `yarn dev`. if you ditch the rewrites, it should show the error overlay again.

author: ghost

title: Cannot import typescript module with generics from outside the project
body:
Hi,

We would like to re-use some of the existing typescript modules in our project, which are located outside of the next.js directory.

Imagine the following structure:
```
project/
  outside-module.ts
  nextjs/
    pages/
      index.tsx
```    

If I try to import a function with a _generic type_ from outside of the `nextjs` directory, I am getting the following error:

```typescript
// In file outside-module.ts:
export function OutsideProject<T>() {}
```

```
error - ../outside-module.ts
Module parse failed: Unexpected token (1:30)
You may need an appropriate loader to handle this file type, currently no loaders are configured to process this file. See https://webpack.js.org/concepts#loaders
> export function OutsideProject<T>() {
| }
|
```

Inside the `nextjs` directory, everything works fine.

Here is an example on github: https://github.com/dennisfrostlander/next-js-generic:
* [Here](https://github.com/dennisfrostlander/next-js-generic/blob/main/my-app/pages/index.tsx#L8-L9) we call a generic function from inside and outside the project dir.
* The error is reported only for the module from outside the project dir.

title: behavior of Suspense on the server changes between v12.1.0 and v12.1.5
body:
### Descriptions
* After I upgraded nextjs to the latest one. I think behavior of suspense on the server changes.
* In the previous version, initial html renders suspense fallback instead of waiting suspended component to be rendered. However, in the latest one, initial html request waits suspended component to be resolved and includes it in the first response.
* I think the previous behavior was accurate than now. (Am I misunderstanding how suspense on the server works?)
* I made two codesandbox. In the first codesandbox, you can check initial html render suspense fallback. However, second codesandbox waits react-query useQuery suspense queryFn to be resolved and includes it in the initial html.
  * [first codesandbox](https://codesandbox.io/s/nextjs-react-rc-9h7d3e?file=/pages/_app.js)
    *  versions
        * react, react-dom: 18.0.0-rc.0 (I can't update react pacakges to stable version cause nextjs is not working with it)
        * nextjs: 12.1.0
  * [second codesandbox](https://codesandbox.io/s/nextjs-react-query-v4-ucf8z0?file=/pages/_app.js)
    * versions
      * react, react-dom: 18.0.0
      * nextjs: 12.1.5 (latest version)

Is there anyone who knows what change is made related to suspense?
Or, If I misunderstands how suspense on the server, let me know. Thanks.

title: What is a usecase for NextResponse.json() method in middlewares?
body: The obvious example would be API routes, but the status cannot be changed after creating the response this way, which makes it of very limited use. For non-200 statuses json responses [have to be written this way](https://github.com/vercel/examples/blob/main/edge-functions/json-response/pages/_middleware.ts), which is quite verbose.

title: Avoid over-optimization of Notion/AWS S3 images (or any image where the url changes across time)
body:
Edit @leerob: [Check out this post](https://twitter.com/jitl/status/1518576164083834880)!

---


I'd like to make the Next.js image component work better when using Notion as a source for images. I don't believe there is a way to make Next.js understand that the image "X" that was optimized before is the same as the image "Y", since it relies on the URL to resolve whether the image is new or not.

Here is [an example](https://github.com/transitive-bullshit/nextjs-notion-starter-kit/issues/a310-4030-b272-fcff8dca66ab/Untitled.png?X-Amz-Algorithm=AWS4-HMAC-SHA256&X-Amz-Content-Sha256=UNSIGNED-PAYLOAD&X-Amz-Credential=AKIAT73L2G45EIPT3X45%2F20220425%2Fus-west-2%2Fs3%2Faws4_request&X-Amz-Date=20220425T191329Z&X-Amz-Expires=86400&X-Amz-Signature=52e726e4d24b7de6a619ab1ad8aca14baed761d1d1f4d4ee6716505ef3349bfa&X-Amz-SignedHeaders=host&x-id=GetObject&w=1200&q=75) of the image that is used by Notion.

I wonder if this pattern might be common enough for Next.js to handle it gracefully, or if it's something too specific to a particular provider (Notion) to be handled by the framework.

Or, if that use-case of "an image for which the url changes over time" is widely-enough used to provide an alternative way to make the Next.js framework understand that it was optimized before and shouldn't be optimized again.

```
{
```

```
  },
  "include": ["next-env.d.ts", "**/*.ts", "**/*.tsx"],
  "exclude": ["node_modules"],
  "references": [{ "path": "../schemas" }]
}
```
the root tsconfig
```
{
  "compilerOptions": {
    "baseUrl": ".",
    "composite": true,
    "declaration": true,
    "module": "commonjs",
    "noImplicitAny": false,
```

title: SSG DB access with Docker build

I've tried using `depends_on` in a `docker-compose.yml`, passing various things into `--add-host`, as well as replacing `localhost` with `host.docker.internal` in my `.env` file, but nothing seems to work. author: sebsonjura

title: Static Flavors / Static Dark-Light Theme support for dynamic generated css
body:
### Describe the feature you'd like to request

Next.js is currently nearly perfect when it comes to ssg, but there is one big feature missing for all emotion users (maybe other similar packages too). You really can't add a dark-light mode toggle to your website because it breaks everything. The problem is that you really can just make one statically generated version of a component/document/app for light or dark theme. If you are just using emotion/css to achive your dark and light theme, you may send two emotion caches to the client and it should decide which to use. This is not optimal in every sense.

### Describe the solution you'd like

I've two idea how to solve this problem:

Allow generating different flavors of an component/document/app with different props. Then decide in the on getServerSideProps which flavor to use. Depending on the request data (Cookie, JWT-Token, other HTTP-Header, .. ) you can directly serve the dark mode version for example.

Provide a way to be able to mess with the initialprops for each request. So that you can save both flavors into the intialprops with prerendering. And then decide which initial props to use.

I know they are both kinda similar, but the second one opens more possiblities. The first one is the more safe way

### Describe alternatives you've considered

If there is a work around I haven't found yet, please tell me. We really need this. We want to add an dark mode to our homepage, but can't without disabling ssg and only use ssr.

title: Next.js should console a notice if `revalidate`, amongst other SSG/SSR keys, were accidently placed in `props`
body:
### Describe the feature you'd like to request

It would be great if Next.js provided a notice in the console if `revalidate`, amongst other SSG/SSR return value keys were accidently placed in `props`. Obviously a key such as `revalidate` is valid in `props`, but it is likely the user misplaced it. Having a gentle nudge in the console could help save developer's time from posting issues/discussions asking why certain functionality isn't working. Such as:

> `revalidate` was found in `props`. Did you mean to place it in the return statement?

### Describe the solution you'd like

Ideally, when one of these SSR/SSG return keys are defined in props, a notice (not an error) should be logged to the console informing the user that it's possible they have mistakenly placed their SSR/SSG property within `props`.

### Describe alternatives you've considered

Alternative considered would be to simply carry on without notifying the user. As this is still a valid use case, it makes sense. However, I have seen first-hand this behavior causing issues.

I would be happy to PR a solution if the team thinks this would be beneficial!

title: Support react-native-web via swc transform
body:
### Describe the feature you'd like to request

[react-native-web](https://necolas.github.io/react-native-web/) is becoming increasingly popular to pair with Next, with package like [Solito](https://solito.dev) making it even easier. It's also included in the [official examples list](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/tree/canary/examples/with-react-native-web). CRA includes built-in support for RNW as well.

But using RNW forces you to opt out of using `swc` because of the need for [babel-plugin-react-native-web](https://www.npmjs.com/package/babel-plugin-react-native-web) and thus a `babel.config.js`. Similar to the work done for styled-components and relay, an swc transform for this relatively lightweight plugin would be great for those adding RN/RNW to their workflows.

### Describe the solution you'd like

Built-in support for swc tranforms of react-native to react-native-web components.

### Describe alternatives you've considered

Learning Rust and doing this myself does sound fun, but I'm not quite there yet. 😄 Otherwise, continuing to use the babel transform works, just more slowly.

title: Initializing firebase shouldn't be called from component

I think this example may not be best practice. A component could call this code over and over to initialize firebase. title: Wrong assumption that args._[0] is a project directory or something like that
body:
### Verify canary release

- [X] I verified that the issue exists in Next.js canary release

### Provide environment information

    Operating System:
      Platform: linux
      Arch: x64
      Version: #45-Ubuntu SMP Tue Mar 29 14:48:14 UTC 2022
    Binaries:
      Node: 16.15.0
      npm: 8.5.5
      Yarn: 1.22.15
      pnpm: 6.11.0
    Relevant packages:
      next: 12.1.5
      react: 18.1.0
      react-dom: 18.1.0

### What browser are you using? (if relevant)

N/A

### How are you deploying your application? (if relevant)

N/A

### Describe the Bug

I'm running the app under supervisor PM2 and once I start it, `next/bin/next` assumes that the first argument in `process.argv` is a command [see here](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/canary/packages/next/bin/next.ts#L56). In the reality, the `argv` looks like this
```js
[
  '/path/to/bin/node',
  '/path/to/app/node_modules/pm2/lib/ProcessContainer.js',
  'ecosystem.config.js',
  'start'
]
```
So in this case, neither the command nor project directory is not what is expected to be, and nextjs throws an error that `/path/to/app/ecosystem.config.js/.env` is not found and running in dev mode




### Expected Behavior

expected to identify command and directory

### To Reproduce

Create a nextjs app and install pm2. Then create an `ecosystem.config.js` file
```js
module.exports = {
  apps: [
    {
      name: 'app',
      script: './node_modules/next/dist/bin/next',
      args: 'start'
    }
  ]
}
```
then start the app using `yarn pm2-runtime ecosystem.config.js`

title: RFC: New Next.js Polyfill Behavior
body:
## Background

Next.js has `next-polyfill-nomodule` and `next-polyfill-module`. The implementation detail of the module can be found here:

- [`next-polyfill-nomodule`](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/canary/packages/next-polyfill-nomodule/src/index.js)
- [`next-polyfill-module`](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/canary/packages/next-polyfill-module/src/index.js)

Next.js uses `<script nomodule>` to load `next-polyfill-nomodule` on unsupported browsers only, and bundles `next-polyfill-module` in the `main` bundle (along with Next.js client-side code).

In RFC https://github.com/vercel/next.js/discussions/33227 (Implemented by https://github.com/vercel/next.js/pull/36584), Next.js is starting experiments dropping legacy browsers support. 

## Pitfalls

The current approach greatly reduces the bundle size when first introduced back in Next.js 9.x. However, it has some problems:

- Next.js will always include the polyfill for `String.prototype.trimStart`, `String.prototype.trimEnd`, `Array.prototype.flat`, and `Promise.prototype.finally` in the `main` bundle (because they are all parts of `next-polyfill-module`), even if a website doesn't use any of those features, or doesn't target legacy browsers.
- Next.js doesn't include polyfills for Math functions (https://github.com/vercel/next.js/issues/11714, https://github.com/vercel/next.js/issues/14716), and developers have to manually include and ship the polyfill in the `main` bundle even for browsers that support it.
- Next.js doesn't include every polyfill required for full ES2019 compatibility (It lacks the polyfill for `Object.fromEntries`). See issues https://github.com/vercel/next.js/issues/16715,  https://github.com/vercel/next.js/issues/33072, https://github.com/vercel/next.js/pull/33170, and https://github.com/vercel/next.js/pull/36426.
- With the ECMAScript Spec rolling out new features every year, the size of the `next-polyfill-module` will continuously increase. ES2020 introduces `BigInt`, `String.prototype.matchAll`, and `Promise.allSettled`, ES2021 introduces `String.prototype.replaceAll`, `Promise.any`, and `WeakRef`. And in June of this year, ES2022 will introduce `String.prototype.at`, `Array.prototype.at`, `TypedArray.prototype.at`, and `Object.hasOwn`. The bundle size will become huge even if we only include part of those features' polyfill into `next-module-polyfill`. And once again, not everyone is using all of the new ES features or targets old browsers.
- `core-js` not only provides polyfills for features not supported on legacy browsers but also corrects the behavior for those browsers that implement features incorrectly. For example, `core-js` provides [a workaround for a critical bug about `Array.prototype.reduce` introduced in Chrome 80-82](https://github.com/zloirock/core-js/blob/ce52fdc735c5c809c9e85b2072f92d41b5a3885a/packages/core-js/modules/es.array.reduce.js), [a workaround for a critical bug about `Array.prototype.reverse` introduced in Safari 12](https://github.com/zloirock/core-js/blob/fcbc2c878535b277f840997ab8ee819efd677888/packages/core-js/modules/es.array.reverse.js), [a workaround for a wrong implementation of `Reflect.set` on Edge 18](https://github.com/zloirock/core-js/blob/191151573225fac474b4ebd7c22acc0588505d34/packages/core-js/modules/es.reflect.set.js#L38-L45). Since Next.js doesn't use `core-js` (it maintains a `next-polyfill-module`), although the bundle size is reduced, the critical bug fixes from `core-js` is also omitted.

## Possible Solutions

The proposal is trying to accomplish these goals:

- Next.js should only load required polyfills on legacy browsers.
- Next.js shouldn't include any polyfill for features that sites do not use at all.
- Next.js should include critical bug fixes from `core-js`.
- Next.js should allow sites to experiment latest shipped proposals (E.g. `Object.hasOwn` is already in ECMAScript stage 4, is shipped in Firefox, Chrome, and Safari 15.4 at the time being, and will be landed in ES2022)

Here I propose two solutions:

### Solution 1

- Deprecates `next-polyfill-nomodule`.
- If `browserlist` targets browsers without `<script nomodule>`, Next.js should create two different sets of bundles, one set targets legacy browsers that doesn't have `<script nomodule>` support, and another set targets modern browsers with `<script module>` support. The final emitted HTML will be like this:
  ```html
  <head>
    <!-- old ECMAScript syntax and polyfills for legacy browsers. This will only load on legacy browsers and Safari 10.1 -->
    <script nomodule defer src="main.[hash1].js"></script>
    <!-- modern ECMAScript syntax and polyfills only for modern browsers. This will only load on modern browsers and Safari 10.1 -->
    <script module src="main.[hash2].js"></script>
  </head>
  ```
- If `browserlist` only targets modern browser, Next.js should only create one bundle. The final emitted HTML will be like this:
  ```html
  <head>
    <!-- modern ECMAScript syntax and polyfills only for modern browsers. This will only load on modern browsers and Safari 10.1 -->
    <script module src="main.[hash3].js"></script>
  </head>
  ```

The approach is used by [Parcel bundler](https://parceljs.org/), and Vue CLI (when creating a new Vue app).

#### Pros:

- We can fully utilize SWC's `env.mode` (Babel's [`useBuiltins`](https://babeljs.io/docs/en/babel-preset-env#usebuiltins)).
With `mode` set to `usage`, SWC / Babel will only include required polyfills.

#### Cons:

- ~~According to the SWC's documentation:~~
  ~~> The `usage` mode is currently not as efficient as Babel, yet.~~
- It will emit two different sets of bundles, which means double the compilation time and double the disk space occupied.
- It may reduce the cache ratio of the CDN.
- Safari 10.1 supports `<script module>` but doesn't support `<script nomodule>`, which means Safari 10.1 will download and executed all of the codes.
- If a site defines a `browserlist` that targets legacy browsers, we will have to manually parse and split it into two ranges, one legacy range (for `<script nomodule>`) and one modern range (for `<script module>`).

### Solution 2

- We will keep `next-polyfill-nomodule` to include polyfills targeting legacy browsers without `<script nomodule>` supports.
- We will let SWC / Babel transforms the modern JavaScript syntax to support browsers defined in `browserlists`.
- We will let SWC / Babel only include polyfills from `core-js` to target the modern browsers that support `<script nomodule>`.
- The final HTML output will be like this:
  ```html
  <head>
    <!-- old ECMAScript syntax, polyfills for legacy browsers only. This will only load on legacy browsers and Safari 10.1 -->
    <script nomodule defer src="polyfill.[hash1].js"></script>
    <!-- old ECMAScript syntax, polyfills for modern browsers only. This will load on all browsers -->
    <script src="main.[hash2].js"></script>
  </head>
  ```

#### Pros

- Next.js only needs to create one set of a bundle and a separate polyfill bundle from `next-polyfill-nomodule`.

#### Cons

- ~~According to the SWC's documentation:~~
  ~~> The `usage` mode is currently not as efficient as Babel, yet.~~
- Currently, it is impossible for SWC or Babel to both transforms modern ECMAScript syntax to legacy compatible syntax while including polyfills from `core-js` to target only modern browsers.
- If a site defines a `browserlist` that targets legacy browsers, we will have to manually parse and split it into two ranges, one legacy range (when transforming syntax) and one modern range (to include polyfills for modern browsers).

----

cc @kdy1 @styfle @timneutkens @developit

title: Support specifying `onRecoverableError` for `ReactDOM.hydrateRoot`
body:
### Describe the feature you'd like to request

With the upgrade to React 18, we are getting a lot of hydration errors. These can be hard to debug on production because the source maps are minified. 

I'd like to see the ability to pass in `onRecoverableError` to [`hydrateRoot`](https://reactjs.org/docs/react-dom-client.html#hydrateroot) which will allow better logging of errors to help us track down hydration issues. (This would also let us ignore hydration errors if we decide to, or not send them to Sentry.)

### Describe the solution you'd like

You can define an `onRecoverableError` in `next.config.js` which will be called on [`hydrateRoot` ](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/search?q=hydrateRoot).

### Describe alternatives you've considered

I've tried tracking down hydration errors and I've found some of them, but not all of them.

title: Generate non-HTML files on `next export`
body:
### Describe the feature you'd like to request

When using `next export`, I would like to generate non-HTML files, but this is not currently supported.

For example, we may want to do the following.

- `GET /feed.xml` to return XML for RSS
- `GET /sitemap.txt` to return text for Google crawler

With SSR, we can use API Routes and Rewrites, or `getServerProps` to prepare such endpoints, but with SSG by `next export`, none of these functions are available.

Are there any plans to support the ability to generate non-HTML files with `next export`?

### Describe the solution you'd like

If we can default export functions that return non-HTML content on regular pages, we should be able to achieve this using `getStaticPaths` and `getStaticProps`.

### Describe alternatives you've considered

I saw the following workaround in some blog posts:

- Create non-HTML files in `./public` directory between `next build` and `next export`
    - e.g. https://github.com/iamvishnusankar/next-sitemap recommends this apporach
- Create non-HTML files in `./public` directory in `getStaticProps` of some pages

title: reason for importing from next/dist?
body:
I'm working on a change that updates some types, which is failing during `yarn lint` in `body.test.ts`:

```
import { Crypto } from 'next/dist/server/web/sandbox/polyfills'
...
beforeAll(() => {
  ...
  global['crypto'] = new Crypto()   // fails because Crypto is missing some properties
```

`Crypto`, defined in `polyfills.ts`, receives those properties by extending from its dependency `@peculiar/webcrypto`. I believe that the TypeScript compiler cannot figure this out, however, because both `body.test.ts` and `polyfills.ts` imports from the compiled `dist` directory instead of from the TypeScript sources themselves.

Is there a reason for this? If so, what would be the way to work around it?

P.S. For context, the PR I'm working on is https://github.com/vercel/next.js/pull/36562.
author: tjni

title: Environment variable load order:  Opposite of what docs say?
body:
Hello!
I'm working on setting up different environments and was reading the "[environment variable load order](https://nextjs.org/docs/basic-features/environment-variables#environment-variable-load-order)" part of the docs.

It currently says that (just focusing on the `dev` example for now):

> Environment Variables are loaded from the following sources in **top-to-bottom order**. In all environments, the existing env is not overridden by following sources:
>
>`NODE_ENV=development`
>
> 1.  `.env.development.local`
> 2.  `.env.local`
> 3.  `.env.development`
> 4.  `.env`

My understanding from reading that is that if I had the same environment variable defined in each of those files, that it would be successively overwritten as each next environment file got read-in, from top to bottom.  Thus, I would expect the one defined in `.env.local` to take precedence over the one defined in `.env.development.local`, and so on and so forth, down to the one defined in `.env`.

However, my testing shows that it appears to happen in the opposite order.  That is to say, it appears to be happening from **bottom-to-top**.

Is the wording in that docs page wrong or am I misinterpreting them?

Thanks!

--------
**UPDATE** (5/04/22)

Based on the answers from @thibautsabot and @icyJoseph, it appears that Next.js is using a **"first one wins" approach** with respect to specific variables defined in more than one env file.

My follow-up question to that is, isn't that backwards from how most software systems work?  Isn't "last one wins" the norm?  I'm not sure if env-file-aggregation usually goes against that norm though, so I'm interested in learning that. :-)

Since I don't see Next.js changing their approach to the "last one wins" norm, I would recommend (if possible) updating the description in the docs.  Personally, I did not find "In all environments, the existing env is not overridden by following sources" to communicate "first one wins" at all.

If others agree that it is worth updating the docs, what is the next step?

title: Dynamically import only used components to a page/catch-all route (statically optimized)
body:
Working with a headless CMS and catch all routes we have had the problem of all components being imported to every single page, even if they are not used. This is a big problem because it results in a lot of unused javascript, which heavily affects your performance score. 

We've tried to use `next/dynamic` ([#11291](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/discussions/11291)), but for a ton of components this leads to a ton of extra requests which again doesn't help performance and sometimes leads to layout shifts and inconsistency because of missing javascript that is just being loaded.

Is there any way to dynamically render and import only the needed components to a page's initial javascript, without using `next/dynamic` and loosing prerendering?

author: janpio

title: When including font file in build output, add `<link rel="preload">` for those files
body:
### Describe the feature you'd like to request

When Next.js finds a `@font-face` statement in the CSS that points to a local font file, Next.js puts that file into the `.next/static/media` folder with a content hash. However, the font files don't have a `<link rel="preload">` element to start loading them before the CSS is downloaded and parsed. This creates a request waterfall that prevents the page's text from displaying as quickly as it could.

Next.js should provide some way to preload necessary font files.

### Describe the solution you'd like

Naive solution: any font file that is encountered and moved to the `.next/static/media` folder should have a corresponding `<link rel="preload>` element.
Issue with naive solution: just because a font is referenced in a `@font-face` rule, doesn't mean that that font is referenced by a CSS statement, and it doesn't mean that CSS statement applies to any text on the page. Preloading every font file could have a negative performance impact due to loading more fonts than the page actually requires.

Better solution: expose the list of fonts found to the page somehow, and allow the developer to determine what to preload, either in `_document.js` or `_app.js`.

### Describe alternatives you've considered

If the font exists in the `/public` folder, then we know the path to it at dev time (because there's no content hash on the outputted file). However, as @alexasselin008 describes in [his discussion post here](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/discussions/35554), this isn't always possible if the `@font-face` rules aren't under our control.

I tried playing around with the `file-loader` Webpack plugin, but I was unable to get the content hash to match the content hash that Next.js creates and injects into the CSS.

title: Environment variables can't be sourced indirectly
body:
I have an issue using environment variables with NextJS.

Basically I collect all ENV variable in a config file and use this config file in my project.
In this case I can't source the config file in my util function but I can source the env variable directly.

What's wrong?

**Project structure**
```
|__ config
|    |__ index.js
|
|__ pages
|    |__ api
|    |    |__ formHandler.js
|    |
|    |__ page.js
|
|__ utils
    |__ index.js
```

**config/index.js**
```javascript
export const getEnvVar = (envVar) => {
  const unvalidatedEnvVar = process.env[envVar]

  if (!unvalidatedEnvVar) {
    throw new Error(`Couldn't find environment variable: ${envVar}`)
  } else {
    return unvalidatedEnvVar
  }
}

export const config = {
  PREFIX: getEnvVar('MY_SECRET_PREFIX'),
  STRING: getEnvVar('MY_SECRET_STRING'),
}
```

**utils/index.js**
```javascript
import { config } from '@config'

export const getPrefixedString = (string) => {
  const prefix = config.PREFIX // THIS causes getEnvVar to throw

  return `${prefix}_${string}`
}
```

**page/api/formHandler.js**
```javascript
import { config } from '@config'
import { getPrefixedString } from '@utils'

const prefixedString = getPrefixedString(config.STRING)

export default async (req, res) => {
  return res.status(200).json({ string: prefixedString })
}

```

**page/page.js**

```javascript
// ... a form component which onSubmit calls the formHandler api throw fetch('/api/formHandler')
```

title: [Tree Shake] Next.js emit unused code into the `main` bundle
body: Originally posted by **@SukkaW** April 5, 2022 at [#35900](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/issues/35900) ### Verify canary release - [X] I verified that the issue exists in Next.js canary release ### Provide environment information Operating System: Platform: darwin Arch: x64 Version: Darwin Kernel Version 21.4.0: Mon Feb 21 20:34:37 PST 2022; root:xnu-8020.101.4~2/RELEASE_X86_64 Binaries: Node: 16.14.0 npm: 8.5.1 Yarn: 1.22.18 pnpm: 6.32.4 Relevant packages: next: 12.1.5-canary.1 react: 18.0.0 react-dom: 18.0.0 ### What browser are you using? (if relevant) _No response_ ### How are you deploying your application? (if relevant) next build / export ### Describe the Bug I notice the `main` bundle emitted by `next build` is a bit heavy, so I use `@next/bundle-analyzer` to analyze my bundle: And there are many features I don't use at all that are included in the bundle: **next/dist/shared/amp.js** I don't use the AMP feature at all. So I take a look at the source code. `shared/lib/amp.ts` has 2 named export, `useAmp` and `isInAmpMode`: https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/0eb9f7e76d0d7ec6aa7d85d3eaf57b151f381fb3/packages/next/shared/lib/amp.ts#L4-L15 `isInAmpMode` is then imported by `shared/lib/head.tsx`: https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/fbfe430cf0d21513eadb7be7e9c05a6ac6d5048a/packages/next/shared/lib/head.tsx#L5 I do use `next/head` component, so it is very reasonable that `isInAmpMode` is included in my bundle. However, `useAmp` is also included in the final bundle: **next/dist/client/script.js** I don't use `next/script` component at all. And the `scriptLoader` property of the `__NEXT_DATA__` is also an empty array: So I take a look at the source code again. `client/script.tsx` has a default export and a named export `initScriptLoader`: https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/fbfe430cf0d21513eadb7be7e9c05a6ac6d5048a/packages/next/client/script.tsx#L132-L134 ...which is then imported by `client/index.tsx`: https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/0eb9f7e76d0d7ec6aa7d85d3eaf57b151f381fb3/packages/next/client/index.tsx#L254-L257 However, the entire `next/dist/client/script.js` is still included in the final `main` bundle: > As you can see there are keywords like `text/partydown`, `afterInteractive` and `lazyOnLoad`. **next/dist/client/streaming/vitals.js** I do use `reportWebVitals` named export on my custom `_app`, but I don't use `unstable_useWebVitalsReport`. So once again, I take a look at the source code of `next.js`. There are tons of named export at `client/streaming/vitals.ts`: https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/0eb9f7e76d0d7ec6aa7d85d3eaf57b151f381fb3/packages/next/client/streaming/vitals.ts#L5 https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/0eb9f7e76d0d7ec6aa7d85d3eaf57b151f381fb3/packages/next/client/streaming/vitals.ts#L10-L12 https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/0eb9f7e76d0d7ec6aa7d85d3eaf57b151f381fb3/packages/next/client/streaming/vitals.ts#L19-L22 https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/0eb9f7e76d0d7ec6aa7d85d3eaf57b151f381fb3/packages/next/client/streaming/vitals.ts#L24 `flushBufferedVitalsMetrics` and `trackWebVitalMetric` are imported by `client/index.tsx`: https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/0eb9f7e76d0d7ec6aa7d85d3eaf57b151f381fb3/packages/next/client/index.tsx#L36-L39 ... which is used by `` component: https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/0eb9f7e76d0d7ec6aa7d85d3eaf57b151f381fb3/packages/next/client/index.tsx#L1032-L1036 ... and `hydrate` function: https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/0eb9f7e76d0d7ec6aa7d85d3eaf57b151f381fb3/packages/next/client/index.tsx#L323 However, `useWebVitalsReport` is also included in my final bundle: **next/dist/client/streaming/refresh.js** I am pretty sure I don't use `useRefreshRoot` at all. And Next.js itself doesn't uses `useRefreshRoot` either. However, it is still included in the final bundle: ### Expected Behavior All those unused codes I mentioned above should not be included in the final `main` bundle. ### To Reproduce I introduce `@next/bundle-analyzer` into another bare bone Next.js reproduction (the one I used in https://github.com/vercel/next.js/issues/35286), and all those unused codes are included in the `main` bundle:

title: Why is baseUrl: "." not actually needed?
body:
Yesterday I finished Next.js tutorials and today I began writing my first real app. One thing I didn't like during the tutorials was having to write relative imports like: `import Layout from '../../components/layout';` So first thing I did today was to look for a way to avoid that, and sure enough I found about `jsconfig.json` and `"baseUrl": "."`. However... to my surprise, I found it is actually not needed. Absolute import paths actually do work for me out-of-the-box! Consider the tree: ``` . ├── components │   └── layout.js └── pages ├── components │   └── layout.js └── index.js ``` Consider the components: `components/layout.js` returns `

Hello World!

` `pages/components/layout.js` returns `

Hello again World!

` I did the following tests with `index.js`. 1. `import Layout from '../components/layout'` gave `Hello World!` (as expected) 2. `import Layout from './components/layout'` gave `Hello again World!` (as expected) 3. `import Layout from 'components/layout'` gave `Hello World!` (NOT expected) 4. `import Layout from '/components/layout'` gave `Hello World!` (wasn't sure what to expect from this one) 5. `import Layout from 'pages/components/layout'` gave `Hello again World!` (expected this to fail compilation) 6. `import Layout from '/pages/components/layout'` gave `Hello again World!` (wasn't sure again) Neither test changed when I made a `jsconfig.json` file in the root, whatever its value for `baseURL` was. Thinking a little, I figured this wasn't so much unexpected, since non-relative imports like `next/head` do work. However, I expected the default behavior to be, at least, searching FIRST in the current directory, THEN in root -- that is, I very much expected Test #3 to be `Hello again World!`. Can somebody shed some light on this? If this really is the expected behavior, then why are relative imports always used in the tutorials, and why is `baseUrl` considered so important? PS: my Next.js version is 12.1.6

author: TuanaCelik

title: The Bypass and Preview Cookie Names should be configurable
body:
### Describe the feature you'd like to request

At the moment, the cookies used for bypass and preview data are hardcoded:

```js
// next.js
export const COOKIE_NAME_PRERENDER_BYPASS = `__prerender_bypass`
export const COOKIE_NAME_PRERENDER_DATA = `__next_preview_data`
```

This lends itself to a DDOS risk where someone can attempt to introduce these cookies to their requests in an attempt to bypass any edge-level caching.

### Describe the solution you'd like

If these could be configurable, perhaps via server-side environment variables, then we could add obscurity to the security of these cookies.

### Describe alternatives you've considered

An alternative might be introducing some custom middleware to rewrite the cookies for each request/response. If there's a recipe for doing that it might be useful.

That said, this is an easy to use vulnerability that could easily be closed by the use of environment variables and a recommendation in the docs to introduce a custom cookie name - perhaps with an environment-specific hash in it.
author: ashleyfrieze

title: Ability to disable "gssp-no-mutating-res" ServerResponse mutation warning
body:
### Describe the feature you'd like to request

As of Next12, when running locally Next will fail a page load with a Server Error if you mutate a `res` after the `getServerSideProps` functions have resolved. The error links you to this documentation page: https://nextjs.org/docs/messages/gssp-no-mutating-res

While this is almost always good, its introduction breaks the ability to work with some high-profile Next integrations like [Auth0's](https://github.com/auth0/nextjs-auth0/issues/524) because of the use of packages like [on-headers](https://www.npmjs.com/package/on-headers) that fire a callback at the end of the req/res cycle. While these use cases should be migrated to Next 12's middleware and/or edge function features, those are still in Beta and organizations like Okta need time to accommodate these changes. It would be nice if we could keep using both Next12 and integrations that were built for Next11 without having to hack in warning-suppression for this issue.

### Describe the solution you'd like

Ideally, a flag in next.config.js that would allow for this warning to be suppressed and for that flag to be documented on the corresponding landing page.

### Describe alternatives you've considered

I've considered opening a PR to help Auth0 migrate away from onHeaders, but I don't have time at the moment to learn the internals of their package to implement that change.

I will, in lieu of this, be implementing the [blanket suppression tactic](https://github.com/auth0/nextjs-auth0/issues/524#issuecomment-1013747054) suggested in their Issue's thread.

author: AlexandrosGounis

title: Use different Referrer-Policy for different origins
body:
Is it possible to use a different Referrer-Policy for different origins? 

I'm currently building an application that uses NextJS for the frontend and Express for the backend. I'm building a new feature for this application which allows a document owner to generate an `editToken`. Then, any user who visits a page with `?editToken=<valid token>` will have permissions to edit that document.

To perform authorization on the backend, I want to have that `editToken` query param to be sent with the request - then the backend can check if the provided edit token matches the value in the database for the specific document being viewed. 

I've found that since I'm running my frontend on port 3000 and my backend on port 5001 (locally), a Referrer-Policy of `strict-origin-when-cross-origin` strips out the query params - this means the backend doesn't know if the user is visiting a URL with the correct `editToken`. Similarly, when I run my app in production, the frontend runs at `https://mywebsite.com` and the backend at `https://api.mywebsite.com` which runs into the same cross origin problem. Ideally I would like to keep this referrer policy.

I've found I can set a Referrer-Policy of `no-referrer-when-downgrade` for the `editToken` to be passed to the backend as part of the request but I'd rather not leak info to origins I don't control.

Is there a way to dynamically change the Referrer-Policy based on which origin the request is going to? Alternatively, is there a way to whitelist certain origins to which all referrer info should be sent?
author: paymog

title: Cannot use <table> - "Hydration failed" - HTMLUnknownElement
body:
I cannot make a simple table using Next.js. The page crashes in browser with `Hydration failed because the initial UI does not match what was rendered on the server`. My environment is Next.js 12.1.4 in Windows 10 (WSL). ### Steps to reproduce 1. Create a new Next.js project (I used `npx create-next-app@latest`). 2. Create `pages/table.js` with the following contents: ``` export default () => (
Alien football stars
Player Gloobles Za'taak
TR-7 7 4,569
Khiresh Odo 7 7,223
Mia Oolong 9 6,219
) ``` Note: this example table was taken directly from [MDN](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTML/Element/th). 3. Start server (`npm run dev`) and visit page. ### Expected result Correct rendering of a table without errors. ### Actual result Page with table does render, but a big modal div is displayed over the page with the contents below -- view screenshot [here](https://postimg.cc/YGjmwm6K). ``` 1 of 3 unhandled errors Unhandled Runtime Error Error: Hydration failed because the initial UI does not match what was rendered on the server. author: darwin808

title: Next.js APIs should be auto-documentable and exist in a "routes" folder rather than a "pages" folder
body:
There are two issues that jump out at me: one feature that is lacking for professional API development and one semantic in nature.

### OpenAPI specs cannot be generated automatically

There is only one library that seems to work with Next.js for generating OpenAPI specs: next-swagger-doc. It is not an automatic tool. All specs are manually defined via JSDoc, which means there are multiple sources of truth and no real contract between the spec and the implementation.

There is a library I prefer when using Express, and it is called express-oas-generator. What it does is spy on all of your server's routes, and it generates OpenAPI specs based on what the server responds with. It cannot truly parse the code itself, but its spying can be extremely useful when paired with thorough testing. E2E testing therefore creates this contract between the code and the spec. If you test every response type, you will get good spec coverage.

I think this is something that Next.js could handle in the framework. But if not, it's something that is lacking in the community.

### /pages -> /routes

Here's what always annoyed me about `pages`: what if I want to deploy just a backend? I still have to write something at `/pages/api/someEndpoint.ts`. This is just nonsensical. I would rather it be called `routes`, which is an isomorphic concept. A "page" is only really a front end concept. Do you want Nextjs's API route functionality to _only_ be used as a backend-for-frontend in the same app that you have your front end, or do you want people to look at it as potentially a general purpose tool for writing backends quickly and painlessly?

I propose `/routes` and `/src/routes` in addition to or in replacement to `/pages/` and `/src/pages`. I would be fine with this as a hardcoded value, but it also seems reasonable to have literally a single config value that can be defined for where your `pages` dir is.

Also note that people use the 'router' abstraction on the front end and back end, so it _is_ a universal concept. There's react router, and there's express router.

(_Originally posted by @miloofcroton in https://github.com/vercel/next.js/discussions/23640#discussioncomment-2705487_)

title: NextResponse: use a decorator to handle cookies
body:
### Describe the feature you'd like to request

The `NextResponse` has built-in cookies manipulation, like:

```jsx
import { NextResponse } from 'next/server'

const response = new NextResponse()

// set a cookie
response.cookies.set('foo, 'bar') // => set-cookie: 'foo=bar; Path=/'`

// set another cookie
response.cookies.set('fooz, 'barz') // => set-cookie: 'foo=bar; Path=/, fooz=barz; Path=/'`

// delete a cookie means mark it as expired
response.cookies.delete('foo') // => set-cookie: 'foo=; Path=/; Expires=Thu, 01 Jan 1970 00:00:00 GMT, fooz=barz; Path=/'`

// clear all cookies means mark all of them as expired
response.cookies.clear() // => set-cookie: 'fooz=; Path=/; Expires=Thu, 01 Jan 1970 00:00:00 GMT, foo=; Path=/; Expires=Thu, 01 Jan 1970 00:00:00 GMT'`
```

The extra logic for handling cookies nicely means extra bytes in your bundle.

Even you don't need to manipulate cookies, your bundle contains the cookie's logic, since this logic is attached to `NextResponse`. 

### Describe the solution you'd like

So users that don't need to manipulate cookies can potentially can save some bytes in their bundles.

That could be achieved adding the cookies logic to `NextResponse` just when it's necessary, like using a decorator:

```jsx
import { NextResponse, withCookies } from 'next/server'

const response = withCookies(new NextResponse())
```

title: Feedback: Script `worker` strategy
body:
*tl.dr:* Thread to collect feedback on `<Script strategy="worker" />`

----

Details: In v12.1.1-canary.11, `next/script` introduced a new `worker` strategy that allows you to relocate and execute third-party scripts in a web worker with Partytown (see [documentation](https://nextjs.org/docs/basic-features/script#off-loading-scripts-to-a-web-worker-experimental)). This strategy is still experimental but I would like to use this thread to gather all types of feedback.

Some questions that may be useful to answer (although any feedback is worth sharing):

- Have you noticed any performance benefits after switching to the `worker` strategy for any scripts on your site?
- Have there been any scripts that failed to load after you switched to using the `worker` strategy?
- Do you have any problems with the current developer experience?
- Is there anything that can be improved with the current documentation?

title: Ability to store small amount of data in serverless functions provided by vercel
body:
### Describe the feature you'd like to request

# My Use Case
I was writing an app which uses Next Js Api routes. These Api routes are deployed by Vercel in form of serverless function. The Functions boot up on request and shut downs after a specific amout of time. My app needs a small amount of storage (So Small to consider a database, Just a few key-value pairs carrying same value for everyone). I need the storage like so that I can read and write that storage anytime and it have to hold up that data till the next deployment. 
# The Problem
I initially tried using a JSON file and fs module but file read write is not allowed in serverless functions.
Then i tried using `global` variable. It works good in local environment but in serverless function it stays for sometime and gets reset after sometime.
My project needs only 5-6 key value pair storage and bringing an database would be an overkill.
# My Request
A Simple Key Value Paired database like `localStorage` to hold data during the lifespan of an deployment. (Wouldn't mind if the data gets reset after each deployment)

### Describe the solution you'd like

I think a new module like `next/database` should be added which can hold some data after each deployment.
Keys for storing data may be decided initially with their initial Value. Dynamic Keys may be also considered.
Also we need two function to read and write data.
I think Vercel's infrastructure also need to be modified to hold this data.

### Describe alternatives you've considered

I am not able to figure out any permanent solution till now.
I have another github repo setup where i have hosted an file and change that according to my need and fetch that data in api routes.
It works but is very painfull.
Data can only be read through api routes and writes are to be done manually.
