author: Lolly1150

title: Serverless execution environment shouldn't be shared between all branches on a project
body:
I found out The Hard Way™ that a failed/failing serverless function on one branch within a project can spiral and cause unintended side effects for other branches.

One thing I love about Vercel is the ability to leverage automatic preview apps that are quickly created for every commit. It's nice to use preview branches both as a place to review changes and share them with stakeholders but I've also thought of them as a safe staging ground for engineering changes. This thought process is heavily influenced by past usage of review apps on Heroku that are a completely isolated environment from production.

Last night, a change was pushed up that caused an infinite redirect in a preview environment. This function's execution spiraled and caused the dreaded 429 error[^429]. Not only did the preview app start failing, but because the limits are project-based[^project], the production app started timing out as well.

My expectation would be that errors on a preview app would be sandboxed to that preview app itself. I would expect an error in `branch-new-feature`, for example, to:
- contribute to execution errors in my preview deployment for `branch-new-feature`
- bloat my monthly bill for usage overages

I would not expect:
- The production deployment to be affected

I'm flagging this issue mostly to say that this is a pain point and was unexpected behavior for me. As a workaround, I thought about creating a new project for all preview apps but according to the docs:

> Serverless Functions can only have a maximum concurrency of 1000 across **all projects within a given account scope and region**.

With this wording, it seems that simply creating a new project would not alleviate my issue. If I were to create a new project under my current team **AND** assign a different serverless function execution region (something other than the default of Washington DC), would any 429 errors only affect functions in that region or would it have adverse effects for all my projects?

[^429]: https://vercel.com/support/articles/what-should-i-do-if-i-receive-a-429-error-on-vercel
[^project]: https://vercel.com/support/articles/what-should-i-do-if-i-receive-a-429-error-on-vercel#concurrency-limit
author: imjared

title: next/image does not seem to follow redirects
body: I've been experimenting with next/image and found that it does not follow HTTP redirects. Here's what I'd like to accomplish: An API route `/api/images/[imageId]` responds with a redirect: ``` GET /api/images/123 ``` ``` HTTP/1.1 302 Found Connection: keep-alive Date: Tue, 10 May 2022 14:15:06 GMT Keep-Alive: timeout=5 Location: https://redacted.s3.eu-central-1.amazonaws.com/redacted-signed-url-goes-here Transfer-Encoding: chunked ``` The `/api/images/[imagedId]` route creates a [signed URL to read an AWS S3 object](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonS3/latest/userguide/ShareObjectPreSignedURL.html). It then redirects to said URL with the `Location` header. This works fine in a regular `` tag. As soon as I try to use it with `next/image` though, it fails. This is the HTTP response I'm getting: ``` GET /_next/image\?url\=%2Fapi%2Fimages%2F123\&w\=640\&q\=75 ``` ``` HTTP/1.1 302 Found Cache-Control: public, max-age=0, must-revalidate Connection: keep-alive Content-Security-Policy: script-src 'none'; sandbox; Date: Tue, 10 May 2022 14:17:24 GMT ETag: 47DEQpj8HBSa+-TImW+5JCeuQeRkm5NMpJWZG3hSuFU= Keep-Alive: timeout=5 Transfer-Encoding: chunked Vary: Accept ``` The body is empty. `` does not render anything and there are no logs in the console. Is this intended behaviour? Why isn't the redirect being followed? Am I doing something wrong? **Why do I even want this?** My intention is to have a constant URL that can be used as a cache key by `next/image` that then returns a temporary signed URL to S3. This way I don't need to make the S3 objects public. If I would just pass the signed URL to `next/image`, they would be different for each request and the caching mechanism would be broken.

title: Can't resolve 'fs' in Next 12.1.6
body:
I went to go upgrade to Next@12.1.6 but there looks to be a _bug_. I have a a file called `build-rss.js` that looks like

```js
import fs from 'fs'
import { Feed } from 'feed'
import { parseISO } from 'date-fns'
import { getClient } from '@lib/sanity.server'
import { allPosts } from '@lib/sanityGroqQueries'
import { urlForImage } from '@lib/sanity'
import toPlainText from './toPlainText'

const generateRssFeed = async () => {
  const baseUrl = 'https://www.redshirtsports.xyz'
  const posts = await getClient().fetch(allPosts)

  const feed = new Feed({
    title: 'Redshirt Sports',
    description: 'All the latest news from Redshirt Sports.',
    id: baseUrl,
    link: baseUrl,
    image: `${baseUrl}/images/icons/RS_horizontal_513x512.png`,
    favicon: `${baseUrl}/favicon.ico`,
    copyright: `All rights reserved ${new Date().getFullYear()}, Redshirt Sports`,
    feedLinks: {
      rss: `${baseUrl}/feeds/feed.xml`,
      json: `${baseUrl}/feeds/feed.json`,
      atom: `${baseUrl}/feeds/atom.xml`,
    },
    author: {
      name: 'James Singleton',
      link: 'https://twitter.com/@Design__Pattern',
    },
  })

  posts.forEach((post) => {
    const url = `${baseUrl}/${post.slug}`

    feed.addItem({
      title: post.title,
      id: url,
      link: url,
      description: post.excerpt,
      content: toPlainText(post.body),
      author: [
        {
          name: post.author.name,
          link: post.author.twitterURL,
        },
      ],
      date: parseISO(post.publishedAt),
      image: urlForImage(post.mainImage).url(),
    })
  })

  fs.mkdirSync('./public/feeds', { recursive: true })
  fs.writeFileSync('./public/feeds/feed.xml', feed.rss2())
  fs.writeFileSync('./public/feeds/atom.xml', feed.atom1())
  fs.writeFileSync('./public/feeds/feed.json', feed.json1())
}

generateRssFeed()
```

And it is called in the `next.config.js` like

```js
webpack(config, options) {
    if (!options.dev && options.isServer) {
      let originalEntry = config.entry

      config.entry = async () => {
        let entries = { ...(await originalEntry()) }
        entries['lib/build-rss'] = './lib/build-rss.js'
        return entries
      }
    }

    return config
  },
```

My `postbuild` script looks like `"postbuild": "if [ -f './.next/serverless/lib/build-rss.js' ]; then node ./.next/serverless/lib/build-rss.js; else node ./.next/server/lib/build-rss.js; fi"`. This worked in 12.1.5 but upgrading to 12.1.6 breaks this with 

```
Failed to compile.

./lib/build-rss.js:1:0
Module not found: Can't resolve 'fs'
> 1 | import fs from 'fs'
  2 | import { Feed } from 'feed'
  3 | import { parseISO } from 'date-fns'
  4 | import { getClient } from '@lib/sanity.server'

https://nextjs.org/docs/messages/module-not-found
```

title: Serve CMS images from CDN

Currently the final build thats served from the CDN still has the images being served from the ghost instance. That makes sense but is problematic because our small ghost instance cannot handle very much traffic.

Is there an easy way to have the site pull from ghost when it is building and download the images into the public folder or something so all of the contend is served from the CDN?

title: Upgrade to MDX 2
body:
### Describe the feature you'd like to request

Currently MDX 2 is supported, but everything in Next.js is focused on supporting MDX 1. I would like to update this. I noticed this while implementing #36815.

### Describe the solution you'd like

- The [`with-mdx`](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/tree/canary/examples/with-mdx) example is updated to use MDX 2
- The [`with-mdx-remote`](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/tree/canary/examples/with-mdx-remote) example is updated to `next-mdx-remote` 4
- Preferably `@next/mdx` uses a dependency on `@mdx-js/loader` instead of a peer dependency.
- The `@next/mdx` peer dependency on `@mdx-js/react` is removed, as it’s redundant now.

I’ll gladly do this myself. Just let me know if you’ll accept a PR for this.

### Describe alternatives you've considered

The alternative is not to upgrade, but I think it’s a bad idea to keep suggesting older versions of MDX.

title: [Feature Request] Run TypeScript check and ESLint simultaneously
body:
### Describe the feature you'd like to request

Currently, Next.js runs TypeScript type check and ESLint one by one:

https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/b122178ead5f6a50e0ea543e1e806de93bde23af/packages/next/build/index.ts#L245-L255
https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/b122178ead5f6a50e0ea543e1e806de93bde23af/packages/next/build/index.ts#L278-L291

It takes about 12s to finish type check and 10s to finish ESLint in my project. If we can type check and lint simultaneously, we might be able to reduce the build time.


### Describe the solution you'd like

Currently, Next.js already runs ESLint in a separate worker:
https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/b122178ead5f6a50e0ea543e1e806de93bde23af/packages/next/lib/verifyAndLint.ts#L20-L23

However, the typescript check is still run in the main thread:
https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/b122178ead5f6a50e0ea543e1e806de93bde23af/packages/next/lib/verifyTypeScriptSetup.ts#L77-L82

### Describe alternatives you've considered

N/A

title: Global paths redirect based on custom header
body:
Hello here!

I'd like to redirect our users (from our mobile app) to a prefixed path `/:path*` => `/webview/:path*` for every pages of our website when requested with a custom header.

I tried to perform a redirect in the next config as following:
```js
{
  source: '/((?!webview).*)', // every path which is not starting with "/webview"
  has: [
    {
      type: 'header',
      key: 'x-app-webview', // request should contain "x-app-webview:1"
      value: '1',
    },
  ],
  permanent: false,
  destination: '/webview/(.*)', // every path should be prefixed like "/webview/*"
},
```

I'm using Next js Internationalized Routing:
https://nextjs.org/docs/advanced-features/i18n-routing

Spanish:
https://history-maps.com/es/story/Grand-Principality-of-Serbia

Arabic:
https://history-maps.com/ar/story/Grand-Principality-of-Serbia

I have a language selector on the header, but if I'm not at the home url, every time I switch to another languages, it navigates back to the home url. Is there a way with useRouter I can stay on the same page but just switch the locale ?
```js
export const LanguageSelector = () => {
  const router = useRouter()
  const handleChange = (e) => {
    router.push("/", "/", { locale: e.target.value })

}

### Describe the Bug

Error: Must use import to load ES Module: /Users/flcwl/code/hiui-docs/node_modules/@babel/runtime/helpers/esm/typeof.js
require() of ES modules is not supported.
require() of /Users/flcwl/code/hiui-docs/node_modules/@babel/runtime/helpers/esm/typeof.js from /Users/flcwl/code/hiui-docs/node_modules/@hi-ui/picker/lib/cjs/utils/type-assertion/lib/esm/index.js is an ES module file as it is a .js file whose nearest parent package.json contains "type": "module" which defines all .js files in that package scope as ES modules.
Instead rename typeof.js to end in .cjs, change the requiring code to use import(), or remove "type": "module" from /Users/flcwl/code/hiui-docs/node_modules/@babel/runtime/helpers/esm/package.json.

### Expected Behavior

It's ok when using `@babel/runtime`

### To Reproduce

1. using `@babel/runtime`
```
import '@babel/runtime/helpers/esm/typeof';
```

2. run `next dev`

title: Catch all route takes precedence on reload while dynamic route takes precedence on routing
body:
So I have a catch all route and a dynamic route, let's say

pages
--category
----[name].js
--[...parameters].js

The dynamic route, `[name]` in the `category` folder has `getStaticPaths` based on which it generates `/category/A`, `/category/B`, `/category/C` etc. `getStaticProps` returns some data, and if `context.params.name === 'blabla'` returns also `notFound: true`

The catch all route, `[...parameters]` is able to handle `/name/blabla` path and also its `getStaticPaths` returns it.

When I build the app, the build goes fine, `/category/A`, `/category/B` and `/category/blabla` are all generated.

**The issue:**

If I'm loading/reloading the app with the `/category/blabla` route -> all is fine, and I can see that pages/[...parameters], the catch all route is handling this.

If I'm loading/reloading the app with the `/category/A` route -> all is fine, however if I click on a link to `/category/blabla` and am routed to `/category/blabla`, I can see that now `category/[name]` is handling this. In my case my app breaks because `category/[name]` does not know how to handle  `/category/blabla` route. If I reload while on `/category/blabla`, all is fine because it's once again handled by `pages/[...parameters]`, the catch all route.

**So to make it short**
`/category/blabla` => catch all route is handling this
`/category/A` -> `/category/blabla` => dynamic route is handling this

So does anyone know if there is anything that I can do about this? I want `/category/blabla` to always be handled by the catch all route.

Also yes I am aware of the precedence, however the behaviour related to this is obviously inconsistent.

title: what is the best way to use a react component that makes xhr requests in multiple pages?
body:
Hi All,

I just started with Next.js 12.x couple of weeks back and would like to get some input on how to best implement below scenario.

In my project, I have a react component. Let's say CompZ.  It has a prop 'searchText' of type String. Inside this component,
it makes multiple XHR calls in parallel. Calls looks like below where PARAM_1, PARAM_2 and PARAM_3 are constants (do not change) and {searchTex} is the only dynamic parameter.
 xhr1 = <<endpoint>>?query={searchText}&param=PARAM_1
xhr2 = <<endpoint>>?query={searchText}&param=PARAM_2
xhr3 = <<endpoint>>?query={searchText}&param=PARAM_3

Also, we have PageA, PageB, PageC

When CompZ is used in PageA, {searchText} will always be  'pageA'. 
When CompZ is used in PageB, {searchText} will always be  'pageB'. 
However, when CompZ is used in PageC, {searchText} can change based on user input.

I would like to know 
a) if it is possible to use getStaticProps in PageA and PageB so that these pages can be build during build time because parameters to the API are known ahead of time?

b) if this API provides server-side pagination feature, can we use getStaticProps anyway?

Any help is greatly appreciated.  Thanks.

title: Rewrites silently modify query string when "=" is missing
body:
### Verify canary release

- [X] I verified that the issue exists in Next.js canary release

### Provide environment information

    Operating System:
      Platform: linux
      Arch: x64
      Version: #28-Ubuntu SMP Thu Apr 14 04:55:28 UTC 2022
    Binaries:
      Node: 16.15.0
      npm: 8.5.5
      Yarn: 1.22.10
      pnpm: N/A
    Relevant packages:
      next: 12.1.7-canary.8
      react: 17.0.2
      react-dom: 17.0.2

### What browser are you using? (if relevant)

N/A

### How are you deploying your application? (if relevant)

I haven't deployed it yet, currently using the dev server

### Describe the Bug

When a request with query string params that are have only a key (no value or "=") is made to a route which matches an external rewrite Next.js will modify that query string as part of the rewrite to include "=" for each param. For example the query string in "/path?foo=bar&cake&tree" becomes "?foo=bar&cake=&tree=".

I don't know whether sending query string params without a "=" is technically valid but if a client has sent such a request to Next.js I don't think Next.js should silently try to be helpful and modify it to be "valid" since doing so changes the request and that can break things. For example in my use case I'm trying to proxy a request to a third-party api that expects params without "=" and will return an error it has one.

### Expected Behavior

I would expect Next.js to not modify the query string and to pass it on as is (or at the very least for this behavior to be documented).

### To Reproduce

1. Create a new request bin https://requestbin.com/ (If you click the "create a public bin" you don't need to create an account or anything).
2. Create a rewrite in next.config.js with some path as the source and the url of the request bin you created as the destination. E.g.
```javascript
{
    source: '/rewrite-test',
    destination: 'https://en93icipchvct.x.pipedream.net',
}
```
3. Make a request to that route with a query string that includes params that don't have a "=". E.g http://localhost:8000/rewrite-test?foo=bar&cake&tree
4. Observe that the query string is modified in the request send to the request bin.
![Screenshot from 2022-05-19 13-54-38](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/346340/169206345-0088982b-3400-47ab-a872-75c4163b5bf7.png)

author: rajesh40

title: Global Static Data
body:
I've wrestled with this topic a few times and am keen to see if the community can provide any insights or potential solutions. Often in projects I like to use some type of `` component, usually encompassing the `
` and `
`, that wraps the page content and then persists across route changes, helping to give that SPA feel and preventing re-rending the same components for every page. In Next, this means including it in the `_app.tsx` file like so: ```jsx /* Layout.tsx */ interface Props {} const Layout: React.FC> = ({ children }) => ( <>
{children}
) export default Layout ``` ```jsx /* _app.tsx */ const App = ({ Component, pageProps }: AppProps): JSX.Element => ( ) export default App ``` However, the issue arises when we need to get data into these components. On a recent project, we had a rather elaborate mega-menu contained within the header. The data for this was stored in a CMS but was pretty much static _(potentially changing every 3-6 months)_ so it didn't make sense to re-fetch it for every user. Here is where the problem lies... This data was essentially static, or static enough that it could be fetched at build time, so ideally we'd fetch it once then and use the data to statically generate the HTML for the menu component. However, as you can only fetch static props at the _page_ level, there's currently not an efficient way to do this in Next _(that I'm aware of)_.
## Potential Solutions Here are some options I've considered in the past: - **Fetch the data in every pages GSP call:** This is less than ideal as we're then having to add the `
` to every page and not adding it in the _app.tsx file. - **Fetch the data in the App using getInitialProps:** I'm less keen on this as GIP will run on a per-user basis and I'd rather not have to continually hit the CMS's API to re-fetch the same data - **Storing the static JSON files in the bundle:** Also an option but again not ideal as it requires manually updating the data by hand if/when something changes Would love to know if anyone else has faced a similar issue and how they've handled it. Would also be great to hear if Next plans to add any features to address this like using `gSP` in the App component or component-level data fetching.
## Linked issues A few issues i've found discussing similar topics: - https://github.com/vercel/next.js/discussions/26346 - https://github.com/vercel/next.js/discussions/36806 - https://github.com/vercel/next.js/discussions/12595
author: nathnhughes

title: Reverse proxy with url rewrite and unwanted hydration
body:
Hi everyone! I don't really know how to ask this question, but I'm hoping through discussion I can articulate my problem better. I'll do my best for now:

Our organization uses Fastly to help us route traffic to various applications, one of them obviously being a NextJS app. We use the [Serverless Next](https://github.com/serverless-nextjs/serverless-next.js) component for deployment. 

Our app has pages like this  `/[market]/[country]/[language]/my-page.jsx` and we statically build various markets such as `market1`, `market2`, etc. 

The user facing website will access this page like:
- https://public-website-market1.com/ca/en/my-page

next.config.js has `assetPrefix` set to be the public domain  

Fastly rewrites the above requests to point to a backend like this:
- https://private-backend-app.com/market1/ca/en/my-page

We also have Fastly rewrites that map requests for `/_next/data/*` to the correct path of the backend. For example:

`/_next/data/xx_some_hash/ca/en/my-page.json` ==> `/_next/data/xx_some_hash/market1/ca/en/my-page.json`
```
set req.url = if(req.url ~ "(/_next/data/.+)?/(.*)", re.group.1 "/" var.market-context "/" re.group.2, req.url);
```

So here's the problem: 

- I load my-page and it pulls the statically rendered page from my backend app as I expect.
- However, I believe the `Router` on the client is detecting that my url path is `/ca/en/my-page` when really all it knows about is `/[market]/ca/en/my-page`. 
- And because of this difference it seems to want to "hydrate" and then make a fetch call to get the StaticProps via `/_next/data/.../ca/en/my-page.json`, which doesn't include `[market]` and hence why I have the Fastly rewrite. 
- After this happens, the Router seems to misbehave and renders my root `index` page.

I see a flash of the page I want and expect (the static page deployed to S3), but after some Client side render, I get the wrong page. 

My hunch is that NextJS is barfing because I'm using some external mechanism (Fastly) to do URL rewrites. 

Can anyone here explain to me what's going on? I don't really want Next Router to hydrate, prefetch, or client-side-render this page. Would love some insight / comments to help me work this out.

THANKS!

Notes:

- I can't use Next 12 middleware, which might be helpful, but Serverless Next currently doesn't support it. 
- I can't use next.config.js rewrites to map `/ca/en/my-page` to `/market1/ca/en/my-page` because, well - we don't know what the `market` is at this point.

### Describe the feature you'd like to request

With the framework I've been developing (sensiblestack.com) we are creating the page routes in a central package called UI so it can also be shared with expo apps (we use reactwithnative.com to make that possible)

We are using Nextjs by default now in our boilerplate generation, but it forces us to copy paste every screen and import it from the ui package, because nextjs requires you to have the path in the pages folder.

It would be great if we can either:
a) change the path of the pages folder to a path outside of the root nextjs project (from the package)
b) create our own routing so we can import our screens from a different package and create it from that

I'd like to contribute to this myself if anyone could guide me through it, or if it's an easy fix, please help me!

Kind regards,
Wijnand

### Describe the solution you'd like

It would be great if we can either:
a) change the path of the pages folder to a path outside of the root nextjs project (from the package)
b) create our own routing so we can import our screens from a different package and create it from that


### Describe alternatives you've considered

I think we're going to move to bare React in our default boilerplate generation if this doesn't get solved.

body:
Version: next@12.1.6

__Summary__

- I am trying to use next/image's custom loader with cloudinary with `layout="fill"`.  
- I'm getting an error when one (or more) urls generated by the loader in the srcSet matches the src. 
- I'm not sure how to resolve the warning with my set up.

__Context__

I have my own transforms (in cloudinary). 
When a user uploads an image it will eagerly generate smaller images (amongst other things). 
I'm using using different params from the cloudinary loader, so I am using the custom loader.

__Example__
- the original image size which is the largest at 3440px.
- generated preset resizes e.g.: 234px, 750px,1000px

```
// next config
  images: {
    domains: ['localhost', 'res.cloudinary.com'],
    imageSizes: [234],
    deviceSizes: [600, 1000, 3440],
    path: 'https://res.cloudinary.com/<cloud-name>/',
    loader: 'custom',
  },
```

My loader spits out the srcSet which is as desired, where the 3440w image is the same as the src image.  

```
https://res.cloudinary.com/<name>/image/upload/w_234/v1/test/pic.jpg 234w, 
https://res.cloudinary.com/<name>/image/upload/w_750/v1/test/pic.jpg 600w, 
https://res.cloudinary.com/<name>/image/upload/w_1000/v1/test/pic.jpg 1000w, 
https://res.cloudinary.com/<name>/image/upload/v1/test/pic.jpg 3440w <-- same as src
```

next/image then gives me the warning because the 3440w url in the srcSet is the same as the src.

```
Image with src "${src}" has a "loader" property that does not implement width. Please implement it or use the "unoptimized" property instead.`
```

I don't want to use unoptimized as it removes the srcset.
Is it wrong to use a url which is the same as the src in the srcSet?  
Am I just going about this the wrong way?

title: RFC: add prop "linkTypes" to next/link to limit acceptable URLs
body: ### Objective I'd like to be able to pass scheme relative URLs to ``. ([More Info](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/issues/30947)) The current implementation merges the slashes for scheme relative URLs, so they become domain relative URLs instead. Issue is tracked here: https://github.com/vercel/next.js/issues/30947 I submitted a PR which minimally changes routing so that `//` would be considered the scheme if the URL started with this. However, that was unfortunately closed due to wariness that allowing it may result in unintentional open-redirects. ([Comment](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/pull/31119#pullrequestreview-980899657)) (It's worth noting, absolute URLs which are already allowed are also susceptible to open-redirects.) ### Proposal I propose an alternative solution to allowing scheme relative URLs, which I believe, would also further improve security if things like open-redirects are a concern. This way scheme relative URLs can be supported, while still preserving the security benefit of rejecting external URLs if desired. From what I know, there are 6 types of Links. ```ts /** A type of URL that an href can be. */ export enum LinkType { Absolute, SchemeRelative, DomainRelative, PageRelative, Query, Fragment, } ``` The idea is to add a prop to `next/link` where the developer can specify exactly what kind of links they're expecting or will allow there. By default, it uses the same link types that are supported now, which is all of them except scheme relative URLs. ```ts export function resolveHref( router: NextRouter, href: Url, resolveAs?: boolean, linkTypes: LinkType[] = [ LinkType.Absolute, LinkType.DomainRelative, LinkType.PageRelative, LinkType.Query, LinkType.Fragment, ] ) ``` A developer can pass the optional `linkTypes` prop if they want to limit or extend this. For example, to limit it to domain relative URLs only. This further protects from open-redirects as now scheme relative and absolute URLs can't be used here. ```ts import Link, { LinkType } from 'next/link' export default function Example() { return ( ); } ``` And in my use case, I could use something like (usually it should be domain relative, but we have some scheme relative URLs too): ```ts import Link, { LinkType } from 'next/link' export default function Example() { return ( ); } ``` I've got an implementation of this here, all that's left is choosing what to do when an unacceptable link is provided and updating tests: https://github.com/SethFalco/next.js/commit/c7d70d8790c8c0da98b62cd8f872f7ea02fc1f15 Can I please get opinions on this? --- If this is not deemed favorable, at the very least I'd like to at least propose that scheme relative URLs are rejected explicitly rather than being converted to domain relative URLs for duplicate slashes. (Which is unexpected behavior, and may bring up a discussion similar to this one again.) I'm completely fine with scheme relative URLs being rejected, but I'd like if it was documented behavior that the implementation enforces rather than an incidental thing from the repeated slash check. (Though I do think `next/link` should support all valid links, including scheme relative links.)

title: RFC: Layouts
body:
Update: https://github.com/vercel/next.js/discussions/37136#discussioncomment-3978551

---


This RFC outlines the biggest update to Next.js since it was introduced in 2016:

- **Nested Layouts:** Build complex applications with nested routes.
- **Designed for Server Components:** Optimized for subtree navigation.
- **Improved Data Fetching:** Fetch in layouts while avoiding waterfalls.
- **Using React 18 Features:** Streaming, Transitions, and Suspense.
- **Client and Server Routing:** Server-centric routing with _SPA-like_ behavior.
- **100% incrementally adoptable**: No breaking changes so you can adopt gradually.
- **Advanced Routing Conventions**: Offscreen stashing, instant transitions, and more.

[👉 Read the RFC](https://nextjs.org/blog/layouts-rfc) – we'd love to hear your feedback and comments.
author: timneutkens

title: Build mode with readable minified output
body:
### Describe the feature you'd like to request

We have a build flag we use for debugging React build output. `--pretty`

It applies all the usual minification algorithms but **leaves all the variable names intact** and runs the minifier output through Prettier. This makes it perfect to analyze what's in your bundle with casual scanning. It's also invaluable for profiling because you get full method names in the flamegraph but the behavior itself is 100% prod-like (so, no DEV branches).

I want the same but for Next.js app code.

### Describe the solution you'd like

A flag to `next build` that applies all the usual optimizations but (1) doesn't change variable names, (2) runs the output through Prettier. Note this is *not* the same as "don't minify the code". I want literally all minifier passes to run, *except* the variable renaming step. It should still inline stuff, reuse variables, DCE, etc, whatever the production behavior is.

### Describe alternatives you've considered

- Dev builds are not representative of what gets shipped and aren't helpful for debugging perf issues
- Profiling builds are good but lack the symbolic information
- Production builds also lack the symbolic information

So none of the existing options are great.
author: gaearon

title: Enable @typescript-eslint/recommended(-type-checked) in create-next-app --typescript
body:
### Describe the feature you'd like to request

Right now, Next.js projects created with `create-next-app --typescript` use the TSESLint parser for TypeScript projects -great!- but haven't enabled any of its [preset configurations](https://typescript-eslint.io/docs/linting/configs). Those configurations are recommended for most-to-all TypeScript projects by the TSESLint maintenance team _(including @armano2, @bradzacher, @JamesHenry, and myself; CCing)_. At the very least they'd resolve some of the issues mentioned in #27051 around detecting floating promises.

To verify the list of ESLint rules used in an app newly created with `create-next-app --typescript`, you can run:

```sh
npx eslint --print-config pages/index.tsx > rules.json
```

Is there a particular reason not to include the ~`@typescript-eslint/recommended` and `@typescript-eslint/recommended-requiring-type-checking`~ `@typescript-eslint/recommended-type-checked` _edit: updated for the v6 streamlining of the config_ configs for CNA users by default? 

A couple of notes on why that's likely more desirable now than in the past:

* The recommended configs should no longer contain opinionated rules that wouldn't be good in most projects
* ~If there are still overly chafe-y rules, we're gearing up for a 6.X release and would be happy to move those to the new [strict configuration](https://typescript-eslint.io/docs/linting/configs#strict)~ _Edit: we did this already_ 🙂 

Cheers!

### Describe the solution you'd like

1. Make a `next/typescript` lint config in `eslint-plugin-next` that extends from ~`@typescript-eslint/recommended` and `@typescript-eslint/recommended-requiring-type-checking`~ `@typescript-eslint/recommended-type-checked` _edit: updated for the v6 streamlining of the config_
2. In `create-next-app`, when `--typescript` is provided, change the `.eslintrc.json` file contents to:
    ```json
    {
      "extends": [
        "next/core-web-vitals",
        "next/typescript"
      ]
    }
    ```

Note that this was started in #28181 by @kyliau.

### Describe alternatives you've considered

@kyliau brought up a good point in #28181 that this adds a dependency on the [`@typescript-eslint/eslint-plugin`](https://npmjs.com/package/@typescript-eslint/eslint-plugin) package -which is separate from [`@typescript-eslint/parser`](https://npmjs.com/package/@typescript-eslint/parser)- for JS consumers. An alternative approach could be to have both of those be added to `package.json` and `.eslintrc.js` as described in https://typescript-eslint.io/docs/linting/ only for `--typescript` users.

_Edit July 11th, 2023: renamed from `recommended-requiring-type-checking` to `recommended-type-checked` per [Announcing typescript-eslint v6](https://typescript-eslint.io/blog/announcing-typescript-eslint-v6)_

title: Hot reload or any changes do not reflect when component is built into node_modules folder
body:
### Verify canary release

- [X] I verified that the issue exists in Next.js canary release

### Provide environment information

Operating System:
      Platform: win32
      Arch: x64
      Version: Windows 10 Pro
    Binaries:
      Node: 16.13.2
      npm: N/A
      Yarn: N/A
      pnpm: 7.1.2
    Relevant packages:
      next: 12.1.6
      react: 18.1.0
      react-dom: 18.1.0

### What browser are you using? (if relevant)

not relevant

### How are you deploying your application? (if relevant)

_No response_

### Describe the Bug

I am using bit.dev to develop the components for my application. The way it works is by compiling components into node_modules so that they can be imported in source file via the syntax: @company/scope.test. This seems to be very similar to how npm link works for importing libraries (which seems to have the same problem)

So any component which is built this way, when I make changes it is not reflected in the node all - Even though nextjs appears to detect the change as I see it recompile in the logs when I save. Even stopping and restarting the server does not reflect my change.
It appears I need to delete the .next folder and then restart the server and I can see my change.

This obviously is not very efficient for development, seems like a bug.

### Expected Behavior

Running application should immediately reflect changes to updated components bia bit.dev or npm link.

### To Reproduce

Reference a component in the node_modules folder, update it and hit save. See nextjs notices the change as this event fires (event - compiled client and server successfully in 113 ms (262 modules)), but change is not reflected in application until delete the .next folder and restarting the application.

title: Managed Node.js scripts
body:
As far as I know, if you want to create an ad-hoc Node.js script, you have to run it as an ordinary, CommonJS Node script unless you do more setup. See [with-sitemap](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/tree/canary/examples/with-sitemap) for an example of what I mean. 

This has some drawbacks:
- You don't get frictionless ESM support (need to use `.mjs`)
- More setup needed to write TypeScript
- Lose context with the rest of the project (e.g. you can't use your project's module path aliases)

**It would be cool if Next.js managed ad-hoc scripts for you such that we could effortlessly write ESM, TypeScript, and get the same developer experience as when writing application code.**

Here's my idea on how it could work from a developer's perspective. 

In `next.config.js` you could have:
```js
module.exports = {
  scriptsDir: './scripts' // `false` by default, which indicates there are no ad-hoc scripts
}
```
Since `scriptsDir` is `false` by default, that means this feature could be _completely opt-in_.

Here's a contrived example of an ad-hoc script someone might create. Notice we can use ESM, TypeScript, module aliases, and all the DX features we enjoy when writing application code.
```ts
// scripts/seed_db.ts
import db from '@lib/db' // module aliases, ESM syntax

async function seedDatabase(databaseUri?: string) { ... } // TypeScript

await seedDatabase(process.env.DATABASE_URI) // top-level await, environment variables
```

Then perhaps to run the script, you could do something like:
```bash
next script seed_db
```

Perhaps flags could control the behavior further:
```bash
next script seed_db --env=development --watch
```

I find myself often needing to create ad-hoc Node.js scripts when working on a Next.js project, and it's always a bit of a disappointment when I have to fall back to CommonJS and no TypeScript without extra setup. It'd be great if Next.js made doing this simpler. Does anyone else think this would be useful?

NextJs will generate the pages(which are not present in getstaticprops) when the request hits the next server.

I explained the scenario in the below screenshot.

![IMG20220526084155~3](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/4039657/170417852-ac700630-3a67-4772-9e07-3dd43524dc55.jpg)

For example, If I triggered the new build using next build, all my previously generated pages are gone. again when the user requests it will be generated again, now the user has to wait for some time until the page is generated.

If the already generated page during the previous build is kept, nextjs will not regenerate.

For example, my changes in my new build will affect only 2 out 100 pages, but the remaining 98 pages are the same as my previous build.

Is there any way to optimize it?



**Question No2:** 

 If nextjs will cache the pages for more time, will it make the server memory like database memory when all the pages are generated and cached (using ISR)

Thanks in advance for any clarifications. I have these doubts in my mind

author: bytrangle

title: NextJS Rewrites Exclude Query Param  Rules
body:
Hi,

I am having difficulties in in putting not condition in nextjs rewrite rule.
Here is the use cases.

1. URL /s or /s/{slug} should go to /s/:slug (This is achievable)
2. But if above URLs contains only one query param e.g. productId then it should go to to /product:/productId (/s/kitchen?productId=22213134)
3. But if above URLs contains any other query param other than productId then it should go to other destination like /category/:slug  (e.g /s/kithchen?page=5)
author: imranweb

title: Recommended way to rebuild live site
body:
Hello,

I have a Nextjs site and have made some changes to the code. I have it running with **forever**, so I need to rebuild the site, stop the process, and restart it, if I'm not mistaken. So I run something like

> npm run build && npm stop && npm start

(__npm stop__ stops the **forever** process, __npm start__ restarts it with **forever** and __next start__)

The problem is the moment I start __npm run build__ the public site breaks and it is not accessible again until __npm start__. Normally, that's just a few seconds, but also, if something goes wrong with the build, the site breaks until I find a fix.

Is there a way to prebuild the site without affecting the live website and then move the built contents to their folder?

Thank you.
author: JuanIrache

title: Allow excluding page extensions
author: awareness481

title: Getting 500 error response with blank page on Vercel with no logs, but works fine in dev locally
body:
Where to start digging?
The page in question is a file uploading form which gets processed on the server using [`formidable`](https://github.com/node-formidable/formidable), saves to the local temp folder, then another function reads said file and uploads it to the cloud storage. 
The process is a bit convoluted but mostly because `formidable` requires writing a ton of streaming boilerplate to get the `Buffer` from the form directly and I've read the lambdas do allow some form of read/write on their temporary fs and this whole process takes less than 10 seconds so it should work.
I assume by the nature of error Vercel blocks the request even before reaching the app hence the blank page instead of `/pages/500`, but how do I debug this?

title: Sharing logic between Components and getServerSideProps
body:
Hello,

I am new to React.js & Next.js, coming from an Angular background and trying to figure out how everything fits together in React.js.

My scenario is quite common so I wanted to get some input. I am using a third party sdk wrapped by a logic service that I need to use in context/provider, components/hooks and getServerSideProps. Looking for some patterns for code reuse.

Thanks
author: jbeckton

title: Add `locales` to `NextURL` for access in Middleware
body:
### Describe the feature you'd like to request

The `NextRequest` `nextUrl` provides other locale info (`locale`, `defaultLocale`, `domainLocale` - which does include `locales`), but is notably missing `locales`. This is useful information to have particularly in middleware which is handling rewrites and redirects.

### Describe the solution you'd like

Included the configured i18n locales under a `locales` prop on `NextURL` so that it is accessible on [`NextRequest`](https://nextjs.org/docs/api-reference/next/server#nextrequest) in middleware. This would be in line with [i18n config accessibility in other areas of the API](https://nextjs.org/docs/advanced-features/i18n-routing#accessing-the-locale-information).

### Describe alternatives you've considered

Duplicating configured locales in middleware code, or storing locales in common file.

title: Custom Font request not being made
body: Hello everyone! I am creating a [website ](https://visits.vercel.app/)using Next.js and Tailwind CSS. I want to use Inter as my main font and have imported it in the custom document file as mentioned [here](https://nextjs.org/docs/basic-features/font-optimization#usage). Next.js is not making the request for the custom font when I visit the homepage. The request for the font is made only once I open any of the [listings ](https://visits.vercel.app/visits/cl3ift76n0007xotw9hfc6h74) created using dynamic routes. Thanks in advance for any help!
author: alabhyajindal

title: Cache API calls at build time
body:
Seeking for tips regarding a NextJS project with multiple hundreds of pages. Each page is calling multiple API endpoint again and again (with the same data). For example, data to build the menu on each page.

Any ideas on how to properly cache an API call to be reused and shared across page builds?

Additionally, how would this work for SSR / ISR?

title: 【Feature Request】Server Function
body:
### Describe the feature you'd like to request

As React is working on server-side components for do some server-side work in the front-end project,I thinking of implementing isomorphic application in nextjs framework.In nextjs, we can write api route and call it in client code.But this method is not easy enough, especially when you have more and more apis,this will require significant maintenance costs.To make the project more structured, we usually put logically related files in the same folder on a page by page basis.So I want to implement a special file type called "Server Function", this js module export a function,when nextjs building, this function will automatically generate an API route.In client code,we can call this function directly, and SWC/Babel will convert it to calling API route.











### Describe the solution you'd like

Specify a module that defualt export a function, this function will automatically generate an API route.If client code call this function, SWC/Babel will convert it to calling API route.

### Describe alternatives you've considered

no

author: akhilpamwani

title: Optimized image cache on vercel
body:
By default, optimised images (if using the `next/image` component for example) are cached in the /_next/images folder. By default this cache is NOT flushed when you trigger a new deploy. Which is good, a project with probably thousands of images that are cached this way, you don't want to be optimising images on every build.

It does bring another problem though. I can't really get this from the documentation, but how long are those images cached? I've got an image that has changed on the source (different image, same url) but the cache remains the same.

Is it possible to clear the image cache (preferably for just one image)? Or any other way to instruct dat the image should be processed again?

title: Can't use next/link in a component library
body:

When I try to click on my Link component I get this error: `TypeError: null is not an object (evaluating 'router[replace ? 'replace' : 'push']')`.

Looks like the nextjs Link component can't handle routing when imported from an external library. 

Does anyone know how to solve this issue?

author: miko-code

title: Next.js Performance Questions/Discussion
body:
A recent project I've been working on has been suffering from a few performance issues, mostly related to Core Web Vitals/Lighthouse Audits. With this in mind and after doing a bunch of research myself, I was keen to come on here, share my findings and see if anyone has experienced anything similar and how they ultimately resolved it.
# The problem So, with this medium-sized Next app, the performance issues seem to be related to long [TBT](https://web.dev/tbt/) and [TTI](https://web.dev/tti/) times which are caused by long tasks hogging the main thread. This is an issue now more than ever as [TBT](https://web.dev/tbt/) is [the single most heavily weighted metric when calculating lighthouse scores](https://web.dev/performance-scoring/#lighthouse-8), accounting for 30% of the score. Seeing as Next.js prides itself on allowing users to build fast, scalable SPAs, I was a little surprised by this. A site like [https://jynx.vercel.app/](https://jynx.vercel.app/) for example _(which I also built with Next.js)_ gets an almost perfect lighthouse score...
# Research As I began to research this, I decided to take a look at the [Next.js showcase](https://nextjs.org/showcase) to see if any of the big players had run into similar issues and if so, how they handled it. As expected, a lot of the sites performed relatively well, however, there were a few that surprised me with the metrics they returned: Site | Overall | [FCP (s)](https://web.dev/fcp/) | [TTI (s)](https://web.dev/tti/) | [Speed Index (s)](https://web.dev/speed-index/) | [TBT (ms)](https://web.dev/tbt/) | [LCP (s)](https://web.dev/lcp/) | [CLS](https://web.dev/cls/) -- | -- | -- | -- | -- | -- | -- | -- [Invision _(Blog)_](https://www.invisionapp.com/inside-design/) | 34 | 0.8s | 5.5s | 3.6s | 1890ms | 3.9s | 0 [Invision _(Blog Post)_](https://www.invisionapp.com/inside-design/freehand-design-handoff-inspect/) | 36 | 0.9s | 6.9s | 3.6s | 2900ms | 3.0s | 0.043 [Deliveroo](https://deliveroo.co.uk/restaurants/london/the-city) | 47 | 1.3s | 3.3s | 3.4s | 480ms | 3.3s | 0 [Netflix _(Jobs Post)_](https://jobs.netflix.com/jobs/168470047) | 70 | 1.1s | 2.5s | 1.7s | 40ms | 3.0s | 0.263 [Hulu](https://www.hulu.com/hub/tv) | 39 | 1.1s | 8.2s | 4.6s | 3130ms | 2.2s | 0 [Nike _(Help)_](https://www.nike.com/help/a/returns-policy) | 38 | 0.8s | 2.8s | 2.3s | 850ms | 3.0s | 0.374 [IGN](https://www.ign.com/) | 58 | 0.5s | 4.1s | 1.1s | 680ms | 1.2s | 0.307 The big thing that’s impacting the overall score here is the [TBT](https://web.dev/tbt/), which in these cases was caused by long JavaScript execution tasks. Discovering this lead me to then profile the performance of the sites to see if there were any common themes. ### [Invision _(Blog)_](https://www.invisionapp.com/inside-design/) ![https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/97189204/169271020-990fe247-c523-4224-b08c-1e0849740a2c.png](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/97189204/169271020-990fe247-c523-4224-b08c-1e0849740a2c.png) ### [Invision _(Blog Post)_](https://www.invisionapp.com/inside-design/freehand-design-handoff-inspect/) ![https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/97189204/169271047-db3b4e0b-19d6-46e5-b1a8-f19c4c0dc084.png](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/97189204/169271047-db3b4e0b-19d6-46e5-b1a8-f19c4c0dc084.png) ### [Deliveroo](https://deliveroo.co.uk/restaurants/london/the-city) ![https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/97189204/169271069-1de6aef8-4b7e-44ea-af5b-9dc2ef1ac444.png](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/97189204/169271069-1de6aef8-4b7e-44ea-af5b-9dc2ef1ac444.png) ### [Netflix _(Job Post)_](https://jobs.netflix.com/jobs/168470047) ![https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/97189204/169271085-a119cd8e-3472-4caf-8421-28c66e2c1ad3.png](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/97189204/169271085-a119cd8e-3472-4caf-8421-28c66e2c1ad3.png) ### [Hulu](https://www.hulu.com/hub/tv) ![https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/97189204/169271097-ecf8bc95-dfd4-47c3-b6aa-0cc55eef2229.png](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/97189204/169271097-ecf8bc95-dfd4-47c3-b6aa-0cc55eef2229.png) ### [Nike _(Help)_](https://www.nike.com/help/a/returns-policy) ![https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/97189204/169271119-41f19932-7b4a-416e-8484-745325fd82a7.png](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/97189204/169271119-41f19932-7b4a-416e-8484-745325fd82a7.png) ### [IGN](https://www.ign.com/) ![https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/97189204/169271137-c86dfaf6-d0fb-4275-899c-155e6fefd1f9.png](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/97189204/169271137-c86dfaf6-d0fb-4275-899c-155e6fefd1f9.png) Looking at the above graphs, it appears the longer tasks seem to be run around the hydration period. This is perhaps a Next.js/React-specific issue *(happy to be enlightened on this)*, however, I've found myself wondering whether maybe there is another common cause.
# The potential root cause One thing to note here is that all the sites mentioned so far *(bar Deliveroo)* are using CSS-in-JS libraries like styled-components, emotion or styled-jsx. Site | Using CSS-in-JS? -- | -- Invision _(Blog)_ | styled-components Invision _(Blog Post)_ | styled-components Deliveroo | - Netflix _(Jobs Post)_ | emotion Hulu | emotion Nike _(Help)_ | emotion IGN | styled-jsx After some further investigation, my conclusion is that when using a large library of CSS-in-JS-based components or having a large number of DOM nodes that depend on CSS-in-JS—generated styles, the rehydration process can begin to impact on [TTI](https://web.dev/tti/)/[TBT](https://web.dev/tbt/), even when using SSR. This comes from the fact that a library like styled-components will generate `classNames` to be used with `html` on the server but then have to rehydrate the client and check for differences, etc. Doing this with 1500+ DOM nodes, for example, can obviously bump up the [total blocking time](https://web.dev/tbt/). [This issue](https://github.com/styled-components/styled-components/issues/3518) on the styled-components repo provides some useful additional insights into why this happens: ![Screenshot 2022-05-31 at 16 49 23](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/97189204/171215921-4199f376-1e90-40bf-b1e1-9ec9e69d4338.png) as does [this post](https://spectrum.chat/styled-components/general/performance-during-hydration~f1286390-9473-4539-81dd-f740ad41016f?m=MTYxNDI1MzE5MDI1NA==) on Spectrum: ![Screenshot 2022-05-31 at 16 50 35](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/97189204/171216145-b2c34d9a-4bc5-45e4-b77c-7047b22edbe3.png)
# Solutions I've already looked into: ### Compile-time CSS-in-JS libs There are a few out there that could potentially be the answer _([Stiches](https://stitches.dev/) and [Linaria](https://linaria.dev/) spring to mind)_, however not completely sure this would have the desired effect and whether it would allow for any level of dynamic-ness? ### Lazy Hydration > _"Lazy Hydration is a form of Partial Hydration where you can trigger hydration at a later point and not immediately after loading the site. A good example is components outside of the viewport. You don’t need to hydrate them instantly, but you can delay hydration until the component is visible."_ - [Markus Oberlehner](https://markus.oberlehner.net/blog/partial-hydration-concepts-lazy-and-active/) (<-- worth a read) I ran some tests for this using the library [react-lazy-hydration](https://github.com/hadeeb/react-lazy-hydration) where offscreen components weren't hydrated until they were scrolled into view. This actually worked surprisingly well, however, there are fair few bugs with the library and it doesn't appear to be actively maintained anymore 😢 ### Plain Old CSS _(Scss)_ Out of curiosity I also ran a few tests comparing the use of styled-components to Scss modules. I created two visually identical versions of a card component, one constructed with styled-components and the other using JSX and a Scss module. Then I placed them on individual pages and benchmarked them. The results are as follows: #### styled-components ![sc-version--perf](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/97189204/169274015-eb17f9bc-d03e-4be1-80a2-2df37c04f970.png) #### Scss ![css-version--perf](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/97189204/169274063-14fe953f-c521-4c2f-bb56-fd14a29a4faf.png) Both pages were profiled using **Fast 3G** data throttling and **4x slowdown** CPU throttling. Using the [TBT](https://web.dev/tbt/) metric in the bottom-left corner, we can see that the styled-components version took 596.72ms vs the Scss version taking 149.91ms _(4x less!)_. I'm not sure how these metrics scale and whether there's a certain amount of initial overhead whether you're using 1 styled component or 100, but it's certainly eye-opening. Of course, using CSS/Scss over CSS-in-JS ultimately means less JavaScript is shipped to the browser, however, I'd be keen to understand how much of an enhancement switching the entire app to CSS would have, as it would require a decent amount of work. ### Some other potentially interesting ideas that are currently in development: - Next.js is working with React to implement 'Selective Hydration': https://github.com/vercel/next.js/discussions/28445 - React 18 could yield some improvements with concurrent mode: https://github.com/reactwg/react-18/discussions/37 - Server Components should open the door for plenty of server-side optimizations and reduce the app's overall bundle size, once they're released stably: https://reactjs.org/blog/2022/03/29/react-v18.html#server-components-is-still-in-development
# My question Taking all of the above into consideration, my question, I suppose, is whether anyone here has any personal experience with this or knows of any potential solutions? Essentially, I think it boils down to just shipping less JavaScript. CSS-in-JS is a fantastic tool for DX, but this has me wondering whether the trade-offs are worth it as it kind of defeats the point of using SSG/SSR if you still need to run a *(sometimes costly)* rehydration for every DOM node in the client.

title: Including private data folder in build output
body:
I'm building a small personal site, and rather than messing with database management, I'm using a folder with static files in it.

Then I have an API route which accesses those files during runtime.

Running on my development machine, this works just great.  When I build on Vercel, however, the folder is not included.

I've tried a number of approaches to make this data available, but haven't found anything that works.

At one point, I was able to include the content as part of the "public" folder, but that has two problems for me:
1 - the files are publicly exposed
2 - I don't have a way to search them

I fully admit I'm way out of my depth when it comes to webpack.

Is it possible to include a folder in the build output that isn't publicly exposed, but is visible to server functions?

body:
We have a Next.js-application running behind Nginx hosted on a public server with a domain name. Because we're not ready for production yet we've set up HTTP Basic Auth on nginx. However, when visiting the website two HTTP Basic Auth  dialogs appear requiring us to login to each one. 

Anybody experienced something similar?
Could this be a problem in our next.js-application or a problem with our nginx-configuration?

(EDIT: This mainly seems to happen with Chrome and not Firefox)
author: j-mattsson

title: Page data not updating when using router.push("/route")
body:
### Verify canary release

- [X] I verified that the issue exists in Next.js canary release

### Provide environment information

Linux/Ubuntu 20.04

### What browser are you using? (if relevant)

_No response_

### How are you deploying your application? (if relevant)

_No response_

### Describe the Bug

As stated in the doc the getServerSideProps method gets called in these cases

- When you request this page directly, getServerSideProps runs at request time, and this page will be pre-rendered with the returned props
- When you request this page on client-side page transitions through [next/link](https://nextjs.org/docs/api-reference/next/link) or [next/router](https://nextjs.org/docs/api-reference/next/router), Next.js sends an API request to the server, which runs getServerSideProps.

But in my nextjs app it's not working that way. I have a form page to create new data after what I router.push to the data list page. The actual issue is that the data list is not showing the new entry. Why is that ? I even tried using router.replace(router.asPath) in the component I'm routing to but same thing. I'm wondering if the fact that I'm using next-pwa is the reason to this. Maybe the last request is cached or some stuff like that. Just wanna understand 

### Expected Behavior

GetServerSideProps should be called when I'm on the data list page and new added entry should show up automatically

### To Reproduce

I will soon provide a way to reproduce this issue.

title: Make <Image /> tags work with unstable_runtimeJS
body:
### Describe the feature you'd like to request

Currently the <Image /> tag does not work (shows nothing) if `unstable_runtimeJS: true` is used on a given page. But the tag already has a fallback for when JS is disabled in the browser, and I was wondering if it would be possible to use this fallback when the page has `unstable_runtimeJS: true`.

My use case is a simple blog which does not need any javascript runtime but the image optimizations would be a nice to have.

### Describe the solution you'd like

Ideally the solution would not require any code changes for the user.

### Describe alternatives you've considered

The only alternative I see at the moment is not using `next/image` with `unstable_runtimeJS: true`.

- Project flattening
- Conditional API behavior for dev vs. prod

title: Preload images
body:
Lazy loading images is nice. But there is one major issue with this method.
No matter how good they are optimized and how fast internet connection is, there is bound to be a delay before image appears. And the way they appear isnt exactly pleasant. On slower connection images may pop up when they are already half way above top viewport border without user even knowing.
One of solutions people suggest every time for this question is to use eager loading or priority. It may look like a solution, but in reality it is not. In order for it to work it have to be applied to each and every meaningful big image on page. It in turn starts loading all big images at the same time at initial page load, how many of them there are. Which essentially ruins all effort for quickest delivery of page.

What I think could be a solution is to still use lazy load, but load images ahead of time, at certain range from current scroll position. This way image will already be loaded when user scrolls to its first pixel. But how can it be done? Are there native methods to do so? Can it be done by fiddling with wrapper component?

title: Increasing size of .next directory
body:
Hi,

I have a problem with constant memory growth of the **.next** folder.
We have an application running on docker.
We are using Incremental Static Regeneration and Server-Side Rendering.

Version of next
```
"next": "^11.1.0"
```

docker-compose.yml
```yml
version: '2.4'
services:
  # Frontend prod
  node-prod:
    build: 
      context: ../www
      dockerfile: ../.docker/node/Dockerfile
      args:
        - ENV_FILE=prod.env
    container_name: test-prod
    environment:
      - NODE_OPTIONS="--max-old-space-size=1536"
    expose:
      - "3000"
    restart: always
    cpus: 1
    mem_limit: 1536m
    networks:
      - test
networks:
  test:
    external: true
```

DockerFile
```DockerFile
ARG NODE_ENV=production

FROM node:16-slim AS base

ENV NODE_ENV $NODE_ENV

ARG PORT=3000
ENV PORT $PORT
EXPOSE $PORT

RUN npm i npm@latest -g

RUN mkdir -p /opt/node_app
WORKDIR /opt/node_app

RUN mkdir -p /opt/node_app/app/.next

RUN mkdir -p /opt/node_app/_logs

COPY ./package.json ./package-lock.json* ./.npmrc ./

RUN npm ci --legacy-peer-deps && npm cache clean --force
ENV PATH /opt/node_app/node_modules/.bin:$PATH

WORKDIR /opt/node_app/app
COPY . .

ARG ENV_FILE=production.env
COPY ./$ENV_FILE .env.local

# Development
FROM base AS next-app-development
CMD npm run dev

# Production
FROM base AS next-app-production
RUN npm run build
CMD npm start

FROM next-app-$NODE_ENV AS next-app-final
```

Bellow you can see the logs of memory increasing of the **.next** folder.
It is during midnight and the website is not being used as much as during the day.
```
Fr 3. Jun 00:00:01 CEST 2022
588M	/opt/node_app/app/.next
Fr 3. Jun 00:30:01 CEST 2022
610M	/opt/node_app/app/.next
Fr 3. Jun 01:00:01 CEST 2022
631M	/opt/node_app/app/.next
Fr 3. Jun 01:30:01 CEST 2022
655M	/opt/node_app/app/.next
```

title: Configure custom Image loader globally / with context
body:
### Describe the feature you'd like to request

I would like to be able to use a custom `loader` for `Image` without passing `loader` to each usage of `Image`.

https://nextjs.org/docs/api-reference/next/image#built-in-loaders

The docs say I can put `loader: 'custom'` in `next.config.js`, but then I'd have to pass every `Image` the loader prop.

### Describe the solution you'd like

A possible solution would be exposing the `ImageConfigContext`, or a new `Context` that could be merged with it.

### Describe alternatives you've considered

I'm considering writing my own wrapper around `Image` that sets the loader prop. This is not as clean a solution because it doesn't work across packages using `next/image` without them each implementing this solution.

title: Run Plugins for SWC when running Jest
body:
### Describe the feature you'd like to request

Hi 👋

I'm using [vanilla-extract ](https://vanilla-extract.style/) a typescript based css pre-processor with `next` and it works great when running and developing my application. However, when trying to run `jest` on my UI tests it chokes on `vanilla-extract` and throws errors because I believe it doesn't know where the generated CSS is. In my `next.config.js` I use the [vanilla-extract plugin](https://vanilla-extract.style/documentation/setup/#nextjs) and next works flawlessly with this. 

### Describe the solution you'd like

I would like jest to be able to work successfully with `vanilla-extract`. I believe at the moment it is failing because it might not be using the [vanilla-extract plugin](https://vanilla-extract.style/documentation/setup/#nextjs) so it fails when running tests. Is it possible to make jest aware of the plugin? If so I think that may be the best solution.

### Describe alternatives you've considered

The main alternative that i've considered is enabling the use of babel/webpack with only jest when running tests, but i'm not exactly sure how that would look like or if there is prior art in next land for this. My understanding is that it's all `swc` or all `babel + webpack` but not both, and that makes sense in general.

In the worst case we'd have to opt out of using SWC unfortunately 😞

title: Getting 413 response from getStaticProps Vercel serverless function with 750kb response payload
body:
Hi - I'm pretty stuck on this one...I've got a number of SSG pages (that use ISR to regenerate) that are returning a 413 error in Vercel from the serverless function that backs `getStaticProps`:

`LAMBDA_RUNTIME Failed to post handler success response. Http response code: 413.`

I've got a log inside that function with the size of the payload that's called right before I return it and I know that the pages in question are between 750-780kb, but I'm still getting this 413 error.

This only happens when deployed to production in Vercel and only when you navigate directly to the page in the browser, not when you do client-side routing from other pages to an affected page.

I'm pretty baffled as I'm not even anywhere near the 5MB limit (not even 20% of it), but I'm getting this? The [docs](https://vercel.com/docs/concepts/limits/overview#serverless-function-payload-size-limit) say this is for request body or response body - I've validated that it's not the response body, how can I check the request body? I don't control this function - it's generated automatically for me and all my `getStaticPaths` returns for each path are the 3 strings needed to build the dynamic path, so we're talking nothing at all on the way in either.

The closest I've found to this issue on here is someone who had 4MB payload and the marked answer referred to the docs saying the limit is 5MB, which makes no sense as the OP was at or under 4MB. In my case, I'm way under. I can't find the link to that item now unfortunately...

If I reduce the payload down to something small like 10kb by removing some things (that I actually need still - just for debugging purposes), the error goes away - so it feels like the limit isn't actually 5MB in reality if 750kb triggers this error?

Any ideas how I can get to the bottom of what's going on? Is there some other payload added in that I can’t control?

Thanks!

Next.js 12.1.6.

title: Standlone output + Dynamic Routes results in 404 of /index
body:
### Verify canary release

- [X] I verified that the issue exists in Next.js canary release

### Provide environment information

    Operating System:
      Platform: win32
      Arch: x64
      Version: Windows 10 Home China
    Binaries:
      Node: 16.15.0
      npm: 8.5.5
      Yarn: N/A
      pnpm: N/A
    Relevant packages:
      next: 12.1.7-canary.29
      react: 18.1.0
      react-dom: 18.1.0



### What browser are you using? (if relevant)

Chrome 102.0.5005.63

### How are you deploying your application? (if relevant)

_No response_

### Describe the Bug

I use Dynamic Routes to generate pages for the docs. In the dev mode it works fine and the `index.md` will be generated as page at path `/index`. The `start` command works as well. 

The problem is when I build with `outputStandalone` enabled, the `/index` shows `404` when I started the service with `node .next/standalone/server.js`. 

here is my `next.config.js`.
```
/** @type {import('next').NextConfig} */
const nextConfig = {
  experimental: {
    outputStandalone: true,
  },
  reactStrictMode: true,
}

module.exports = nextConfig
```

The minimal reproducible example was uploaded here: https://github.com/wangpin34/next-standalone



### Expected Behavior

The `/index` page is loaded successfully. 

### To Reproduce

Download the minimal reproducible example from https://github.com/wangpin34/next-standalone.  

Install and build
```
npm install
npm run build
```

Then start the server
```
node .next/standalone/server.js
```

Finally open http://localhost:3000/index on your browser.
author: wangpin34

title: How to reduce page payload for shared data
body:
I have a site generated with SSG (plus with ISR) and for a few of the routes there is common data to all of them.

E.g. for the following 2 routes... (contrived example using same route structure)

`/widgets/[foo]/[bar]`
`/widgets/[foo]/[bar]/[widget]`

...they both contain a left nav with a hierarchical tree of all the widgets. For each unique combination of the `foo` and `bar` param the tree is the same. With the addition of the `widget` path param too, there may be up to hundreds of pages all under the same `foo` / `bar` combination.

In the current approach I use `getStaticProps` to return the tree data and in some cases it can be up to 780kb (see related issue https://github.com/vercel/next.js/discussions/37466).

So that means if I am on one of the widget pages, then the prefetching of links in the tree will need to get up to 780kb per link, 95%+ of which is the same for every link (the tree data).

What's the right approach for loading this data _**once**_ per `foo` / `bar` combo and having that data cached so that the page data doesn't need to return this in `getStaticProps`?

Or do I need to look to fetch this tree data in the actual page itself from an API that caches it? Should I consider using an API route with caching headers to achieve this?

Would welcome any ideas / insights on this one thanks.

author: Musho-T

title: The fron-end doesn't render properly
body:
We have two different Ubuntu 20.04LTS machines with nodejs version 16.15.1 yarn version 1.22.19 and npm version 8.11.0. We have a codebase that is on the same git commit (same package.json/next.config.js/yarn.lock). When we run yarn local -> next build on these two machines, the +next/static/chunks have differences in them, which then result in one of the machines rendering incorrect css output to the browser. 

When we started to debug the issues we saw in the building the front-end by using the command “yarn install --frozen-lockfile && yarn local”. The logs of this command show workers spanning, as the machine that has the correct front-end render shows 3 workers but the machine that has the alignment issues shows 1 worker is spanning. On further investigation we found out that the build folder that contains the front-end files has some difference, the files that are built by nextjs come under  ” _next/static/chunks” have some difference in it. When we replace the js files under _next/static/chunks from the front end machine that renders correctly to the other machine and observe that the front-end is fixed. The version of the nexttjs is 9.1.1 that is the same on both machines, still somehow the front-end is not rendering correctly.   

Perhaps someone knows what we are missing? Or what yarn/nextjs are picking up that is not in the git code.

title: Is there a way to import a scss map into javascript?
body:

./styles/variables.module.scss
SassError: (small: 0, medium: 640px, large: 1024px, xlarge: 1200px, xxlarge: 1440px) isn't a valid CSS value.

4 │     breakpoints: $breakpoints;

```

I'm clear on why it's erroring out, but is there any way to work around this while still using a map? I know I could break the breakpoint values into separate variables, but that makes things a bit clunkier to use on the javascript side, as I'd have to do something like

```
import {breakpointSmall, breakpointMedium, breakpointLarge, breakpointXLarge, breakpointXXLarge} from "./variables.module.scss";
```
instead of the much more concise
```
import {breakpoints} from "./variables.module.scss";
```
author: jaandrews

title: Docs: Better explain risks and strategies for including large local images.
body: ### What is the improvement or update you wish to see? https://image-component.nextjs.gallery/ is a nice start to image docs, but there's a lot more that needs to be covered to reduce the steep learning curve required to not shoot yourself in the foot, which is easy with the `` component. A few questions that I have, that docs could help with: * How do you recommend handling large images? Should I be doing some downsizing/optimizing before putting them in `/public`, or can I trust NextJS? right now, using this component is actually quite worrisome for me. * Code examples for an image galleries of different styles (masonry, percent-based widths, using ``, other `layout` and `objectFit` usages) * Code examples of using `sizes` with explanations based on the images usages. Also demonstrate the risk of *not* using it. Possibly a warning banner, since in my case, it seems *required* to have reasonable asset-loading times * Code examples for usages, ie: `position: 'relative'` seems like a commonly asked stackoverflow question, as it's not an intuitive requirement * Can I define an image based on a single-dimension? ie: not more than 300px wide? (whenever i try this, the image usually gets stretched), I'd rather do that as my default, rather than relying on me getting the `sizes` string right ### Is there any context that might help us understand? I find the `` to be a steep learning curve. For example, I've not used `srcset` or `sizes` before. Even debugging them is a bit of a challenge: * in order to test what users will actually see, needing to either disable cache/use an incognito window. hard refresh doesn't work * inspecting the `properties` tab of the style panel to see what the `currentSrc` is * guessing at what my browser is actually try to load when i tell is `1000px`, based on my device's pixel density For my designs, I'm using a lot of ``, filling a bootstrap column-based layout (percentages within a fixed or fluid-width div). I've included images in my `public` directory that are large 1-2mb jpegs. However, what I'm finding is that by default, the browser is typically loading 3840px or 2048px-wide images, when the rendered size is at most 360x360 in my usage. There is some free image performance, as this will cut them down to ~300kb-800kb, but for an image gallery of 30 images, ~18mb is pretty significant for out of the box behavior. Also, even though the [docs say that "Next.js supports IE11 and all modern browsers (Edge, Firefox, Chrome, Safari, Opera, et al) with no required configuration"](https://nextjs.org/docs/basic-features/supported-browsers-features), `srcset` and `sizes` [only started being supported in in Chrome in late 2014](https://caniuse.com/srcset). So if a browser doesn't support them, the user is going to get the 3048px images. I'm actually considering going back to using `` and doing pre-processing myself (generating thumbnails per size, the old-school way) just because I'm not confident in my usage of this component that promises so much but is quite dangerous in practice. ### Does the docs page already exist? Please link to it. https://nextjs.org/docs/api-reference/next/image

author: MathanBE

title: Not compatible with Swiper Lazy Load
body: ### Verify canary release - [X] I verified that the issue exists in Next.js canary release ### Provide environment information Not able to run this... It says the folder info doesn't exist. Seems the wrong command. ### What browser are you using? (if relevant) _No response_ ### How are you deploying your application? (if relevant) _No response_ ### Describe the Bug I've a slide of images using Swiper.js library and next/previous images just loads when they kick in, when the slide goes to the next/previous it should be already loaded. So, basically, when I try to use lazy load from swiper, so it can load the next and previous image from a slide before it slides, it doesn't work with next image. Would be great if we could add swiper requirements: - Each lazy loaded image/element should have additional "swiper-lazy" class (Possible) - Lazy image source for element should be specified in "data-src" attribute instead of "src" (Not possible) - Lazy image source set for element should be specified in "data-srcset" attribute instead of "srcset" (Not possible) - Lazy background image source should be specified in "data-background" attribute (Not possible) To bypass this issue, I added loading = eager to next image, so it can load right away. ### Expected Behavior I was expecting a way to add data-src, data-srcset and data-background ### To Reproduce Just install Swiper.js, create a slide of images with next image component with lazy load enabled on next image and even with swiper lazy load enabled to get next/prev images: ``` lazy={{ enabled: true, loadPrevNext: true, loadPrevNextAmount: 1, loadOnTransitionStart: true, }} ```

title: require() returns a Promise?!?
body:
I have code where a call to `require()` returns a Promise. WTF? require calls are supposed to be synchronous, not return promises! This not only happens in client code, but also in getStaticProps on the server. Even when the path is completely static and known and resolvable at build time. When I replace the `require()` call with an import declaration it works.

The returned value has this shape:

```
Promise {
  Object [Module] {
    countryDimension: [Getter],
    dimensions: [Getter],
    fieldKeys: [Getter],
    fields: [Getter],
    source: [Getter]
  },
  [Symbol(async_id_symbol)]: 570009,
  [Symbol(trigger_async_id_symbol)]: 569994,
  [Symbol(destroyed)]: { destroyed: false },
  [Symbol(webpack exports)]: Object [Module] {
    countryDimension: [Getter],
    dimensions: [Getter],
    fieldKeys: [Getter],
    fields: [Getter],
    source: [Getter]
  },
  [Symbol(webpack then)]: [Function (anonymous)]
}
```

The stuff is there, correct, but wrapped in a promise.

Minimal project to reproduce issue, see this comment: https://github.com/vercel/next.js/discussions/37520#discussioncomment-2900296

As of now all my CSS is rendering serverside on 1st load and working absolutely fine.
But I am wondering is there a way to render only device-specific CSS on serverside.
For example, I don't want mobile-specific CSS if a user is viewing on a desktop.
As of now a lot of CSS is being sent to the client's browser as it includes a lot of media queries.
If I can render device-specific CSS then I don't need to send media queries to the client's browser.

Thanks
Anjan

title: Optimizing webpack chunks for dynamic routes with 1000s of pages
body:
I have a route used to serve "generic pages" like `/[slug]`.

Next build though creates a chunk for the /[slug] route, so including the heavy dependencies for all the pages.

What are my options here to optimize the chunks built ? 
Can I somehow instruct webpack to generate some chunk **at build time** when the pages are fetched from the CMS and the list of components of the page becomes known ?
