      react-dom: 18.1.0
```

### What browser are you using? (if relevant)

_No response_

### How are you deploying your application? (if relevant)

_No response_

### Describe the Bug

The correct way to import from `helper.mts`, per https://github.com/microsoft/TypeScript/issues/16577, is
```ts
import {message} from "../components/helper.mjs";
```
However, Next.js only works if I import `helper.mts` instead. In my application I need to put an Express server in front of Next.js due to [another Next.js bug](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/discussions/23988), and the normal Node code won't work if I change the imports to `.mts`.

### Expected Behavior

Importing with `.mjs` extension should work.

### To Reproduce

https://github.com/ysulyma/next-mts-repro

author: RigottiG

title: [Proposal] New optional file naming convention
body:
Hi, on this occasion I would like to create a proposal with the intention of improving (even more) the Nextjs development experience.

### Optional file naming convention

![basic-example](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/10292582/172934024-c83102ba-29af-43b8-b3d6-9570fe213259.png)

The proposal is to **optionally** support a new file naming convention specifically for `layout` and `page` files.

The main rule is that the file must contain the name of its parent directory, separated by `_`, followed by its functional name (`layout` or `page`).

The final name would have the sanake case style.

### Examples:
- app/dashboard/dashboard_layout.tsx
- app/dashboard/dashboard_page.tsx

And because it would be optional, the root layout/page could ignore this new naming convention.
- app/layout.tsx
- app/page.tsx

### ...But why?
As I said before, the intention is simply to improve the development experience.

In medium to large projects it is common to end up with your code editor full of open files with the same name, so sometimes it can get confusing.

### Additional notes
Rust (lang) also faced this "problem"! => [Rust 2018 Path changes | No more mod.rs](https://doc.rust-lang.org/edition-guide/rust-2018/path-changes.html#no-more-modrs)

### Describe the solution you'd like

emit a "handler.js" or similar file that does _not_ listen but can be plugged into middleware for AWS lambda

Not running on lambda (but ie on fargate) would introduce too high costs for us, so If I can't make that work reliably that would be a complete showstopper for the framework for us. 

I am also open to use a Docker container on lambda, but this just shifts the problem slightly instead of solving it

author: LoveDesignUA

title: Issues using getDataFromTree in Next.js 12
body:
I am in the process of attempting to upgrade a large production application (~80k SLOC) from Next.js v7 to v12. Our application was built using Apollo Client which was a heavily encouraged approach at the time.
After upgrading we found that support for an API provided by Apollo Client that is critical to our SSR approach was basically removed by the Next.js core team in recent versions of Next.js. This API, getDataFromTree, is what allowed the original authors of our application to perform nested queries - which is critical to the functionality of hundreds or thousands of components throughout our application.
Having done some research on this issue it seems that we are not alone in being very frustrated and confused that the Next.js core team would remove this functionality and not provide a workable alternative for large, pre-existing production apps that were built around this approach. We can't simply re-factor all the components in our app on the whims of the core team, so we may be blocked from upgrading from a very old, insecure version of Next.js.
I understand that this is open source software so "YMMV", but this just seems like a blatant disregard for the users of your flagship software product. It seems like the Next.js core team has decided that they don't like some of the approaches/methods the Apollo team have taken, so they are just trying to cut off support for the patterns they don't like and users stuck in the middle are just disregarded. I understand that OSS communities are entitled to be somewhat opinionated in the direction they take but this just seems very excessive.

title: Signed Exchanges (SXGs) Support
body:
It would be great to see Next.js or Vercel integrate with signed exchanges.

Introduced by Google in 2020, [Signed Exchanges (SXGs)](https://web.dev/signed-exchanges/) allow developers to accomplish the following:

- Improve your website's performance by making cacheable resources available on Google’s Signed Exchanges
- Enable Chromium-based browsers to prefetch your website on Google's search results page and make your website faster
- Improve the Largest Contentful Paint (LCP) which is part of the Core Web Vitals and increase your SEO ranking

To get started, check out the [platform-specific SXG tooling](https://web.dev/signed-exchanges/#platform-specific-sxg-tooling), [general-purpose SXG tooling](https://web.dev/signed-exchanges/#general-purpose-sxg-tooling) and [SXG libraries](https://web.dev/signed-exchanges/#sxg-libraries).

title: Next.JS page cache
body:
### Describe the feature you'd like to request

The customized server is no longer used JS file for caching. For SSR websites, add the built-in caching function, which depends on the JS internal cache or redis
（不在使用自定义的server.js文件进行缓存，对于SSR网站添加内置的缓存功能，依赖于js内部缓存或者redis）

### Describe the solution you'd like

The customized server is no longer used JS file for caching. For SSR websites, add the built-in caching function, which depends on the JS internal cache or redis
（不在使用自定义的server.js文件进行缓存，对于SSR网站添加内置的缓存功能，依赖于js内部缓存或者redis）

### Describe alternatives you've considered

Use the built-in HTTP module of express or node as the routing system to verify or cache the path in the specified format. The plug-in uses LRU cache or redis
（使用express或者node内置的http模块作为路由系统，对指定格式的路径进行验证或缓存，插件使用lru-cache或者redis）

author: eAmin

title: Is HEAD supposed to return content-length ?
body:
Hello, we are using NextJS in production for our website and we noticed that each HEAD request is sending a content length in the HEAD request response.

It's a bit problematic for us as we need to cache those pages through our CDN.

CURL example on localhost : 

````
curl -v -X HEAD http://localhost:3000/account
Warning: Setting custom HTTP method to HEAD with -X/--request may not work the
Warning: way you want. Consider using -I/--head instead.
*   Trying 127.0.0.1:3000...
* Connected to localhost (127.0.0.1) port 3000 (#0)
> HEAD /account HTTP/1.1
> Host: localhost:3000
> User-Agent: curl/7.79.1
> Accept: */*
>
* Mark bundle as not supporting multiuse
< HTTP/1.1 200 OK
< Server: nginx/1.21.6
< Date: Tue, 14 Jun 2022 13:05:47 GMT
< Content-Type: text/html; charset=utf-8
< Content-Length: 2047
< Connection: keep-alive
< Cache-Control: no-store, must-revalidate
< ETag: "7ff-LsLbg2R68RG2rxVPIby9T0L46Mk"
< Vary: Accept-Encoding
<
* transfer closed with 2047 bytes remaining to read
* Closing connection 0
curl: (18) transfer closed with 2047 bytes remaining to read
````

We have checked RFC 9110 HTTP Semantics, which allowes this content-length but it seems to be better not having this header.


RFC 9110 HTTP Semantics:
> A server MAY send a Content-Length header field in a response to a HEAD request (Section 9.3.2); a server MUST NOT send Content-Length in such a response unless its field value equals the decimal number of octets that would have been sent in the content of a response if the same request had used the GET method.

Is that behavior normal or not ? Could we fix it or not ?

title: Ambiguous resolution of dynamic routes catch all with another same level dynamic route on export
body: ### Verify canary release - [X] I verified that the issue exists in Next.js canary release ### Provide environment information Operating System: Platform: darwin Arch: arm64 Version: Darwin Kernel Version 21.3.0: Wed Jan 5 21:37:58 PST 2022; root:xnu-8019.80.24~20/RELEASE_ARM64_T8101 Binaries: Node: 16.13.1 npm: 8.3.0 Yarn: 1.22.18 pnpm: 7.1.7 Relevant packages: next: 12.1.7-canary.38 react: 17.0.2 react-dom: 17.0.2 ### What browser are you using? (if relevant) Version 102.0.5005.115 (arm64) ### How are you deploying your application? (if relevant) next export ### Describe the Bug The previous image show my pages folder structure in which I have the following dynamics routes **(be carefull that i'm aware of the [dynamics routes' caveats](https://nextjs.org/docs/routing/dynamic-routes#caveats))** - [...catch-all] - [slug] - - [deep] I'm always sure that where is no overlapping with catch-all, slug or deep paths. ```sh Page Size First Load JS ┌ ○ / 2.7 kB 77.7 kB ├ └ css/d94d137a14864d9c.css 684 B ├ /_app 0 B 72.8 kB ├ ● /[...catch-all] 438 B 75.4 kB ├ ├ /One/one ├ ├ /two-two ├ └ /Three ├ ● /[slug] 412 B 75.4 kB ├ ├ /four ├ ├ /five ├ └ /six ├ ● /[slug]/[deep] 450 B 75.4 kB ├ ├ /four/four-four ├ ├ /five/five-five ├ └ /six/six-six ├ ○ /404 2.97 kB 75.7 kB └ λ /api/hello 0 B 72.8 kB + First Load JS shared by all 72.8 kB ├ chunks/framework-e70c6273bfe3f237.js 42 kB ├ chunks/main-2a48abc85e3b6a19.js 28.6 kB ├ chunks/pages/_app-50ea61a192ce43b2.js 555 B ├ chunks/webpack-74ba1aba2cfe5288.js 1.57 kB └ css/27d177a30947857b.css ``` All the pages I expect have been exported properly. But what happen is that when i try to go (through the next/link component or through the next/router functions) to the page /One/one this is going to render the [slug][deep] page component instead of the [...catch-all] page component. It's happing the same with "two-two" with is going to be rendered with the [slug] component page. **BUT. The catch is that when I try to reach the /One/one page through the url on the browser or through an tag with the href the page is going to render the "expected" [...catch-all] page component.** https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/12732872/173597525-57dda918-ddaa-4dbe-9dc1-29655587f79b.mov ### Expected Behavior I would expect the next/link or next/router components/functions to behave as like as the anchor tag with the href behave in order to prevent mismatched client side page render. ### To Reproduce I have created a repository with the minimal example in order to reproduce the bug, its's the same code which is shown in the screenshot and the video. https://github.com/aendel/nextjs-catch-all-proof In order to reproduce it: 1. Clone the repo: `git clone https://github.com/aendel/nextjs-catch-all-proof.git` 2. Install the dependencies: `npm install` 3. Build - Export - Serve static output: `npm run export:serve` 4. Follow the video at the end of the section "Describe the bug"

author: rohanreddy888

title: next/image calculated sizes attribute
body:
Hi, I'd like to get some input from the next.js / react community about the way we are using responsive images in our current projects. As a small background, we are currently using Vue.js / NuxtJS and looking into switching to next.js with our agency.
For that reason, we started a project using next.js and are getting familiar with it.

One part is the image handling. We are delighted with next/image because it's a well-designed, polished and feature rich component. But there is something we implemented in our custom vue component, we can't see anyone doing in the next.js world.

We calculate the sizes attribute dynamically based on the image and viewport width. We start with `sizes="1px"` and calculate the actual size on load:
```js
this.sizes = Math.ceil(image.getBoundingClientRect().width / window.innerWidth*100)+'vw'
```
We do the same on window resize. Additionally, our smallest image in the srcset is an inlined blurred svg which represents the actual image.

With that solution, we can get superfast images which start blurred, and then the closest image gets loaded without needing to specify the `sizes` attribute.

next/image has that blurred image feature, which works great. But we don't see an obvious, clean way to calculate and set the sizes attribute dynamically because we don't have access to the image element. However, the component seems so well put together and thought out and got us thinking we are doing something "wrong" 😄 
That said, we are using this solution for multiple years now and didn't encounter any problems so far.

I'm very excited to hear feedback from this community. Please tell me if I need to explain our solution better.

Just for reference, Imgix has something similar:
https://blog.imgix.com/2021/07/21/auto-sizes

title: force /en on default locale without adding a fake "default" locale
body:
I followed the [middleware example from the docs](https://nextjs.org/docs/advanced-features/i18n-routing#prefixing-the-default-locale), but I'm unsatisfied with the result. 

Adding this dummy default locale causes a lot of secondary config issues that makes things tricky to work through. In my case, we are using Storyblok as the CMS, and setting things up the is way means I have to have a copy of my /en homepage, and a few others, in a "default" top level folder, which isn't really used (or it is used, but my /en homepage content isn't). It also outputs lang="default" on the html tag which isn't a meaningful locale.

I can work around most of these issues (except setting the <html lang> attr properly on the / route), but it seems kind of silly. All I really need is a way to get the real pathname, rather than the locale translated pathname, in middleware. If I could get that, I could simply detect whether the pathname contains the /en part or not, and redirect or not based on that. Easy peasy. But I can't figure out how to get it. Is it possible to grab the raw pathname from the request object in middleware?

I really don't want this default locale in there.

Update: I forgot an important detail. We want all the routes to forward to /en - except the homepage. That one should redirect the opposite way - from /en to /.

Update 2: I'd settle for some way to set the lang field on the default homepage's html tag to something other than "default". I can work around everything else.

Update 3: OR - if there's a way to simply get nextjs to stop forwarding "defaultLocale" routes to the unprefixed routes (which is only does _sometimes_) then I wouldn't even need to use middleware, and could just manage it by setting my links properly.

title: SSR with Apollo Client and GraphQL-Scalars
body:
This question is closely related to other questions stemming from the isSerializable check performed when using getServerSideProps, but specifically inquires about the use of Apollo Client's cache.

Referencing the [with-apollo example](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/canary/examples/with-apollo/pages/ssr.js) , addApolloState is used within getServerSideProps, but if the data includes values resolved by the popular [graphql-scalars package](https://www.npmjs.com/package/graphql-scalars), errors are thrown within Next's [isSerializable check](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/canary/packages/next/lib/is-serializable-props.ts). The typical suggestion to stringify and parse values returned from getServerSideProps doesn't work because the values are already in Apollo's cache.

Is there a suggestion to dealing with this isSerializable check and Apollo's cache?

In addition, I'm left wondering if having people run into this problem due to the serializable check is burning more or fewer resources than some people having to figure out the actual problem during hydration.
author: willrust

title: Ability to Handle a Prefetch in Middleware
body:
### Describe the feature you'd like to request

I would like to have some sort of way to detect if a middleware file is being called by a prefetch. Due to Next.js Links prefetching the content of the webpage when the link loads, it causes the middleware to run at undesirable times. I would like a variable to know if the request is prefetched, so I can have custom logic to prevent my code running on prefetch.

### Describe the solution you'd like

I am working on a custom analytics solution to fit my needs with Next.js. I am currently using a middleware file to get analytics on every page, but because Next.js prefetches any links on the page, I have extra page views in my analytics. I would like to have a way to only trigger middleware on the actual viewing of the page.

### Describe alternatives you've considered

I have considered disabling prefetching on all of my links, but I would lose performance and the page would [still prefetch on hover](https://nextjs.org/docs/api-reference/next/link#:~:text=When%20prefetch%20is%20set%20to%20false%2C%20prefetching%20will%20still%20occur%20on%20hover.). I have also considered using the built-in `<a>` tag, but I would again lose performance and my page transitions.

author: rk610

title: How to get raw request path in middleware?
body:
Is there a way to get the raw request pathname in middleware? I specifically want the full pathname with locale - for example, `/en/about-us`. By default, the next API only grants access to the transformed pathname - that is, it'll say `/about-us` for a locale prefixed route.

This is all I need to solve the inconsistent prefix problem for the defaultLocale. Without that though, I can't detect if I'm on a prefixed route or a prefixless route.
author: CaptainN

title: How to disable nextjs's random locale redirects?
body:
I have a multi-locale site, and if I just use the standard default route (en in my case) with links that have a /en/ prefix, everything works great - at runtime. Like, clicking between the pages works, and I stay in the /en/ prefix for every link. But for some reason, if the user loads a link that has /en/ in it, it forwards to the non-prefixed version of the same link after it loads. 

Why does it do that? And why does it only do that on a fresh load? It definitely does NOT do that when you navigate to the same page.

Anyway, I just want to disable that. Is there any way?
author: CaptainN

title: Disable load time redirect for defaultLocale to the unprefixed version of the same page.
body:
### Describe the feature you'd like to request

I want to disable the load time redirect for defaultLocale to the unprefixed version of the same page.

### Describe the solution you'd like

Add a setting to next.config in the i18n field - something like `disableDefaultLocaleRedirects: true`.

### Describe alternatives you've considered

I've looked at trying to use middleware with a default locale to manage this, but that has a lot of complexity and draw backs, when all I really need is for nextjs to stop trying to "correct" my links ([but only sometimes](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/discussions/37759)). I set the links the way I want, and don't need next to redirect anything.

Really, the only thing I need `defaultLocale` for is to configure the homepage's defaultLocale (it's the only route I don't want a prefix for). Everything else should be prefixed. Some setting for that would work as an alternative to this feature request.
author: CaptainN

title: Add ability to manage dir="ltr" through i18n config
body:
### Describe the feature you'd like to request

I'd like to be able to set the html dir attribute along with the lang property in the next.config. For example, when setting `lang="ar"` I also want to set `dir="ltr"`.

### Describe the solution you'd like

For the locales list, instead of just strings, we could accept an object with lang and dir props:

```js
  i18n: {
    locales: ["en", "fr", "de", { lang: "ar", dir: "rtl" }, "jp", "zh"],
    defaultLocale: "en",
    localeDetection: true,
  },
```

### Describe alternatives you've considered

Right now I'm using client side settings only, which has the effect of not serving up a document from SSR/SSG that has the property set correctly, causing a flash of incorrect direction.

I could also add logic to `_document.tsx` (which only works in SSR/SSG, and doesn't respond to client changes). These 2 workarounds would work great, but the setting in `_document.tsx` doesn't seem to be supported by Vercel (the language prop is not defined).

Since the html tag is completely managed by next, best to manage it completely.

title: eslint: next vs typescript-eslint
body:
### Background

I think this might be a bug, but defaulting to user error. Im putting together a small shared repo that includes npm workspaces for common packages and for webapps (all part of the same bounded context). I am also using typescript-eslint with the type-checking rules across the codebase and want to use 'next/core-web-vitals' on the next apps. This appears to a be an issue with the next eslint config and not typescript-eslint.

Relevant repo structure:

> .eslintrc.js
> tsconfig.json
> tsconfig.eslint.json
> apps/next-starter/.eslintrc.js


### What Happens

When extending next/core-web-vitals in the apps/next-starter/.eslintrc.js config, eslint reports that:

>  You must therefore provide a value for the "parserOptions.project" property for @typescript-eslint/parser.

But when I move the extends setting to the repo's config rather than the next app's local config, eslint is able to run.

In both cases, `eslint --print-config` shows the property configured parserOptions, including the project and tscconfigRootDir. This also only happens when using  'plugin:@typescript-eslint/recommended-requiring-type-checking'. The eslintrc file is local to the next app but just in case, setting the [rootDir](https://nextjs.org/docs/basic-features/eslint#rootdir) had no effect.

### What Should Happen

I have local eslint files rather than a single root level config with completed overrides. I'd like   `extends: 'next/core-web-vitals` configuration to only be in the next apps' eslint configs.
 
### Configs
**.eslintrc.js**
```js
module.exports = {
  root: true,
  parser: '@typescript-eslint/parser',
  parserOptions: {
    tsconfigRootDir: __dirname,
    project: ['./tsconfig.eslint.json', './packages/*/tsconfig.json', './apps/*/tsconfig.json'],
  },
  plugins: ['@typescript-eslint'],
  extends: [
    'eslint:recommended',
    'next/core-web-vitals',
    'plugin:node/recommended',
    'plugin:@typescript-eslint/recommended',
    'plugin:@typescript-eslint/recommended-requiring-type-checking',
  ],
  rules: {
    'node/no-unsupported-features/es-syntax': ['error', { ignores: ['modules'] }],
  },
};
```
**tsconfig.json**
```json
{
  "compilerOptions": {
    "allowJs": false,
    "allowSyntheticDefaultImports": true,
    "allowUnreachableCode": false,
    "allowUnusedLabels": false,
    "checkJs": false,
    "declaration": true,
    "declarationMap": true,
    "emitDecoratorMetadata": true,
    "esModuleInterop": true,
    "experimentalDecorators": true,
    "forceConsistentCasingInFileNames": true,
    "isolatedModules": true,
    "lib": ["es2017"],
    "module": "commonjs",
    "moduleResolution": "node",
    "noEmit": true,
    "noImplicitAny": true,
    "noImplicitReturns": true,
    "noImplicitThis": true,
    "noUnusedParameters": true,
    "outDir": "dist",
    "paths": {},
    "pretty": true,
    "removeComments": true,
    "resolveJsonModule": true,
    "skipLibCheck": true,
    "sourceMap": true,
    "strict": true,
    "strictNullChecks": true,
    "target": "es2017"
  }
}
```

**tsconfig.eslint.json**
```json
{
  "compilerOptions": {
    "types": ["@types/node"],
    "incremental": true,
    "noEmit": true,
    "allowJs": true
  },
  "extends": "./tsconfig.json",
  "include": [
    ".eslintrc.js",
    "./packages/*/tsconfig.json",
    "./apps/*/tsconfig.json",
    "./apps/*/.eslintrc.js"
  ]
}
```

**apps/next-starter/.eslintrc.js**
```js
module.exports = {
  extends: 'next/core-web-vitals',
};
```

### Versions

- eslint 8.17.0
- next 12.1.6
- tsc 4.7.3

body:
Hi there,

I'm building an online food delivery app for restaurant. So it's all about a small catalog (about hundred rows), but updating frequently because of stop lists. I'm trying to make it fast and seo-friendly with ssg.

I have two pages.
1. The first is index with all meals.

body:
### Verify canary release

### Provide environment information

next build

### Describe the Bug

When using server-side rendering with next@12.1.6, the context doesn't get passed down to the children component.
Due to this, the ReactDom does not work as expected and the expected component is not getting rendered on the page.

NOTE: This issue cannot be reproduced with other versions of nextjs its only specific to 12.1.6.
           The issue also gets resolved when upgrading react to the latest version i.e. 18.2.0 (this also works with next@12.1.6)
           Due to breaking changes we cannot upgrade to react18 and asking our customer to downgrade to lower versions of nextjs 
           doesn't sound very friendly.


### Expected Behavior

The context should get passed down to the children and all components should render as expected.

### Link to reproduction

N/A

### To Reproduce

Described above

### Describe the Bug

I had my development server running in the background without me remembering and I wanted to build my app using `next build`, that's what I've got:
```sh
$ yarn next build
yarn run v1.22.17
$ C:\development\activity-builder\node_modules\.bin\next build
info  - Loaded env from C:\development\activity-builder\.env

title: :global selector should be made available on root css module level
body:
Currently i'm working on some projects which use Nextjs and (s)css modules. Sometimes though, it's really easier to just make your class globaly selectable so that other components can access it. 

However, most of the time it's a pain in the ass because the `:global` selector is not available on the root level of the css file (next throws an error), so you have to wrap your code in a `.root` class to avoid said crashing. But sometimes you might not want to alter your html structure.

A workaround perhaps: have a `component.global.scss` or something that lives next to your `.module` file which automatically global scopes your classes. A generic (s)css file can't be imported in the component itself because it has to be imported in `_app.tsx` file.
I'd like to keep my component's css in the same folder and this could potentially solve that.

Would love to see some improvements so styling cross-component would become a bit easier while maintaining scoped code. It's definitely better than one massive global css file but I have to admit I miss the easy developer experience sometimes.

title: Configurable proxy options for rewrites
body:
### Describe the feature you'd like to request

Currently, there is no way to configure the proxy options for the "rewrites" function. For instance, if I want to disable [x-forward-x headers](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/pull/17557), it's hard coded with option `xfwd: true`.

### Describe the solution you'd like

I think making the `new Proxy({options})` configurable would be a way that users can easily customize the proxy's behavior.

OR making the proxy instance replaceable (injecting from next.conf.js?)
author: imZack

title: How to handle local dates in the client (browser timezone) with UTC dates on the server? (Hydration mismatch / Text content did not match)
body:
I would like to ask for a case where a mismatch is not unexpected, but rather **expected**: Local dates.




There are some discussions around problems related to hydration mismatches, the typical "Text content did not match" problem. With React 18, this throws a real error instead of being a `console.error`, so a lot more devs will notice it. e.g. https://github.com/vercel/next.js/discussions/35773#discussioncomment-2510947

---

In my app, I want to show the time in the browser timezone. But the static/server pages are created on the server (via `getStaticProps`/`getServerSideProps`) with UTC timzezone. For me, this is an expected mismatch:

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/29319414/174776666-60cac6e2-c45d-4d8b-8af2-612647cd3272.png)

To my knowledge, there are a few ways to handle this:

- Use Next.js' [Internationalized Routing](https://nextjs.org/docs/advanced-features/i18n-routing) to create different pages. This assumes you're using the language identifier in the routes (like `../en/..`), which then are used for creating the pages with the matching timezone. Using locale identifiers in the routes is not something that everyone wants to do though.
- When rendering a date, render `null` on the server and only create the formatted date on the client (e.g. via `useEffect`). This brings "flickering" with it, as content that was not there on the server now has to be there on the client.
- Set the timezone of the server statically to the one that is expected in the browser. This obviously only works when you're only serving to one specific timezone.

...but all of these are not perfect - I feel like I'm missing something?

Does anyone have a better way or other proposals on how to handle this?

title: Docs: Escaping regex characters in redirects, rewrites
body:
Hi, I'm currently working on using regex in `redirects`, and thought that the documentation could be a little confusing. >

The following characters (, ), {, }, :, *, +, ? are used for regex path matching, so when used in the source as non-special values they must be escaped by adding \\ before them:

Both [redirects](https://nextjs.org/docs/api-reference/next.config.js/redirects#regex-path-matching), [rewrites](https://nextjs.org/docs/api-reference/next.config.js/rewrites#regex-path-matching) have the same sentence. The thing is, this rule of escaping does not apply only in **`source`**. It also applies to `destination`, or `value` in `has` field. So the sentence is not really wrong, but I think it is misleading that it applied only to `source`. How do you think?

body:
Hello, next.js team

You are using Next.js well, but you have encountered one problem.

When sending a request to the Next.js API from another domain, Next.js is not receiving cookies.

The client set it to withCredentials: true and next.js set it to credentials: true using the cors package.

Requests between cross-origin are normal, but cookies cannot be imported.

I suspect the google same-site policy issue. Can I set same-site to none in next.js?

Help me.
author: hoo00nn

title: css property scroll-behavior: smooth  breaks next Link scroll to top
body:
### Verify canary release

- [X] I verified that the issue exists in Next.js canary release

### Provide environment information

Operating System:
      Platform: linux
      Arch: x64
      Version: #58~20.04.1-Ubuntu SMP Tue Jun 14 11:29:12 UTC 2022
    Binaries:
      Node: 14.15.1
      npm: 8.7.0
      Yarn: N/A
      pnpm: N/A
    Relevant packages:
      next: 12.1.6
      react: 18.1.0
      react-dom: 18.1.0

### What browser are you using? (if relevant)

firefox

### How are you deploying your application? (if relevant)

next 

### Describe the Bug

When I adding 
html{
    scroll-behavior: smooth;
}

Next js Link tag doesnt work scroll to top

### Expected Behavior

I need scroll-behavior: smooth for all website

### Link to reproduction

locale

### To Reproduce

add 
html{
    scroll-behavior: smooth;
} to css file
author: AnnaShubskaya

title: Any ideas of why my contact form works from localhots3000 but not live on the web?
body:
So I got a contact form working good when I test it from my localhost3000....  I check the console, the terminal, and everything is good... few seconds later I got the email.

No npm run build erros.... I actually updated some meta tags and they are now showing good on the live website too...


But the contact form doesn't send any emails...  I check the console and seems to work OK... I cannot check the terminal because I don't have a terminal from the live browser...   but what else can I possibly do to test the contact form from the live website? 


I could wait 48 hours for propagation? ... but the meta tags were uploaded later, and the meta tags are showing up OK, but the form still not sending me an email...

If interested in the code this is the api:
https://github.com/Elindo586/nextprojectb/blob/main/pages/api/contact.js

and this is the form:
https://github.com/Elindo586/nextprojectb/blob/main/pages/components/contact-form.js
author: Elindo586

title: Javascript operators is no longer recognised after upgrading from version 11 to 12.1.6
body:
We have upgraded our app from Next 11 to Next 12.1.6.
Everything works fine locally and also when I run "npm start run".

The issue comes when the app is deployed. I currently get the following error:
![CleanShot 2022-06-22 at 17 32 05](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/100878713/175072235-9d8333d1-b15a-4535-9e29-edf28b59672c.png)

### An here is the next.config.js:

`{
    "presets": ["next/babel"]
}`

Please let me know if you needed more information and I provide it right away.

Any help would be appreciated, Thanks!

title: Relation between hashChangeStart, hashChangeComplete and hashchange
body:
Hello, 

I am wondering why next router is not connected to the `hashchange` event. Currently I have to manually trigger the events `hashChangeStart` and `hashChangeComplete`. Is that an expected behaviour or a bug?
author: zuffik

title: Issue with router.push
body:
I am using next along with react 18.
On using push, url of my app gets changed by app is stuck in a loop as the new page keeps on getting rendered. 

This issue was not present when I was using react 17. 

I am using push something like

```
push({
        pathname: `${routeName}`,
        hash: `${hashValue}`,
      })

```
Is this bug known or anyone has a similar issue
author: amanSethSmava

title: Cannot destructure property 'components' of 'object null' as it is null
body:
Today, my application suddenly stopped working in my local environment after running `npm run build` and then restarting the server with `npm run dev`.

I'm getting the following stack trace:

```
TypeError: Cannot destructure property 'components' of 'object null' as it is null.
    at DevServer.renderToResponseWithComponents (/Users/my-user/github/My-Github-Account/my-repo/node_modules/next/dist/server/base-server.js:705:90)
    at DevServer.renderErrorToResponse (/Users/my-user/github/My-Github-Account/my-repo/node_modules/next/dist/server/base-server.js:1203:35)
    at async DevServer.pipe (/Users/my-user/github/My-Github-Account/my-repo/node_modules/next/dist/server/base-server.js:628:25)
    at async Object.fn (/Users/my-user/github/My-Github-Account/my-repo/node_modules/next/dist/server/base-server.js:504:21)
    at async Router.execute (/Users/my-user/github/My-Github-Account/my-repo/node_modules/next/dist/server/router.js:226:36)
    at async DevServer.run (/Users/my-user/github/My-Github-Account/my-repo/node_modules/next/dist/server/base-server.js:606:29)
    at async DevServer.run (/Users/my-user/github/My-Github-Account/my-repo/node_modules/next/dist/server/dev/next-dev-server.js:450:20)
    at async DevServer.handleRequest (/Users/my-user/github/My-Github-Account/my-repo/node_modules/next/dist/server/base-server.js:321:20)
```

My project deploys to Vercel, which is building correctly and the application is running in the cloud.

Things I have tried without luck:
- Restarting my macbook
- Cloning a fresh copy of the repo and running `npm i && npm run dev`
- `rm -rf ./node_modules && npm i && npm run dev`
- Checking out an older commit which should work
- Downgrading NextJs a couple different minor versions
- Running a different version of node with `nvm`

I have no idea what's happened here. But I can't get past this error now.

Use node v16.14.2, npm 8.5.0.

package.json
```
{
  "name": "with-typescript",
  "version": "1.0.0",
  "scripts": {
    "dev": "next -p 4200",
    "build": "next build",
    "start": "next start -p 4200",
    "type-check": "tsc",
    "lint": "next lint"
  },
  "dependencies": {
    "@ant-design/icons": "^4.7.0",
    "@hubspot/api-client": "^7.0.0",
    "@mapbox/mapbox-gl-draw": "^1.3.0",
    "@mapbox/mapbox-gl-geocoder": "^5.0.1",
    "@react-pdf/renderer": "^1.6.16",

author: dawnerd

title: Return a JSON response from page instead of a React component?
body:
Next.js has very good caching and cache invalidation features in Static Site Generation and Incremental Static Regeneration. I want to use these features not to generate HTML, but to generate a JSON that my other HTML pages can use.

To generate this JSON, the route will initially request the data from my headless CMS. But, like regular React component pages, further requests will be cached and served for free from the cache. Then after the `revalidate` period has passed, or by using the `NextResponse.unstable_revalidate` feature, the JSON cache will be refreshed.

I don't think this feature is currently supported in Next.js, but when/if it comes, it would be very useful.

My question is, as a stopgap measure, is it safe to assume that `_next/data/<next.js build id>/[param].json` will always be there? Can I have my HTML pages request JSON data from routes under `_next/data` to achieve similar results to the process I described above?

Now if you're wondering, why not just build your JSON in the `getStaticProps` of the page itself? Why do you want the `getStaticProps` of your first page to request JSON data from your second page?

Well, I have 1000s of pages. They all need the same piece of data. If each one of them requests that piece of data from the headless CMS independently, I will have made 1000s requests to the headless CMS. If only one page makes that request and caches it, then my other pages can re-use the results of that request. In that case I would be making 1 request instead of 1000s. And the request/response latency would be a lot faster.
author: msafi

title: Option to change the directory for API Routes
body:
### Describe the feature you'd like to request

Currently, to create API Routes, we have to put those files under the `pages` directory inside `api` folder. Having all the APIs nested inside the `pages` directory makes it a hassle to jump back and forth pages and APIs. Often times, I get lost in the nesting while looking for a specific file inside the `pages` folder. I'm sure a lot of other developers also feel the same problem. 

This is not the end of the world, but if there was any option to change where the `api` folder belongs, it could be better for most of the developers working with Nextjs. Probably we want to get the `api` folder out of the `pages` directory and put it directly in the project's root. Having that kind of flexibility would make the Developer Experience a lot better. Some other frameworks like Nuxtjs already does it with their REST API system.

### Describe the solution you'd like

An option to change where the `api` directory belongs. Probably some kind of property inside `next.config.js` that indicates where the folder should be. Developers can configure this as per their requirements.

Example: 

```
module.exports = {
   apiDirectory:  "pages/api"     // Can be any absolute path
}
```

### Describe alternatives you've considered

I haven't actually found any other alternatives for this issue. I have found some remapping configs for the API, but that is not what I am looking for exactly.

author: JamesSingleton

title: Allow for external `cache/images` dir for next/image processing
body:
### Describe the feature you'd like to request

First, LOVE the `next/image` optimizer. It works great and requires no other setup than simply using `next/image` 👍🏻 

## Problem
The only problem is the limited number of loader options that some of us enterprise companies can't use (ie political red tape, vendor lock-in, what have you). So we're left with the [default loader option of processing/storing optimized images](https://nextjs.org/docs/api-reference/next/image#caching-behavior), which doesn't work well for scaled applications running on 50+ distributed containers. We actually have had to turn image opt OFF with `unoptimized` because there's too much overhead processing of images, since the `cache/images` dir must be repopulated on each pod (eg round robin). Even if we do happen to fill the cache on every pod, after lots of CPU usage, we deploy multiple times a day and those ephemeral containers are wiped restarting the entire process. In the end, we decided we may as well not even have image optimization ☹️ 

## Solution
We can't use one of [external vendors](https://nextjs.org/docs/api-reference/next/image#built-in-loaders) NextJS supports, so we're left with the default loader. In order to support distributed systems, what if we introduced a new `next.config.js` option that allows overriding the default `<distDir>/cache/images` directory to make the cache write/read to an external CDN?

For example,

```js
module.exports = {
  images: {
    loader: 'default',
    cacheDirPath: 'https://cdn.example.com/cache/images',
    cdnAccessKey: 23423432
  },
}
```

This way all default image processing will be written to and read from `https://cdn.example.com/cache/images` to a shared cache dir.

### Describe the solution you'd like

Support distributed web servers by allowing the default image loader to **write to** and **read from** a CDN `https://cdn.example.com/cache/images` to create a shared cache dir. We still want to use nextjs' squooshing capabilities, just send those images to a CDN instead of same server.

For example,

```js
module.exports = {
  images: {
    loader: 'default',
    cacheDirPath: 'https://cdn.example.com/cache/images',
    cdnAccessKey: 23423432  // for uploading
  },
}
```

Now image _could_ be fetched like so:

`https://cdn.example.com/cache/images/some-image&w=1080&q=75.jpg`

and if it also stores as webp:

`https://cdn.example.com/cache/images/some-image&w=1080&q=75.webp`

diff sizes or qualities:

`https://cdn.example.com/cache/images/some-image&w=480&q=75.jpg`

I was thinking I'd leave the params in there as part of filename so it matches the filename in a raw bucket store.

### Describe alternatives you've considered

I've tried implementing a custom loader, doesn't work as I want it to in this case at least I couldn't find anything without being super hacky where I implement my own squoosh/sharp component that processes and uploads images and regenerates the link for me. That just didn't work.

```js
 loader: 'default',
 path: 'https://cdn.example.com/cache/images',
```
AND
```js
 loader: 'custom',
 path: 'https://cdn.example.com/cache/images',
```

title: How to avoid "Error: Hydration failed because the initial UI does not match what was rendered on the server." in Next.js
body:
I am getting this expected error:

```
Unhandled Runtime Error
Error: Hydration failed because the initial UI does not match what was rendered on the server.
```

And

```
Unhandled Runtime Error
Error: Text content does not match server-rendered HTML.
```

This has happened to me twice so far in situations I would like to do it _anyways_, and ignore the error. How can I ignore the error, or in what ways can I accomplish the following.

In the first situation, I add a class name if it's a mobile view vs. a landscape/desktop view, which doesn't exist on the server. This throws the "Hydration failed" error because there is an additional class name added to the DOM on client-side rendering only. I do it in a `useLayoutEffect` because otherwise I get a jolt of desktop-styled content on mobile before the class name is added, if I just use `useEffect`.

In the second situation, I am doing an onboarding flow where you can't skip steps but can only go to the furthest step you have completed (and you can go back to previous steps). But there are no URL changes, it is all kept track of in `localStorage`, because what is linking to the middle of an onboarding flow going to really accomplish in the grand scheme of things? But at the same time I would like to catch common mistakes like accidentally refreshing the page, it should jump you back to where you were in the flow. Etc..

In that situation, say there are 4 onboarding steps, and I left off at the 3rd one. Well, on the server it is going to render the first step, but on the client it is going render the 3rd step, as expected. But it also throws that "Hydration failed" error. This is desired behavior though (at least I think?!). This is how I've always implemented it in the past outside of Next.js.

How can I either ignore this error, or workaround it?

I really like the idea of having server-side rendering, and for all intents and purposes, the server-side rendering is correct (it is stateless). But when it reaches the client, which has some state, it might slightly adjust the content of the page. I don't see why it needs to error, or how to avoid this problem. Any help would be appreciated! Thank you.

title: outputStandalone - allow adding implicit application dependencies in the output
body:
### Describe the feature you'd like to request


**Before** running my application, I need to _preload_ a local module which instruments my app code and customizes some low-level modules which Next uses (e.g.`node -r ./preloadModule.js server.js`). To add, this local module depends on some packages which are not used by the application. Preloading the local module in regular builds works since `node_modules` includes any package. 

This won't work with `standaloneOutput` since the preloaded module's dependencies are not used by any application file.

For a more detailed description of the setup, you can read this [post](https://jake.tl/notes/2021-04-04-nextjs-preload-hack). The post utilizes a [server-preload.js](https://gist.github.com/justjake/6fa39b07421a58ad58ff013b141b7172#file-server-preload-js) which is very similar to my implementation.





### Describe the solution you'd like

If the feature request IS relevant to its scope, I would like a solution that could satisfy the aforementioned use case without adding overhead (custom solutions) or compromisations (disabling features).

Examples
* Would it be possible for example to add our own tracing sources? 
* Could the default server configuration be extended / or at least have the custom server part of the standalone output? I found some discussion for the former as in https://github.com/vercel/next.js/discussions/11686 .

### Describe alternatives you've considered

- I tried to bundle my preload module separately to keep it decoupled. My POC did not work yet though. 
- I probably can cheat with adding the extra dependencies by importing them in some api/route but this seems very hackish.

**Any suggestions are highly appreciated!**

author: molink-huseong

title: Next.js bundle size tracker
body:
One of the questions I usually have when changing code is: How does this change affect the bundle sizes. Mostly as a safeguard to warn me in case an innocent-looking change would lead to excessive changes in the bundle size (which can affect page performance).

When you want to do that, you need a place (some sort of database) that stores these numbers so that you can track them over time and compare (eg. between the main branch and a PR). While there are ways to get by without (eg. by storing the data in the repository itself, or by building the project twice to compare before/after) they are not ideal. Also, having the all historical data available in a database makes it possible to plot the development over a longer period of time, and not just compare two adjacent versions. 

I wrote a web service and GitHub Actions integration with a focus on tracking Next.js page bundle sizes. Though it could be used for a lot more use cases (npm package sizes, page load times, lines of code in the project, …), my focus was definitely on Next.js.

The tool, called Niquis, is freely available to all open source projects (mainly because I haven't done that much to secure the API). The guide how to set it up on GitHub Actions is here: https://niquis.notion.site/Niquis-for-Next-js-via-GitHub-Action-ee3496b7abe540018ab54be5e290fbc6.

Use it, give plenty feedback, send me your ideas how I can improve the tool and make it better.

title: [Next/Router] Component not getting current initial props when routing to same page
body:
Hello there!

I'm using React ^17.0.2 and Next.js 12.1.6. I'm trying to figure out a problem I'm having with Next/Router when navigating to the same route, where the component seems to mount without unmounting + gets an incorrect version of initialProps. I'm routing to the same route, but only changing the router `asPath` and the query.

In my (class) component, I have `getInitialProps` set up. I need to capture a boolean that I get from the query in `getInitialProps` - let's call it `doCoolThing`.

When I `router.push()` from the same page to itself (and change the query), the following happens:
- `componentDidUpdate` fires, with the old version of the props and `doCoolThing`
- `getInitialProps` fires, returning the **correct** value of `doCoolThing`
- The router navigates to the new `asPath` (the Chrome dev console shows `Navigated to ...`)
- `UNSAFE_componentWillMount` and `componentDidMount` fire, with the **incorrect** value of `doCoolThing` in the props

It seems that the correct value of `doCoolThing` disappears when the router navigates! I would expect the `UNSAFE_componentWillMount` and `componentDidMount` to have the correct value, since `getInitialProps` returned the correct value.

I tried adding a unique key to my component's initialProps to force a unmount/remount, by setting it to `query.doCoolThing` in `getInitialProps`, but what instead happens is:
- `componentDidUpdate` fires, with the old version of the props and `doCoolThing`
- `getInitialProps` fires, returning the correct value of `doCoolThing`
- `UNSAFE_componentWillMount` and `componentDidMount` fire, with the **correct** value of `doCoolThing` in the props
- The component unmounts, then the component mounts
- **Then,** the router navigates to the new `asPath`
- `UNSAFE_componentWillMount` and `componentDidMount` fire again, with the **incorrect** value of `doCoolThing` in the props

And again, the correct value of `doCoolThing` disappears when the router navigates! 

I've read [#2819](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/issues/2819), which similarly describes the situation I'm in.

How can I get the correct value of `doCoolThing` in `UNSAFE_componentWillMount`? Could someone explain when `getInitialProps` gets called and why the correct value isn't being passed as I'd expect? I think I'm missing something with how React manages rerenders and when the Next/Router calls are made.

author: jinrilong

title: ISR Pages not using browser cache, (max-age header not there) and reduce CDN requests.
body:
### Describe the feature you'd like to request

right now pages using the **getStaticProps** have the cache header as

`Cache-Control: s-maxage=60, stale-while-revalidate`

_note: I have revalidate: 60 sec in the object returned from getStaticProps_

I understand this means we use the stale response in the browser if available
 for the page,  while checking for updates.

My setup is 

**Browser ---> CDN ---> Nextjs Origin server**


**In CDN:**

When checking updates in the background, the request will go to CDN and CDN will check if it has the page in the cache, then it will serve that otherwise, it will send an expired page response while it refreshes the page in the background with nextjs origin server.

here I see an important point missing is browser cache is missed as we do not have the `max-age` header in the nextjs server response, 





### Describe the solution you'd like

If the nextjs could provide cache control header as

`Cache-Control: max-age=60, s-maxage=60, stale-while-revalidate` --> this can have at-most have 120 sec (cnd 60 _ browser 60) cached data.

then browser cache will be involved for 60 sec since page is fetched and it prevents the request go to CDN for each request in the browser, so that we could avoid CDN RTT and use the browser cache efficiently and serve the page quickly.



### Describe alternatives you've considered

Either we can have some option to include the max-age rather than having s-max-age as default so that we could use the browser cache and avoid hitting the CDN.
author: shmdhussain

title: Environment Variable from shell overwrites all .env*
body:
Maybe this is intentional but why is an environment variable from the shell overwriting variables in .env and .env.local? I have the following situation on a MacBook running macOS BigSur 11.6.7:

```shell
printenv | grep MONGODB_DB
MONGODB_DB=db_shell
```

```shell
cat .env | grep MONGODB_DB
MONGODB_DB=db_env
```

```shell
cat .env.local | grep MONGODB_DB
MONGODB_DB=db_env_local
```

... and when I access the variable in the NextJS app using `process.env.MONGODB_DB` I get `db_shell` - which is not what I want as I have to remove all my environment variables that might collide with variables defines in NextJS.

Btw. the page https://nextjs.org/docs/basic-features/environment-variables does not explicitly mention the overwrite by shell variables (except that what `process.env` is meant to say). It would be nice if it was explicitly mentioned in the [Load Order](https://nextjs.org/docs/basic-features/environment-variables#environment-variable-load-order) section

I can understand that this feature might be interesting when someone needs to inject variables from the outside (e.g., in a docker container) but on a developer machine or a server with multiple applications all shell environment variables (including secrets etc.) are then available within the nextjs app.

If this is intentional - is it possible to disable or clear all variables when running the nextjs app (without removing all variables from the shell)?

title: New Image Future is a downgrade?
body:
Hi guys, I became today the email that a new nextjs version was released and there is a new Image Component in the Future branch. I mean this one: [NextJS Site](https://nextjs.org/docs/api-reference/next/future/image) Changes are listed as the following: Renders a single without
or wrappers Adds support for canonical style prop Removes layout, objectFit, and objectPosition props in favor of style or className Removes IntersectionObserver implementation in favor of [native lazy loading](https://caniuse.com/loading-lazy-attr) Removes loader config in favor of [loader](https://nextjs.org/docs/api-reference/next/future/image#loader) prop Note: there is no fill mode so width & height props are required Note: the [onError](https://nextjs.org/docs/api-reference/next/future/image#onerror) prop might behave differently That now it is only compiled to one img tag is in my view a profit. But that they did remove layout, objectfit and objectposition is kinda a disadvantage in my opinion. For example I used to render a picture of myself as a quadrate the following code: ```javascript ``` But with the new tag everything changes and in my opinion not into the positive. Why should I use the style tags to define the object properties? I think the old way of styling it was more convenient. I also played a lot with the layout properties around when the Image did not look like I expected to do it. How would I even do this with css? What would be the same CSS properties for the layout ones? Am I the only one who thinks so and cannot see the advantages or are there other people who see it like me? Kind regards, Daniel Oberlechner

author: itsikcircle

title: Feature request: a way to regenerate redirects
body:
Hi all,

Love NextJS. Love the new on-demand revalidation feature. That's huge.

We have some large projects in production right now that are hooked up to [Payload CMS](https://payloadcms.com), where within, we allow site admins to manage their own redirects. 

The `redirects` functionality within NextJS is performant and beautiful, and works perfectly when combined with Payload.

But.... we need a way to trigger a rebuild of redirects on-the-fly when a redirect is added or changed. Some of our clients are quite active in trying to keep a handle on their 404s and similar, leveraging redirects from a headless CMS, but without a way for them to publish redirects, they need to reach out to us and have us rebuild the entire site whenever they add / change a redirect. 

It would be great if there was a feature that would allow us to programmatically rebuild redirects. Maybe the same could be said for rewrites as well, but our immediate need is only regarding redirects.

That's all - thank you!

title: Next CLI starts double server
body:
`const start = require('next/dist/cli/next-start')` starts dev server. It overwrites contents of `.next` folder with files of dev server.
```js
// app.js
const start = require('next/dist/cli/next-start')
console.log('process.env.NODE_ENV:', process.env.NODE_ENV)
```
```
> node app.js
process.env.NODE_ENV: development
ready - started server on 0.0.0.0:3000, url: http://localhost:3000
event - compiled client and server successfully in 627 ms (173 modules)

Then `start.nextStart()` starts second server

// app.js
const start = require('next/dist/cli/next-start')
console.log('process.env.NODE_ENV:', process.env.NODE_ENV)
start.nextStart()

title: AWS hosting SSG ecommerce site
body:
Hi there,

My team is trying to host a next.js ecommerce app using AWS services because we have the aws credits in our account so we are thinking to utilise it. We are shifting from a fullstack C# nopcommerce app to RESTful api and a js frontend framework. The apis are ady built in and ready to go, so we are trying to use nextjs as our frontend app that provides features like seo and image optimization.

So far we come across AWS amplify service as the most commonly used service to host nextjs outside of vercel. However, we are not planning to use server side rendering and we are not building any api files in the nextjs app, as we ady a fully working api mentioned above, so the idea is to use SSG + ISG + Client side rendering only to handle the data fetched from api. 

So far we come across AWS amplify service as the most commonly used service to host nextjs outside of vercel. 

Question:
1. Does a SSG site on aws amplify still has the feature of incremental static generation (ISG) ? Because its not practical to rebuild the whole site if we have a product detail changed.
2. Does ISG requires aws lambda to work?
3. Is there any alternative to AWS amplify? We have a working EC2 VM so if possible we are trying to host our nextjs in the VM instead of using amplify

author: santbob

title: Making `next/router` and `next/link` more testable
body:
I'd love to discuss how we could work together to make Next apps more testable.

I’ve been working on [`next-router-mock`](https://www.npmjs.com/package/next-router-mock), which helps tests to mock `next/router` and `next/link`.  However, there’s some difficulty in mocking these modules, and the workarounds all rely on internal paths, which frequently change between Next releases.

I'm sure we can create a solution to make this easily preventable.

Some examples:

- Mocking "dynamic routes" depends on several internal Next modules (eg. `shared/lib/router/utils` and others)
  - https://github.com/scottrippey/next-router-mock/blob/main/src/dynamic-routes/next-12.ts#L1-L9
- Mocking in Storybook depends on the `shared/lib/router-context` module, which has changed paths in past releases
  - https://github.com/scottrippey/next-router-mock/blob/main/src/MemoryRouterProvider/next-11.tsx
- Mocking `next/link` broke in `v12.2.0` because it now uses a `Context` instead of the `router` singleton 
  - https://github.com/scottrippey/next-router-mock/issues/58

I would love to discuss ways to work together to help minimize these kinds of breaking changes.  There are a lot of folks using `next-router-mock` to unit-test their Next application, and I'd love to make sure these folks are not hindered from upgrading to the latest versions of Next!

title: Switch ESLint for Deno Lint
body:
### Describe the feature you'd like to request

Hi,

Since Next.js is very performance oriented and investing in Rust for its toolchain with SWC as the compiler and minifier, I think it'll be cool if you can replace ESlint with Deno Lint.

Deno uses it in production since I think forever and looks like it has many of ESlint's rules, the only problem (maybe) is that I'm not sure how easy it to code custom rules for it.

Anyway, it looks production ready, actively maintained and is over **112x times faster** than ESlint, taking about 0.02 seconds instead of 2.25 seconds in the example provided by the deno team.
Also, it's written in Rust!

Link: https://github.com/denoland/deno_lint

### Describe the solution you'd like

If migration is relatively easy, I think replacing ESLint with Deno Lint will be great.

I know it's possible for Deno Lint to work with other environments like in Node.js, and is **not** limited to Deno.

### Describe alternatives you've considered

One alternative is obviously ESlint which you're already using, which works great but is pretty slow.

Another alternative is RSlint, which is extremely fast and written in Rust, but is not production ready.
Overall Deno Lint seems more stable, more maintained and even faster based on some benchmarks I read.

author: aomini

title: next/image is not honoring the `maxAge` set by upstream images
body:
Hey team,
I see that documentation of next/image about the [caching behaviour](https://nextjs.org/docs/api-reference/next/image#caching-behavior) states that

> The expiration (or rather Max Age) of the optimized image is defined by either the minimumCacheTTL or the upstream image Cache-Control header, whichever is larger.

My upstream image has a header `cache-control: max-age=31536000`. When I use this image with next/image component, this `maxAge` value is not honoured at all and header is added like `Cache-Control: public, max-age=0, must-revalidate`.

When I looked at the [code](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/12.1.7-canary.8/packages/next/server/image-optimizer.ts#L577), it correctly states that it will be always `Cache-Control: public, max-age=0, must-revalidate`.

This was got changed under [commit](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/commit/130f8645a2db18527142a7764eaa5e649183cc74), but the commit message does not talk more about why this was done.

Can you please help me understand is this a bug introduced in next/image?

//cc @styfle
author: RishikeshDarandale

title: Calculate the dynamic max-age from upstream image response headers
body:
### Describe the feature you'd like to request

When next/image honors the max-age from Cache-Control header of upstream image. There is possibility that the upstream image is served from CDN and response might contain `Cache-Control` and [`Age`](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTTP/Headers/Age) header.

i.e.
```
age: 8730
cache-control: max-age=31536000
```

next/image api should set the max-age as `31536000-8730`

### Describe the solution you'd like

The new `max-age` should be calculated based on upstream `Cache-Control` and `Age` header as below:

```
age: 8730
cache-control: max-age=31536000
```

next/image api should set the max-age as `31536000-8730`

### Describe alternatives you've considered

NA

title: Allow standalone output server.js port to be non-numeric
body:
### Describe the feature you'd like to request

When using `output: "standalone"` in next.config.js, a server.js file is automatically generated. The server.js assumes that the port can only be numeric via the following line:
`const currentPort = parseInt(process.env.PORT, 10) || 3000` 

However, when using iisnode to host a Next.js application in Windows Server, `process.env.PORT` is a named pipe (a string). This causes problems where starting the Next server will default to port 3000 instead of using the port provided by iisnode. I'm requesting to allow `currentPort` to be a string.

There have been other libraries that have run into the same problem:
https://github.com/dotansimha/graphql-yoga/issues/232

I'm also willing to open a PR if needed - just let me know!

### Describe the solution you'd like

Instead of generating server.js with
`const currentPort = parseInt(process.env.PORT, 10) || 3000` 
use
`const currentPort = process.env.PORT || 3000` 

From my tests, changing that line allows iisnode to host the application properly. 

### Describe alternatives you've considered

I've considered using a custom server to deploy with iisnode but I'd prefer to keep using features like automatic static optimization. I also thought of not including `.next/standalone/server.js` in every deploy and keeping a single copy of server.js from a previous build. However, I wasn't sure how often the output of server.js would be changing and would prefer not to constantly check a diff between the newly generated one and the existing one.
author: myronng

title: SSG: when is it reasonable to do client-side pagination?
body:
I'm building a statically generated Next.js blog which fetch data from a CMS. In our CMS we have a lot of posts. On our posts page, we want to show a posts list. We want to show 10 posts with a button to load another 10 posts. We also want to filter posts by category.

I see two ways to go about this:

1. We could fetch all posts at build-time using `getStaticProps` and just toggle client-side how many posts should be displayed on the page, and filter with client-side javascript.

2. Or we could fetch 10 posts at build-time and use something like SWR or react-query to fetch more data from the client.

It seems vastly more simple to fetch everything at build-time, so that we don't have to bother with authorizing client requests to our CMS (or setup proxies etc).

However I also realize that it is at somepoint not reasonable to do this. The question is what the bottle-neck with the first approach is and at what quantity of posts I should lean towards the second option instead?

title: Allow starter templates to customize instructions printed by create-next-app
body:
### Describe the feature you'd like to request

The create-next-app prints some very useful instructions after the Next.js app has been created. However, there could be additional instructions that a starter template would like to print. For example, if a starter template requires env variables to be set by the developer, it would be useful to indicate that as part of the instructions printed by the CLI tool.

### Describe the solution you'd like

Ideally, it would be possible to customize the instructions that are printed by the CLI tool as a new Next.js app is created. For example, the commands that a starter template provides could be different.

A potential solution would be to allow starter templates to include a file, for example `get-started.js`, which would then generate the message printed by the CLI tool.

### Describe alternatives you've considered

An alternative solution is that starter templates would be able to append messages, instead of being able to override what is output by create-next-app.

author: Ali-Hussein-dev

title: Precompiling and bundling dependencies with fatal bugs inhibits upgrades of next.js
body:
### Verify canary release

- [X] I verified that the issue exists in the latest Next.js canary release

### Provide environment information

not applicable

### What browser are you using? (if relevant)

n/a

### How are you deploying your application? (if relevant)

n/a

### Describe the Bug

Next.js is taking the position of bundling strategic dependencies for packaging performance. One such example of this is `node-fetch`, which can be seen in the compiled section of the next.js npm tarball.

`node-fetch` _generally works._ However, in our project, we have 10s of teams issues 1000s of queries. With that volume, probabilistically, we start hitting all of the edge cases. `node-fetch` has many open issues (https://github.com/node-fetch/node-fetch/issues), some quite fatal [1](https://github.com/node-fetch/node-fetch/issues/1568) [2](https://github.com/node-fetch/node-fetch/issues/1380). By using package manager solutions (overrides, resolutions, hooks, etc), we were able to circumvent flaws in transitive dependencies easily before, to use versions and patches to workaround issues of interest gracefully. However, precompiling such dependencies in the distribution makes correcting such issues ergonomically challenging. Next.js ejects from traditional dependency distribution, and even [meddles with node internals (module resolution)](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/pull/32742/files#diff-f5e0118d3ce0589ca0de992c92d52aa5bd8014dd4a2d95fb8ff0b6ee153d0d43R132-R143), hindering me from using a specific version of node-fetch that I require in the active node process, both within a next.js context & within my custom server.

Possible solutions:

1. Fix the upstream bugs, then upgrade next.js to use the latest upstream versions. This is the ideal outcome, always. However, in a world of perpetual change, it's not the most tenable. Bugs will always exist, and consuming projects will always lag behind.
1. Use patch-package! This could work, however, next.js code would need to be deeply modified, as various imports are now  explicitly too local `./dist/node-fetch`, vs just `node-fetch`. next.js now has hard coupling to its own vendored 3p dependencies, so a patch would not be simple or precise, it would impact many modules, thus be risky/brittle (as with patch-package, generally).
1. use normal node/npm dependency distribution

### Expected Behavior

Various behaviors could be expected, and are all debatable/subjective.

- To sustain prior art, use normal node packaging-isms for dependencies, that allows custom resolutions as offered by package managers.
  - Perhaps make tradeoffs. For simple/stable dependencies, keep w/ the precompile vendoring strategy. But for unstable (subjective) and problematic dependencies such as something as _core to web as fetch_, choose to not bundle such dependencies.
- Allow users to swap in dependencies. next.js is meddling with `module` resolution to point all imports of `node-fetch` to it's compiled version. This is very problematic, especially given that we use a custom server and do all sorts of stuff with fetch outside of `next.js`. We cannot upgrade to next@latest because of this change.

My ideal outcome would have `node-fetch` removed from https://github.com/vercel/next.js/pull/32742's changeset (cc/ @ijjk , as they may have stronger opinions on this as the implementer of 32742)

### Link to reproduction

n/a

### To Reproduce

n/a, this is a design discussion

title: Add support for a configureable response when getStaticProps encounters an error
body:
### Describe the feature you'd like to request

Right now, I only have a few options in getStaticProps, essentially: `success`, `redirect`, and `not found`. 

### Describe the solution you'd like

I'd like to have my site return a different status page in different scenarios. For example, a 5XX series error if the CMS can't be reached (instead of `notFound: true`) vs a 404 in the event that I could contact the CMS but the requested information simply does not exist.

### Describe alternatives you've considered

I could use `redirect` to send the user to an appropriate 5XX status page. However, that doesn't seem like the most direct way to achieve the desired result (and I'd have to send any dynamic error message in the query params, I'm guessing). I'm new to nextjs so if this is a completely valid pattern I can implement it. However my inclination would be that a redirect would be used when I want to deliberately send the user to some other resource rather than inform them directly of the status/state/error of the resource they are requesting. If redirecting is the intended flow to use, it's at least odd (without any context) that `notFound` gets a special case.

body:

Has anyone been able to deploy a working Nextjs app in a docker image with Pnpm in a monorepo context ?

I want to deploy a nextjs app that depends on a shared package in a monorepo context.
I'm using the following dockerfile.

```dockerfile
FROM node:16-alpine as Builder
RUN apk add --no-cache libc6-compat
RUN npm install -g pnpm@7.4.0


WORKDIR /app
COPY package.json pnpm-lock.yaml pnpm-workspace.yaml tsconfig*.json ./
COPY ./packages/common ./packages/common/
COPY ./packages/bo ./packages/bo/

RUN pnpm install --frozen-lockfile
RUN pnpm --filter common build
RUN pnpm --filter bo build


FROM node:16.14.0-alpine
RUN apk update && apk --no-cache add tzdata && cp /usr/share/zoneinfo/Europe/Paris /etc/localtime
WORKDIR /app

# Uncomment the following line in case you want to disable telemetry during runtime.

COPY --from=builder /app/packages/bo/public ./public

title: Consider using lightningcss as a CSS minifier
body: ### Describe the feature you'd like to request [Lightning CSS](https://github.com/parcel-bundler/lightningcss ) is a new CSS transformer and minifier in Rust, built by the team behind the Parcel bundler. It is significantly faster than any other CSS minifier I am aware of, while producing smaller output in many cases. It's over 100x faster than cssnano, the CSS minifier used by Next.js today. I think Next.js could adopt `lightningcss` as a minifier easily, and users would see reduced production build times, with potential for reduced bundle sizes as well. In the future, additional features such as replacing postcss-preset-env and autoprefixer could also be adopted, but minification is the easiest place to start. The GitHub readme has information about the features. For more about the internal architecture, see the announcement [blog post](https://parceljs.org/blog/parcel-css/). As if March, it has been the default CSS minifier in Parcel. ### Describe the solution you'd like [css-minimizer-webpack-plugin](https://github.com/webpack-contrib/css-minimizer-webpack-plugin) already supports using`lightningcss` with a one line change. ```js new CssMinimizerPlugin({ minify: CssMinimizerPlugin.lightningCssMinify, // ... }) ``` This could easily be opt-in at first (similar to the `swcMinify` option for JavaScript) if you are concerned about bugs or regressions. ### Describe alternatives you've considered I am biased as the main author of `lightningcss`, but I think changing the default would be a good choice. However, if you don't want to do this, I think it would be nice to be able to customize the minifier more easily. I tried to write a Next.js plugin to do this, but it involves some brittle hackery to the webpack config. There's no way to detect the default minimizer plugin, so you have to assume the index in the list of minimizers in the webpack config doesn't change in order to delete the default and add the replacement. Perhaps a config option similar to the one supported by css-minimizer-webpack-plugin could be supported to allow users choice over the minifier they use. I am aware that SWC is also working on a CSS minifier. However, `lightningcss` is ready today. Again, I'm biased, but I think the architecture of Lightning CSS will enable it to continue to be faster and produce smaller output. At least offering users a choice over what minifier they use would be better than locking them in.

title: Support postcss.config.cjs
body:
### Verify canary release

- [X] I verified that the issue exists in the latest Next.js canary release

### Provide environment information

    Operating System:
      Platform: darwin
      Arch: arm64
      Version: Darwin Kernel Version 21.5.0: Tue Apr 26 21:08:37 PDT 2022; root:xnu-8020.121.3~4/RELEASE_ARM64_T6000
    Binaries:
      Node: 16.14.0
      npm: 8.3.1
      Yarn: 1.22.17
      pnpm: 6.11.0
    Relevant packages:
      next: 12.2.1
      eslint-config-next: 12.2.1
      react: 18.2.0
      react-dom: 18.2.0

### What browser are you using? (if relevant)

_No response_

### How are you deploying your application? (if relevant)

_No response_

### Describe the Bug

When Next attempts to find a PostCSS configuration, it doesn't look for files ending in `.config.cjs`, and the configuration is not loaded.

### Expected Behavior

PostCSS supports using `postcss.config.cjs` as a configuration file (https://github.com/postcss/postcss/issues/1329#issuecomment-877863139). Next should find and load this configuration file.

### Link to reproduction

https://stackblitz.com/edit/vercel-next-js-dxk18b?file=pages/index.tsx

### To Reproduce

1. Set up a Next.js app with Tailwind by following the instructions at: https://tailwindcss.com/docs/guides/nextjs
2. Create a component with Tailwind classnames
3. Run the app and see that the Tailwind styles are applied to the component
4. Add `"type": "module"` to the `package.json` of the Next.js app
5. Update `next.config.js`, `postcss.config.js`, and `tailwind.config.js` to use `.config.cjs` file extension
6. Run the app and see that the Tailwind styles are no longer applied to the component

title: Allow additional import for the SWC modularized imports
body:
### Describe the feature you'd like to request

I want to add an additional css source file import when modularizing the import statement of a React component (similar to that of [babel-plugin-import](https://www.npmjs.com/package/babel-plugin-import)), but it seems there is no way of doing so.

Example import in source code:
```
import { Header, Footer } from 'my-library/components/App'
```

Expected-transformed imports:

```
import Header from 'my-library/components/App/Header'
import 'my-library/components/App/Header/style'
import Footer from 'my-library/components/App/Footer'
import 'my-library/components/App/Footer/style'
```

### Describe the solution you'd like

I feel like a viable solution would be to allow additional import alongside the transform:

```
modularizeImports: {
  'my-library/?(((\\w*)?/?)*)': {
    transform: 'my-library/{{ matches.[1] }}/{{member}}',
    additionalImports: [
      'my-library/{{ matches.[1] }}/{{member}}/style',
    ],
  },
}
```
This approach fits my use case, but it might not be a generic way of solving this kind of problem. 

### Describe alternatives you've considered

Can't think of any at this moment.
