title: next-build: don't generate `next-env.d.ts` if if already exists
body:
### Describe the feature you'd like to request

At build, Next.js automatically generates the `next-env.d.ts` in project root, see demo here: https://share.cleanshot.com/nLYI2T

The "problem" is, I place my other types in for example `src/types` (e.g. when augmenting Next-Auth.js module to include more stuff in the user as discussed in [t3-oss/create-t3-app#180](https://github.com/t3-oss/create-t3-app/pull/180).

I would like to keep them in the same place. Currently, the `next-env.d.ts` has to live in one place (project root) where as my other types lives elsewhere (src/types). 

### Describe the solution you'd like

If found somewhere in the project, the `next-env.d.ts` shouldn't have to be generated at build but instead use the available one. This could either be by explicitly telling Next where to find the types or internally looked up at build time before creating a "copy".

Pseudo example of what I mean:
```ts
const generateTypeDef = () => {
  if (findInRepo("next-env.d.ts") {
    return;
  }
  else {
    fs.copy("next-env.d.ts", projectRoot); // or generate it in some way?
  }
}
```

For example, in the demo linked above, the types exists so they won't have to be generated at build time

### Describe alternatives you've considered

I could keep it like it is today:

```
# root
src
  types
    next-auth.d.ts
  ...
next-env.d.ts
...
```

or even keep everything in root
```
# root
src
  ...
next-auth.d.ts
next-env.d.ts
...
```

but this is messy imo. I would rather gather all of them elsewhere (especially if having lots of types)

title: Incremental Static Regeneration seems to regenerate pages that were not visited.
body:
We have a website that runs off of Contentful content, over 600 pages. I have a console log that runs anytime our [[...slug]].tsx page is built (so whenever one of those 600 pages is built). You can see each console log for each page being built when we run a local build. After the build, and running "next start" locally, I was testing the static regeneration with revalidate set to 60. I would expect that, after I load the root page "/", that after 60 seconds pass and I load the page again, I would see that console log run that shows the page is being regenerated. This is true, I do see that console log, but I ALSO see logs for about 10-15 other seemingly random pages being rebuilt, even though I did not visit those pages. Why is this occurring? Shouldn't NextJS only be regenerating the page that I just visited, and not other random pages? Services like Contentful have rate limits, so if pages are just none stop being regenerated, we hit those limits seemingly all the time, which causes other issues inside the application. I am looking to see if anyone else has run into this and if it is expected NextJS behavior. According to their documentation on ISR, it does not seem to be.

Thanks!

author: Aldhanekaa

title: NextApiRequest vs NextRequest headers
body:
Hello,

I'm new to next.js and have a question about parsing headers in different parts of the code.

I want to parse the header injected by my reverse proxy and read specific data from it in different places.

There are two big differences that I have encountered so far:
When coming from the middleware, `req ` is a `NextRequest` object. (https://nextjs.org/docs/advanced-features/middleware)
Coming from an internal API call, `req` is a `NextApiRequest` instance. (https://nextjs.org/docs/api-routes/introduction)

Reading headers needs to be handled differently here:
```
export async function getIdentityToken(req: NextApiRequest | NextRequest): Promise<JwtPayload> {
  let jwt = req.headers['x-forwarded-jwt'] ?? ''
  if (req instanceof NextRequest) {
    jwt = req.headers.get('x-forwarded-jwt')
  }
  return jwtDecode(String(jwt))
}
```

Is there a more streamlined or well-practiced way of handling this without having to check the object type?

author: MoochiesTechnologies

title: Disabling Transfer-Encoding: chunked on production.
body:
Hello,
We've been using NextJS 12.1.0 with Cloudfront successfully for some time. So far the next compress was set to false and we relied on AWS Cloudfront to compress the files (brotli/gzip).

Recently we migrated to 12.2.0 and the HTTP headers from the next were changed to follow the HTTP/1.1 standard instead of the HTTP/1.0 - before the migration we received the Content-Length header (which is required for Cloudfront to work).

> - The file must be of a type that CloudFront compresses.
- The file size must be between 1,000 and 10,000,000 bytes.
- The response must include a Content-Length header so CloudFront can determine whether the file size is in the range that CloudFront compresses. If the Content-Length header is missing, CloudFront won't compress the file.
- The response must not include a Content-Encoding header.

However, after we migrated to 12.2.0 all requests followed the HTTP/1.1 protocol meaning that we no longer receive Content-Length header, but we started receiving `Transfer-Encoding: chunked` and the files were sent in chunks. 

Is there a way to bring back the old behavior without using additional reverse proxies or if not - what would be the proper solution to our problem? We'd like to not compress the files at NextJS level and only compress them at our reverse proxy.

title: GET /_next/image randomly start returning HTTP 500  on Azure App Service static website
body:
### Verify canary release

- [X] I verified that the issue exists in the latest Next.js canary release

### Provide environment information

NextJS 12.0.7
NodeJS Node 14 LTS 
Static Website 
Azure App Service


### What browser are you using? (if relevant)

Any browser 

### How are you deploying your application? (if relevant)

NextJS 12.0.7 SSG and Azure App services  

### Describe the Bug

We have a static website generated using NextJS and for the images we use NextJS Image component. What happens is that images on the website  randomly starts returning HTTP 500 after a while.  The HTTP 500 returns for random images in the website and at random points in time. Some time's it takes days and some just minutes after we deploy the website. When it happens we have to restart the App Service so the error disappears and it starts working again. Only to come back again. 

GET /_next/image returns HTTP 500

### Expected Behavior

Expect the images to continue loading without any errors.

### Link to reproduction

we don't have it.

### To Reproduce

Since this is a random issue... There is no way to reproduce it.

But when I was trying to build docker image, next build didn't work well.

`logs of docker build`
```
 > [builder 5/5] RUN yarn build:
#15 0.647 yarn run v1.22.19
#15 0.728 $ next build

------
executor failed running [/bin/sh -c yarn build]: exit code: 1
```

`next.config.js`
``` javascript
/**
 * @type {import('next').NextConfig}
 */
const nextConfig = {
  /* config options here */
  output: "standalone",
  webpack: (config) => {
    config.module.rules.push({

    });

    return config;
  },
};

module.exports = nextConfig;
```

`Dockerfile`
``` Dockerfile
# https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/canary/examples/with-docker/Dockerfile

# Install dependencies only when needed
FROM node:16-alpine AS deps
# Check https://github.com/nodejs/docker-node/tree/b4117f9333da4138b03a546ec926ef50a31506c3#nodealpine to understand why libc6-compat might be needed.
RUN apk add --no-cache libc6-compat
WORKDIR /app
COPY package.json yarn.lock ./
RUN yarn install --frozen-lockfile

# If using npm with a `package-lock.json` comment out above and use below instead
# COPY package.json package-lock.json ./ 
# RUN npm ci

# Rebuild the source code only when needed
FROM node:16-alpine AS builder
WORKDIR /app
COPY --from=deps /app/node_modules ./node_modules
COPY . .

# Next.js collects completely anonymous telemetry data about general usage.
# Learn more here: https://nextjs.org/telemetry
# Uncomment the following line in case you want to disable telemetry during the build.
ENV NEXT_TELEMETRY_DISABLED 1

RUN yarn build

# If using npm comment out above and use below instead
# RUN npm run build

# Production image, copy all the files and run next
FROM node:16-alpine AS runner
WORKDIR /app

ENV NODE_ENV production
# Uncomment the following line in case you want to disable telemetry during runtime.
ENV NEXT_TELEMETRY_DISABLED 1

RUN addgroup --system --gid 1001 nodejs
RUN adduser --system --uid 1001 nextjs

# You only need to copy next.config.js if you are NOT using the default configuration
# COPY --from=builder /app/next.config.js ./
COPY --from=builder /app/public ./public

# Automatically leverage output traces to reduce image size

COPY --from=builder --chown=nextjs:nodejs /app/.next/static ./.next/static

USER nextjs

EXPOSE 3000

ENV PORT 3000

CMD ["node", "server.js"]

title: _document 'link' element doesn't work in production.
body:
I'm trying to get a font from [google fonts](https://fonts.googleapis.com), so I used `link` element in _document file.


`
    <Html>
      <Head>
        <link
          href='https://fonts.googleapis.com/css?family=Poppins:400,400i,700,700i|Rock+Salt:400,400i,700&subset=devanagari,latin_ext,latin&display=swap'
          rel="stylesheet"
        />
      </Head>
      <body>
        <Main />
        <NextScript />
      </body>
    </Html>
 `

The font works fine on development, and I can see in the `head` tag, the `link` element like that.

`<link href="https://fonts.googleapis.com/css?family=Poppins:400,400i,700,700i|Rock+Salt:400,400i,700&subset=devanagari,latin_ext,latin&display=swap" rel="stylesheet">`

But in production, it doesn't bind the `url` to `href` attribute, but it binds it with `data-href`, So it doesn't work, and it looks like that.

`<link data-href="https://fonts.googleapis.com/css?family=Poppins:400,400i,700,700i|Rock+Salt:400,400i,700&subset=devanagari,latin_ext,latin&display=swap" rel="stylesheet">`

Any idea what can be causing this issue.

Thanks a lot.

title: Double slash for next/image src (and also experimental one)
body:
I'm experiencing a problem with double slash for next/image and experimental image. Believe that this small change will help.
```js
    if (
        (images.loader !== 'default' || result.trailingSlash) &&
          images.path[images.path.length - 1] !== '/'
      ) {
        images.path += '/'
      }
```
For code here
https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/3881bbd3ec2e6a0522aecb5753a2b5bc188885b9/packages/next/server/config.ts#L379-L385

And also this issue happens because next for some reason execute this code twice always.

There has been attempt to fix issue in this commit https://github.com/vercel/next.js/commit/0e5e8881c08d2f13f2604327f3940776cc5c6a42 but seems like this has been direct action without attempt to look into the root of issue.
And that's why we have attempt to fix problem here
https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/0e5e8881c08d2f13f2604327f3940776cc5c6a42/packages/next/client/image.tsx#L881
But also same bug here
https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/v12.2.3-canary.11/packages/next/client/future/image.tsx#L780

author: inwardmovement

title: Make `href` property optional for `<Link>`
body: With the recent [`` behavior rework PR](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/pull/36436) to always make `` produce an `` element, I would like to recommend an additional **backwards-compatible** change to the `` component: **make the `href` property optional.** Anchor elements without a `href` attribute are [valid according to specification](https://www.w3.org/TR/2016/REC-html51-20161101/textlevel-semantics.html#the-a-element): >If the [``](https://www.w3.org/TR/2016/REC-html51-20161101/textlevel-semantics.html#elementdef-a) element has an [href](https://www.w3.org/TR/2016/REC-html51-20161101/links.html#element-attrdef-a-href) attribute, then it [represents](https://www.w3.org/TR/2016/REC-html51-20161101/dom.html#represent) a [hyperlink](https://www.w3.org/TR/2016/REC-html51-20161101/links.html#hyperlink) (a hypertext anchor) labeled by its contents. > >**If the [``](https://www.w3.org/TR/2016/REC-html51-20161101/textlevel-semantics.html#elementdef-a) element has no [href](https://www.w3.org/TR/2016/REC-html51-20161101/links.html#element-attrdef-a-href) attribute, then the element [represents](https://www.w3.org/TR/2016/REC-html51-20161101/dom.html#represent) a placeholder for where a link might otherwise have been placed, if it had been relevant, consisting of just the element’s contents.** *[emphasis added]* *(In terms of browser behavior, `` elements without `href` are not focusable, so they are basically "disabled links".)* There are many situations where you may want to use **conditional links**, eg. disabling the current page in a breadcrumb navigation, and right now dealing with such conditional links with the `` component is a pain because of the required `href` property, leading one to write code along the lines of this: ```javascript // typeof href = string | undefined return href ? {/* content */} : {/* content again */} ``` While you could certainly define the content separately and conditionally wrap it at the end, ultimately it's all extra busywork than if you were simply able to write code like this: ```javascript // typeof href = string | undefined return {/* content */} ``` To date, I have been carrying a custom `` component across my Next.js project that wraps `next/link` with two major changes: making it always produce an `` element as well as making the `href` property optional. With the `` behavior rework PR accounting for the first one, it would be nice if Next.js adopted the second one too so that I could retire my custom `` wrapper entirely in favor of the regular `next/link`.

title: Add an ability to have multiple root elements per page
body:
### Describe the feature you'd like to request I want to be able to create a page that has many root elements, where each root attaches to the specified div so that I could load nextjs into a legacy application where only parts of the (legacy) page would be replaced with react components. Microfrontend functionality that nextjs currently provides is appreciated but not very useful for those of us that are migrating real world legacy applications to react. The main issue is that in order to migrate something to react (in nextjs) you have to rewrite the entire page, this is not realistic, each page in the application that I work on is shockingly complex. We want to use react whenever we write any new functionality but realisticly this new functionality will only ever touch 5% of the page (at most) so noone will ever give us time to rewrite 95% of the page in react just to get the 5% done. ### Describe the solution you'd like This is by no means the only way nor neceseraly correct way, but one thing you could do is provide a new component, lets say `Snippet`, that we could use in our pages, e.g. in `./pages/page.tsx` ```tsx import type { NextPage, Snippet } from 'next'; import { Fragment } from 'react'; const Home: NextPage = () => { return content of snippet 1 content of snippet 2 content of snippet ... ; }; ``` P.S. maybe also add an option to attach snippet as a shadow dom, in most cases that would resolve a number of issues with styling that is common when doing this sort of stuff, but that is a nice to have, there are other ways to deal with styling issues. Then in your legacy application you would need to load the assets associated with the above page, I don't think there is currently a way to easily just load the assets without the initial html template so there would need to be something for that ```html
// assuming nextjs is hosted on `/micro/temp-frontend` path // this should load the assets of `./pages/page.tsx` ``` ### Describe alternatives you've considered I currently maintain my own webpack setup to allow the above. (to be clear this means I can't use nextjs and I would really like to it would make my life way easier if I could)

title: Serverless function does not execute the code completely
body:
Hello all,

I am in the process of implementing On Demand ISR. After adjusting some data, I want to revalidate different paths at the same time. Here are two possibilities in my opinion for the route handler:

(A) - All paths are revalidated directly in the handler.
```
... adjust data ...
Promise.all(paths.map(path => res.revalidate(path)))
... return ...
```

(B) - The paths are sent one by one to an endpoint for revalidation.
```
... adjust data ...
Promise.all(paths.map(path => fetch('/api/revalidate', { path })))
... return ...
```

As you can see, I don't use await because I want the revalidation process to run in the background and the user doesn't have to wait for it.

In my opinion (B) is the better variant, because all paths can be revalidated in parallel.

Both variants work locally without problems - both in development mode and after build/start. Unfortunately, however, it does not work with Vercel:

(A) works only with await - but I want to avoid that.

(B) does not work at all. The first path is revalidated, but the other paths are simply omitted. It seems that a POST request is not sent to api/revalidate for all paths and thus the function is not executed.

What am I doing wrong? How can I revalidate all paths in parallel without having to wait for execution?

Best thanks for your help.

author: italodeandra

title: CLI: Allow `basePath` configuration as command line argument
body:
### Describe the feature you'd like to request

When I want to do a production deploy of a static HTML export of a Next.js site to GitHub Pages, or as a piece of a larger site, I need to:

1. Add a `basePath` configuration of something like `"/repoName/"` to the `next.config.js` file
   - **_But_** I don't want that extra segment present for local development
2. Execute `next build`
3. Execute `next export`

I'm seeking a way to more easily automate Next.js projects across my organization, especially without need to worry about their particular application setup (CommonJS, ESM, TypeScript, etc.). As such, I would like to eliminate the need for the manual editing of the configuration file in Step 1 by combining it into Step 2/3.

### Describe the solution you'd like

I would like to be able to pass the desired `basePath` configuration value as a command line argument to the necessary `next` CLI commands, e.g. `next build --base-path=/repoName/`.

I _think_ the `build` command is the only one that matters... 🤔  ... but it could be possible that the `export` command would also need to updated to accept that parameter? 🤷🏻‍♂️ 

### Describe alternatives you've considered

The only good alternative I've found is requiring all of the projects to use a consistent snippet to support conditional values, e.g. using an environment variable to modify the `basePath` value:

```js
module.exports = {
  basePath: process.env.BASE_PATH || process.env.NODE_ENV === 'production' ? '/repoName/' : '/'
}
```

Alas, that requires changes to all of the repositories' codebase as well as participation/enforcement to ensure they remain in that kind of an ideal pattern. 😕 

### Prior Art

- 11ty's `--pathprefix`: https://www.11ty.dev/docs/config/#deploy-to-a-subdirectory-with-a-path-prefix
- Jekyll's `--baseurl`: https://jekyllrb.com/docs/configuration/options/#build-command-options
- Hugo's `--baseURL`: https://gohugo.io/commands/hugo/
- Gatsby is _basically_ there with its `pathPrefix` configuration that **must** be activated via a command line flag (`--prefix-paths`) or environment variable presence (`PREFIX_PATHS=true`): https://www.gatsbyjs.com/docs/how-to/previews-deploys-hosting/path-prefix/
- Proposed feature request for NuxtJS: https://github.com/nuxt/framework/discussions/5976
- etc.

title: SWC styled-components class name hash differs from babel implementation.
body:
### Verify canary release

- [X] I verified that the issue exists in the latest Next.js canary release

### Provide environment information

This occurs in any environment

### Describe the Bug

The SWC styled-components generates the className in this format:
_FileName__ComponentName-sc-**fileContentsHash**-nthComponent_

Whereas the styled babel plugin implementation follows:
_FileName__ComponentName-sc-**filePathHash**-nthComponent_

This is an issue for certain marketing tools (event tracking etc.) that rely on somewhat consistent class names across releases. The Next.js SWC implementation will generate new class names anytime the styles are changed/file contents update. This makes it not a great fit for these tools. Babel on the other hand will have consistent class names across styling changes.

The below issue expands on the use case:
https://github.com/styled-components/babel-plugin-styled-components/issues/309

### Expected Behavior

SWC styled components generate the classname in this format:
FileName__ComponentName-sc-**fileContentsHash**-nthComponent

Whereas it should be this format for consistent classes across styling changes:
FileName__ComponentName-sc-**filePathHash**-nthComponent

### To Reproduce

The next.config.js SWC config:
```
      compiler: {
        styledComponents: {
          ssr: true,
          displayName: true,
          fileName: true,
        },
      },
 ```

The .babelrc file:
```
{
  "presets": [
    [
      "next/babel",
      {
        "runtime": "automatic",
        "useBuiltIns": "usage"
      }
    ]
  ],
  "plugins": [["styled-components", { "ssr": true, "displayName": true, "fileName": true }]]
}
```

The babel implementation can be seen here: https://github.com/styled-components/babel-plugin-styled-components/blob/a7d839465ec4cdf815ea8bc0b0e4bb2f3ffda5a1/src/visitors/displayNameAndId.js#L179

title: Allow to set a basePath in image-optimizier
body:
### Describe the feature you'd like to request

We have an internal service that serves our images. We use this service and the default image loader to optimize images. Currently this requires to use the internal hostname in the image url, like:

`          <Image
            src={"http://image-service:3070/uploads/logo.png"}
          />
`

This leaks the intern service name to the frontend user, what we would like to prevent.


### Describe the solution you'd like

I would like a env var `NEXT_IMAGE_BASEPATH` or an `images: { basePath: }` config property. 

I think this requires somewhat to change `hrefParsed = new URL(url);` into `hrefParsed = new URL(url, process.env.NEXT_IMAGE_BASEPATH);` in the image-optimizer file.



### Describe alternatives you've considered

We've consider to have an api uploads endpoint that then fetches the image, but it feels somewhere double fetching the image.

title: After upgrading to Next 12.2.2 warning started to show up in production
body:
### Verify canary release

- [X] I verified that the issue exists in the latest Next.js canary release

### Provide environment information

npx next info

    Operating System:
      Platform: linux
      Arch: x64
      Version: Ubuntu 20.04.0 LTS Wed Jul 20 2022 17:24:17 GMT+0200 (Central European Summer Time)
    Binaries:
      Node: 16.14.2
      npm: 7.17.0
      Yarn: 1.22.10
      pnpm: N/A
    Relevant packages:
      next: 12.2.3-canary.14
      eslint-config-next: N/A
      react: 18.2.0
      react-dom: 18.2.0

### What browser are you using? (if relevant)

_No response_

### How are you deploying your application? (if relevant)

Custom server on our own infrastructure

### Describe the Bug

After upgrading from Next 12.1 to 12.2.2 we started to see warning in production that were never there before. These are valid warnings, but up until now we only got the during local development.
Unfortunately we have pages that need to return a great amount of data.

### Expected Behavior

Either not log these warnings, or make them configurable.

### Link to reproduction

https://stackblitz.com/edit/vercel-next-js-kg3nhw?file=pages/index.tsx

### To Reproduce

in getServerSideProps return data over 128KB and create a production build

```ts
export const getServerSideProps = async () => {
	return {
		props: {
			a: 'a'.repeat(300_000)
		}
	};
};
```

Just wondering if it is possible out of box to specify shared memory location as part of nextJS config.

The context is the following: 
After deployment by using Kubernetes orchestrator with Docker container. In [doc](https://nextjs.org/docs/basic-features/data-fetching/incremental-static-regeneration#self-hosting-isr) we have: `
To ensure consistency across all pods, you can disable in-memory caching .... You can use a shared network mount in your Kubernetes pods (or similar setup) to reuse the same file-system cache between different containers. 
`

The key question is:  How can I let the next server know about that shared location, on local it is .next/server/pages

title: Access array of page components to build dynamic features
body:
### Describe the feature you'd like to request When using react-router or other routers that you can for example read your page components and centralize this configurations in one file it makes easier to for instance create a side menu dynamically. Next.js brings us a real nice way of avoiding this central place to manage routes using a file system based routing engine, but this without a way for example to access this list of routes or even a way to access some special properties from our page components dynamically makes it a bit harder to take further advantage of such router. At this moment, what exactly I'm trying to achieve? I would like to build a side-menu that add items dynamically based on my file system routes. (Without keeping a static file) ### Describe the solution you'd like I would like to be able to for example, have a list of all routes my app have and also some custom properties being available. For example: ```tsx // /pages/user/index.tsx const UserListPage = () =>

Yay!

export default UserListPage; export const sideMenu = true; export const sideMenuLabel = 'Users'; export const SideMenuIcon = 'user'; // Or ``` then on my for example `/components/SideMenu.tsx` ```tsx export const SideMenu = () => { const router = useRouter(); const pages = router.getAllPages(); const menuItems = pages.filter(({ sideMenu }) => sideMenu) return (<> {menuItems.map(({ path, sideMenuLabel, SideMenuIcon }) => (

{sideMenuLabel}

); } ``` ### Describe alternatives you've considered An alternative to this approach would be maintaining a static configuration file for this, but then everytime we add a new route we would need to update this file, while the dynamic routing from Next.js could help. Another alternative that I tried was to read all pages and import the component to read a property from it as per #3351 but no success.

author: conorshaw

title: Middleware affecting getInitialProps after 12.2
body:
hello,

I have an app that has a _app.js with a `getInitialProps`, but also some other pages that still have `getInitialProps` implemented. 

After updating to Next.js 12.2 we are noticing that the presence of the middleware file makes it so that these `getInitialProps` run both on client and server always (first the sever then the client), removing the middleware file restores the old behaviour where the `getInitialProps` runs only on the client when doing a client transition and on the server when doing a server reload 

this feels a bit at odds with the documentation on `getInitialProps` https://nextjs.org/docs/api-reference/data-fetching/get-initial-props  "For the initial page load, `getInitialProps` will run on the server only. `getInitialProps` will then run on the client when navigating to a different route via the next/link component or by using next/router" -> The page in question does not implement `getServersideProps` (but I do have a `getInitialProps` in `_app.js` as described above)

My question is : Is this intentional behaviour? that adding a middleware affects how the `getInitialProps` function behaves?  And was this meant to be introduced in 12.2? I understand that middleware was not stable before and so there are changes to it that were to be expected but since this behaviour is not documented I was wondering if this is indeed intentional or not.

Here are 2 videos of a bare bones next app demonstrating the issue : 

#### Without any middleware

Without any middleware the behaviour we can see is that `getInitialProps` works as documented, when doing a server load it runs on th server and when doing a client transition it runs on the client 

https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/2866604/180443142-d664c9eb-490a-4319-b185-02ffefff1ebe.mp4

#### With middleware

With middlware we can see that for the same app the behaviour changes, and it now runs always on server and client even when doing client transitions (where previously it would only run on the client)

https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/2866604/180443276-b6176885-0fab-43e5-95a0-35b8e413346e.mp4

Here is the repo reproducing the problem 

https://github.com/joaogarin/middleware-gip

Thank you!
author: joaogarin

title: Prefetch JSONs are being called on every hover event in v12.2

In version 12.2, I noticed that when we hover on a `<Link />`, a JSON file and JavaScript chunks gets downloaded. Now if we hover on the same link again then, only the JSON gets downloaded. No matter how many times we're hovering over a link it always download the JSON.

I am not quite sure whether it is expected or an issue. But in the previous version of Next.js it wasn't happening. I think calling the same JSON and hitting the server so many time does not make sense.

https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/26157513/180446083-70abe152-8c85-4b90-8865-ac707992678a.mp4

title: i18n and static export
body:
### Describe the feature you'd like to request

There is certain set of cases when nothing stops static export from working with i18n, for example using path-based approach is trivial: `/en/...` or `/fr/...`
There was an issue #37575 here but it got stalled. I would like to once again support rationale for this request:
- SSG is often used to deploy to public S3 storage
- When using in combination CDN there is virtually no other way to cache the language specific variants besides explicit path
- If developer still wants to use domain-first approach, we can also generate static exports in paths like `out/example.fr/...`, `out/example.nl/...`. Then there is gonna be a bit manual but available option to specify corresponding CNAME for each domain on the side of the DNS\CDN

### Describe the solution you'd like

Interestingly when I tried removing this condition:
https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/8b721227cf82a0af3be07663dc8d218430c80514/packages/next/export/index.ts#L332
it just worked exactly as needed for path-based approach: generating correct folders and subpaths. Maybe we should replace the condition with check if the locale is configured to use domain?

### Describe alternatives you've considered

Honestly initially I was considering switching to another library, but now I'm just using this hack described above

title: Provide a way to easily build a static API
body:
### Describe the feature you'd like to request

Provide a way to easily build a static JSON API.


### Describe the solution you'd like

For example, have an `/api-static/` route that prerenders and outputs JSON.

### Describe alternatives you've considered

- Using a custom prebuild script [like this](https://www.grouparoo.com/blog/dynamic-json-pages-with-nextjs#method-2-static-file) to generate the api to `/public/`.
- Cherry-pick `/api/` routes to prerender.
- Using Hugo with [custom output formats](https://forestry.io/blog/build-a-json-api-with-hugo/).

See also:
- https://github.com/vercel/next.js/discussions/14991
- https://github.com/vercel/next.js/discussions/37937
- https://github.com/vercel/next.js/discussions/38704

title: How to efficiently load dynamically import components for statically generated pages?

I've been using the `next export` command to export more than 1000 pages statically based on contents from the Contentful CMS. However, as my site grows, I see the JS chunks being added on all the pages even on pages where dynamically imported components are not present.

Upon investigation, I saw dynamically imported components are outputted as HTML when using `next export` and the associated JS code is bundled in a chunk JS file.

I did a quick mock-up:

- [Netlify demo](https://tubular-gnome-1c7ca0.netlify.app/)

where the footer is a dynamically imported component.

I want to know if there is a way when using `next export` to:
- Load only the content found in the "Above the fold" range
- Load only the JS used on a page by using some aggressive chunking mechanism
- Make the dynamic import works as what is documented [here](https://nextjs.org/docs/advanced-features/dynamic-import)

Thanks

title: NextJS: Build once and get API Endpoints URL at runtime
body:
I am building a new NextJS app. Currently, I am in the stage of deploying the app to multiple environments like dev, qa, staging and prod. All the environments have just one change that it uses different endpoints for API.

Now, I want to create a single dockerfile but use API_ENDPOINTS_URL based on the environment variable passed during the start command.

All the examples that I am see online including NextJS documentation, people are using different build commands for different environments.

```
"build:staging": "cross-env NEXT_PUBLIC_APP_URL=staging.com next build",
"start:staging": "cross-env NEXT_PUBLIC_APP_URL=staging.com next start",
"build:prod": "cross-env NEXT_PUBLIC_APP_URL=production.com next build",
"start:prod": "cross-env NEXT_PUBLIC_APP_URL=production.com next start",
```

Even the example mentioned in the NextJS github example uses 3 different dockerfiles for 3 different environments which is surprising.

https://github.com/vercel/next.js/tree/canary/examples/with-docker-multi-env

Is it possible that I build the app once (one docker image) and send the API_URL as part of environment variable during the start command?
author: aniket-kulkarni

title: Next/Image: Animate Transition from blur placeholder to image
body:
Currently whenever you use the blur placeholder to improve LCP, the transition to the original image is a bit jarring. Instead, we want to add an animation that blurs/fades in the original image over the blur. 

This can be implemented easily with the onLoadedCallback like in this example: https://jagad.dev/posts/custom-image-transition-in-nextjs-with-tailwind-css

However, images are cached in the browser and when the image loads instantly in the browser it would be better to skip the animation altogether. The onLoadingComplete callback happens with a significant delay for cached images. There is an onLoad callback native to the img tag as well, which happens sooner but there is a delay that makes it harder to manage the animation patterns.

**Desired behavior**

For new images:
1. Blur image loads first
2. When the full image loads, transition the image in

For browser-cached images:
1. Shows full image, without blur and without animation

**Actual behavior**(based on implementation in market and experimentation)
For new images: (custom transition code) ✅ 

For browser-cached images:
1. Flickers blurred images
OR
2. Has a pop-in animation.

Note that for browser-cached images the experience means more layout shifts than necessary.

What is the ideal implementation?

title: Defer static page generation until first page access
body:
### Describe the feature you'd like to request

We are building our next.js application using docker, so we don't have database access during build time. This results in next.js generating a static page with our mock data instead of the actual data, which means our users will see incorrect data until the next revalidation occurs on this page.

### Describe the solution you'd like

Similar to dynamic routes with `fallback: true`, if there was an option to avoid generating the static page during the build and instead generate the page on the first page access, it would solve this issue.

### Describe alternatives you've considered

getServerSideProps would solve this issue, but it adds a lot of unnecessary server bandwidth for a page that updates infrequently.

title: Failed to hot reload when rebuild on running node server
body:
### Verify canary release

- [X] I verified that the issue exists in the latest Next.js canary release

### Provide environment information

Operating System:
      Platform: win32
      Arch: x64
      Version: Windows 10 Pro
    Binaries:
      Node: 16.13.0
      npm: N/A
      Yarn: 1.22.19
      pnpm: N/A
    Relevant packages:
      next: 12.1.6
      react: 18.2.0
      react-dom: 18.2.0

### What browser are you using? (if relevant)

Chrome 103.0.5060.114

### How are you deploying your application? (if relevant)

next start

### Describe the Bug

I'm deploying a shop project by self Node.js server and using `next build && next start`. 
The first deployment seems good, but when I fix some files and rebuild the project,  files related to the fixed part in `_next/static/chunk/` will run 404.

### Expected Behavior

The result I expect is
1. After `yarn build && yarn start`, deploy the website and keep the server running.
2. Fix something and commit changes, rebuild automatically by `yarn build`.
3. Website hot reload. After new build files are generated, refresh pages will display new fixed parts. 

### Link to reproduction

https://github.com/dianyuyi/gyoza-project

### To Reproduce

The link to reproduction is another personal side project because I can't post a private project, but I've been testing it in the follow steps:

1. Upload project files to self-host Node.js Server
2. run command `yarn build && yarn start`. 
3. It looks good when [first build ](https://drive.google.com/file/d/1OV0rYGjwGb9fXPCMQhGNs0FESWocMzX0/view?usp=sharing)
4. But if keep server running and rebuild, the website run errors [404 error ](https://drive.google.com/file/d/1Ejd_FMBhgT9uAjCB8_BpzbepISvvYmcS/view?usp=sharing)
5. The only way to solve the problem is to restart the server but must wait 10~20 seconds. That means the user will see 502 between the time.

Is there any way to keep the hot reload when rebuilding, and avoid to restart the server?

title: ISR with new pages
body:
Hi,
We are running our app using SSG and ISR (deployed on AWS). Today we noticed that pages added to our CMS aren't being picked up properly by the ISR. 
In the nextjs [docs](https://nextjs.org/docs/basic-features/data-fetching/incremental-static-regeneration) it says '_When a request is made to a path that hasn’t been generated, Next.js will server-render the page on the first request. Future requests will serve the static file from the cache_.'

Does this mean once that page has been stored in cache, ISR will not run on the page again? So if content changes on it, it won't update? 

If that is the case, would on-demand revalidate allow us to pick up new content?

title: Possibility to translate basepath
body:
### Describe the feature you'd like to request

We are self hosting our Next.js applications, and we have a use case where we need to have an app running with different basepaths based on the locale. The main domain is already being used by another app so can not be used.
For example: 
NL: /nl-be/opleidingen
FR: /fr-be/formations

Right now, we need to deploy the application 2 times to separate node servers, because we can not translate the basepath.

### Describe the solution you'd like

It would be great if it was possible to translate the basepath in the next.config.js file.
Example:

```javascript
basePath: {
  'nl-be': '/nl-be/opleidingen',
  'fr-be': '/fr-be/formations'
}
```

### Describe alternatives you've considered

Maybe there's an alternative to do this with middleware, but I don't really see how we could do this, since we don't have access to the app running on the top domain.

title: How to generate the css after the statically generated html files
body:
I am building an ecomm site with nextjs and tailwindcss fed with data from a headless cms. Tailwindcss is using a jit compiler to generate just the necessary cms, however considering the dynamic nature of our site generation, we don't have access to all that information before the build step.

However, we do statically generate most of our pages so it would be ideal if we could run the css build step of the nextjs build pipeline after the html files were generated. This would allow us to point tailwinds jit compiler to the generated html files so it can parse for the css classes in those files, so it can properly whitelist them in the css generation.

So long story short, I want to switch the order in the build process from:
- ...
- building css
- building static html files

to

- ...
- building static html files
- building css

Is there a simple way to do it? If not, is there a way to at least retrigger the build process just for the css outputs in the nextjs pipeline?

author: florianwalther-private

title: Multilang routing in static exports
body:
I see internationalized routing [isn't supported in static export](https://nextjs.org/docs/advanced-features/static-html-export#unsupported-features). What is the way of doing it then? Having `[lang]` dynamic route at the root of the pages so it would look like this?
```
/pages
  - /[lang]
    - index
  - index
```
This will have added benefit of `lang` being available in `router.query` for all pages but the root one. I guess it also should be built with static paths so only a subset of tags wouldn't 404. But what package to use for translation? Pretty sure next-specific ones rely on its logic to resolve language, so won' work with static export.

author: leamandeep

title: Feature request: next/[future/]image use placeholder=blur by default
body:
### Describe the feature you'd like to request

Not using placeholder=blur is easy to forget when developing as local assets load so quickly, so I often find myself & collaborators forgetting it until we do a preview deploy. But ideally, we wouldn’t have to remember to add the prop on static-imported images, because we could set a default to automatically use it for all images where blur data is available (typically via static imports).

### Describe the solution you'd like

A new flag in `next.config.js`, e.g.

```js
images: {
  defaultPlaceholder: “blur”
}
```

(I don’t care what it’s specifically called, this may not be a great name)

Then images with blur data would use them unless opted out, & images without blur data would continue working without errors.

### Describe alternatives you've considered

Could also enable this functionality for everyone as the default without a flag, but it may not be desirable for all users, so opt-in seems better to me.

title: Allow passing mangle options to swc minify, such as keepClassnames and keepFnames
body:
### Describe the feature you'd like to request

Allow passing the [mangle options](https://swc.rs/docs/configuration/minification) to swc minify




### Describe the solution you'd like

I would like to be able to set the following mangle options when using the swc minify:
```
  mangle: {
      keepClassnames: true,
      keepFnames: true
  }
```

This is important when using class constructor names in the code itself.

### Describe alternatives you've considered

The only alternative is using a patch to add those directly to this file: https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/d091c818c5a66c7137275e2b0b711bc105898e19/packages/next/build/webpack/plugins/terser-webpack-plugin/src/index.js#L169
author: JCMais

title: ApolloGraphQL to Next.js Authentication
body:
Hi,
I am looking for suggestions here, if I am doing something wrong 😀
Recently I have been reading articles about security in Next.js & seems like everyone is making it more complicated than it needs to be.

I have a Apollo graphQl api server that on login call sends a httpOnly cookie with a JWT token in it to the next.js client.

Both Next.js and Apollo graphQl are on the same domain, example:
Apollo GraphQl: [app.mydomain.com](https://app.mydomain.com/)
Next js: [www.mydomain.com](https://www.mydomain.com/)

Once the cookie is on the client(next.js), I simply use two statements in next.js:

1 - The fetch calls in next.js have "credentials: include" in options, so if it is a browser to api call it sends the cookie automatically.
2 - If it is a SSR next.js server call I include the cookie in the header via simply "header: { cookie: req.header.cookie }" so it is sent back to api server for auth.
3 - If the cookie is expired the Apollo graphQl server just sends back a 401, in which case I redirect to the login page.

Now I think this is a simple & valid authentication workflow that just works.

Would anyone disagree? 😃

title: When using getStaticProps, does it matter for loading speed if heavy logic is executed inside getStaticProps or the component itself?
body:
My blog posts are all pre-rendered using `getStaticProps`. Right now, I'm parsing markdown to HTML inside `getStaticProps` and do some processing there.
I now want to switch to [react-markdown](https://github.com/remarkjs/react-markdown), which moves the markdown processing into the component itself. Will this have an impact on the loading speed of the page, or does it not matter because it's all run and compile-time?
author: florianwalther-private

title: Why do my images don't load in production when using URL params?
body:
I use [react-markdown](https://github.com/remarkjs/react-markdown) to parse markdown and replace `img` tags for `next/image`s. To set the width and height of the image, I add them as URL search params in markdown like this:

```
![Calculator App](/images/blog/calulcator_app.png?width=700&height=394)
```

I then **parse** these params with react-markdown and set the image dimensions accordingly. The code for that should not be relevant to the question because it works. In **development mode**, the images are displayed in the correct size: 

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/30524875/181906653-8c045d17-2086-48b3-b551-73b33b30430f.png)

However, in **production**, even tho the image containers have the correct size as well, the **URL of the image seems to be broken**:

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/30524875/181906702-7772e1b8-fd86-4d8a-8d58-1caaffbce12f.png)
 
**Why are my images not loading properly in production?** Does NextJS not like URL params in image src URLs? If so, why does it work in dev mode?
author: florianwalther-private

author: LunarMelody

title: Module not found: Can't resolve 'fs'
body:
Hi,

I'm trying to translate markdown to html using

[rsms/markdown-wasm](https://github.com/rsms/markdown-wasm)

I know you get this error ```Module not found: Can't resolve 'fs'``` when you try to load a JS module in a browser that is supposed to be loaded in node.js. I got this error when I tried to load the module on next.js.

```jsx
import * as markdown from "markdown-wasm/dist/markdown.js" // Module not found: Can't resolve 'fs'
export default function Home() {
  return (
    <></>
  )
}
```

However, this can be somehow resolved using pure HTML & JS like

```js
<script src="markdown.js"></script>
<script>
    window["markdown"].ready.then(markdown => {
        console.log(markdown.parse("# hello\n*world*")) // succeed
    })
</script> 
```

The file ```markdown.js``` is copied from the module folder.

How is this possible?

author: mmdalipour

title: Render pages/_error.js in development
body:
### Describe the feature you'd like to request

Currently a custom error page is only rendered in production if an error occurs on the server (e.g. `getServerSideProps`). 
This is documented behavior but makes development on these pages in my opinion needlessly hard. Especially for internal services we want to display rich error information on the frontend instead of having to go into backend logs.

I view this custom error page similar to an error boundary and therefore it wouldn't make sense to only render it in production. Client-side errors trigger error boundaries regardless of dev or prod. The error overlay is simply an addition during development but does not replace the error boundary.


### Describe the solution you'd like

Render `pages/_error` regardless of dev or prod. The error overlay is a dev addition just like client-side errors are handled.

### Describe alternatives you've considered

We currently have to wrap every `getServerSideProps` in a try-catch that renders our error boundary explicitly instead of relying on NextJS rendering `pages/_error.js`. This adds another layer of indirection and requires manual review of new pages.

title: Image optimization causes an error in displaying the fetched images from strapi
body:
### problem⛔:
 image URLs fetched from strapi API don't display correctly on the page. 
### reason for the problem⁉:
 I find out it happens because of image optimization so when you do unoptimized={true} it will work fine. 
```
<Image
            unoptimized={true}
            src={`http://localhost:1337${image URL from api}`}
            width={400}
            height={400}
            alt="an image from starpi API"
          />
```
### the goal✅: 
how to keep image optimization when working with API from strapi.

### how to reproduce the problem: 
#### 1.strapi :

1.1. create a strapi project 
 

> npx create-strapi-starter my-project next-blog.

1.2. add a content type that includes images.
1.3. add images to the strapi database.

#### 2.next js:

2.1. create nextjs project 
     

> npx create-next-app@latest

2.2. fetch image data from starpi API 
     

> http://localhost:1337/api/<API ID (Singular)>?populate=*

2.3. use the fetched URL in Image component

```
<Image
            src={`http://localhost:1337${image URL from api}`}
            width={400}
            height={400}
            alt="an image from starpi API"
          />
```


### more info 🟢:
everything works fine **as long as you use  unoptimized={true}.**
btw I have added localhost to the config file:
```
/** @type {import('next').NextConfig} */
const nextConfig = {
  reactStrictMode: true,
  swcMinify: true,
  images: {
    domains: ["localhost:1337", "localhost"],
  },
};

module.exports = nextConfig;
```

author: jonlambert

title: Image optimizer/`sharp`: option for including metadata
body:
### Describe the feature you'd like to request

I’m dealing with photos by different photographers and it is important to me that meta data is included in image files, so it’s easier to trace back copyright, photographer, etc.

This is supported by `sharp` using [withMetadata()](https://sharp.pixelplumbing.com/api-output#withmetadata). This can be used to include metadata.



### Describe the solution you'd like

I’d like to see a configuration option in next that allows me to hook in between `sharp('input.jpg')` and saving the file (`.toFile('output-with-metadata.jpg`). This kind of configuration option allows even more fine-grained control, if necessary.

Sharp’s API is chainable, so other calls than `withMetadata()` are possible, too.

Something like:

```
imageOptions: (img) => {
    return img.withMetadata()
    // or: return img.withMetadata({ exif: IFD0: { Copyright: 'Wernham Hogg' }})
}
```

### Describe alternatives you've considered

There’s no viable alternatives other than not using next/image.

We built docker images and use Kubernetes for our deployments. Also, we follow branch based deployments. 
Code from 'develop' branch --> deploys to --> DEV cluster and it needs to built with 'dev' specific environment variables.
For example, backend service domain variable like NEXT_PUBLIC_BACKEND_API_DEV.
Similarly, we have 'stage' --> deploys to --> STAGE cluster and must be built with NEXT_PUBLIC_BACKEND_API_STAGE.

Currently, we are building the app using  "next build" command while building the Docker container image.

Looking at the documentation, I see that Next supports multiple .env files like .env.local, .env.dev, .end.stage etc.... but I am not clear on how to access the branch name of the code that is being built inside my build script of Dockerfile and then pass appropriate .env to 'next build ' command.

If user enters or navigates to url -- https://abc.com/posts/123/1.0.0
On this page, I am using getServerSideProps to fetch the data for version 1.0.0 and pass it to a React Component - VersionDetail.
<VersionDetail /> renders properly.

Now, inside <VersionDetail /> , there is a version dropdown that can list 1.1.1, 2.0.0, 3.0.0 etc..
When user changes the dropdown value within <VersionDetail /> to 2.0.0,  I am planning to use 'router.push('/posts/123/2.0.0') so page will fetch the details of the version 2.0.0 using the same 'getServerSideProps' defined before.

My questions:
1. Is this a good approach ? Are there any cons to it?  
2. Should I just use [client side fetching](https://nextjs.org/docs/basic-features/data-fetching/client-side) using useEffect?

title: Conditional rendering a page that uses getStaticProps
body:
Is it possible to choose whether a page generated with `getStaticProps` is served from cache or just from scratch?

I'm using a middleware, where I detect the presence of a specific cookie and I want to serve the cached version of a page if that's present, otherwise I need to serve the exact same page without changing URL, not cached and possibly use just CSR.

Another premise is that almost all of my pages are generated with `getStaticProps` and the vast majority of them use ISR.

I know this can be achieved using rewrites but I'd need to duplicate all my page files so this isn't much of an option.
I tried to set the `Cache-Control` header to `no-cache, no-store, max-age=0, must-revalidate` but it's just ignored.

Does anyone know if what I'm trying to achieve is feasible with the features Next offers right now?

And I have a couple questions out of curiosity:

When does Next set cache control headers for pages that use `getStaticProps` and `getServerSideProps`? I suppose it's after middleware's response.

The [documentation](https://nextjs.org/docs/going-to-production#caching) says:
> If the page is using getStaticProps, it will have a Cache-Control header of s-maxage=REVALIDATE_SECONDS, stale-while-revalidate, or if revalidate is not used , s-maxage=31536000, stale-while-revalidate to cache for the maximum age possible.

Then is there a way to overwrite them for specific pages?

title: Inconsistent getServerSideProps behaviour using NextJS in vercel
body:
I have the following setup

 - `/template`. Loads a page that shows all the templates owned by the current user.
 - `/template/new`. Loads a page that allows the user to create a template and save it.
 - `/api/save-template`. Receives the frontend call made from `/template/new` and handles storing it on the db.

With those routes, a typical use case is the following:
1. User ends up somehow (irrelevant) on `/template/new`.
2. User creates a template and hit "save". A spiner shows up while saving happens.
3. Template payload is sent via POST HTTP request to `/api/save-template`.
4. `/api/save-template` route handles the saving successfully and responds with a status `200`.
5. The spinner finishes and the user is redirected to `/template`, where the newly created template should hopefully show up.

The problem here is on point 5, where in some scenarios, the newly created template doesn't show up. Here's what happens on different scenarios. The app could be running either using `next dev`, `next build && next start`, or deployed on vercel. The database could either be hosted locally on docker, or on the cloud using supabase.

| db \ app | `next dev` | `next build...` | vercel |
|---|---|---|---|
| docker| ✅ | ✅ | haven't tried|
| supabase | ✅ | ✅ | ❌ |

One thing to keep in mind, which might be related to the reason this is not working, is that the redirection is made on the frontend using `router.push('/template')`.

I'm also checking on the client side what templates are available when reaching point 5. I've confirmed this is not a db delay issue because the templates are found by the client. For those checks I'm using the following code:
```js
useEffect(() => {
    const checkSupabase = async () => {
      const templatesResponse = await supabase
        .from<Template[]>('Template')
        .select('*')
      const templates = templatesResponse.data
      console.log('templates found from useEffect')
      console.log(templates)
    }

    checkSupabase()
  }, [])
```

And the last thing I want to point out is that the logs I get from vercel make me think the `getServerSideProps` is not called. I'm logging `templates found from getServerSideProps` to see what's available on that function. I see the log before creating the template (time 21:31:24:00), and then the `/api/save-template` being hit (time 21:31:47:11), but I don't see the second request to `/template` after the redirect. The logs –note the order is newest on the top– are:

```
21:31:47:11 - [POST] /api/save-template


21:31:24:00 - [GET] /template
templates found from getServerSideProps
[]
```

## Questions
1. How is it that I don't see a second log for `[GET] /template` or the `templates found from getServerSideProps` log? Is it not being fired?
2. If `getServerSideProps` is not fired, how come this works when the app is running locally?
3. Is there a consistent solution for this use case? I guess it must be quite common to want to see recently created data.

Thanks! 

[Also asked on [stackoverflow](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/73241640/inconsistent-getserversideprops-behaviour-using-nextjs-in-vercel)]

```js
 scrollToHash() {
    let { hash } = location
    hash = hash && hash.substring(1)
    if (!hash) return

    const el: HTMLElement | null = document.getElementById(hash)
    if (!el) return

    // If we call scrollIntoView() in here without a setTimeout

  }
```

author: Luis-Enrique93

title: Adding `priority` prop for `<Script />` component ⚡
body:
## Adding `priority` prop for `<Script />` component for better loading performance (Inspired from `next/image`).

Hi, we can `preload` or `prefetch` the scripts by adding `priority` prop which helps to enhance the loading performance of the third-party scripts. 
We can `prefetch` the script if  `strategy` is `lazyOnLoad` or else we can `preload` the scripts.

We can `preload` the scripts which have strategy of `beforeInteractive` or `afterInteractive` since Google Chrome advices that preloaded scripts should be used before 3s. And we can `prefetch` the scripts with strategy of `lazyOnLoad` hence this might take longer than 3s to append the script. 

### Caveat & Solutions
This have significant network bandwidth issue if it is not used wisely and we prefer it to use it with `beforeInteractive` strategy (only when 100% necessary) and we can mark it as `fetchpriority` (new HTML related attribute) `low` to not to collide with hydration related scripts. 

### Need suggestions
1) Do we `prefetch` even the scripts with strategy of `afterInteractive` ?
2) Any other ideas ?

title: Add the `apiExtensions` option alongside `pageExtensions`
body: Currently there is no way to colocate API Routes next to the page where they are actually used. Custom page extensions allows us to colocate components like so: ``` - pages - about - components - Staff - Staff.test.tsx - Staff.tsx - index.page.tsx ``` But what if I want to fetch staff members, but I'll only ever need that endpoint for that very specific page? Ideally I could do something like this: ``` - pages - about - components - Staff - Staff.test.tsx - Staff.tsx - staff.api.ts 👈 - index.page.tsx ``` And hit an endpoint like `fetch('/pages/about/components/Staff/staff.api')` Even just allowing me to colocate endpoints by page would be better than nothing: ``` - pages - about - components - Staff - Staff.test.tsx - Staff.tsx - index.page.tsx - about.api.ts 👈 ``` Then hit `fetch('/pages/about/about.api')` You could add this feature by extending `next.config.js` to accept a `apiExtensions` option. It's weird `pageExtensions` was added specifically to support colocation, but API Routes were just bunched in with it and not handled differently. It might make this hard to implement now without introducing a breaking change (thus I included the `/pages` part of the path in the `fetch` examples). Sorry if this seems niche, but I'm a huge colocation fan right now.

title: Render on server for SEO value but defer hydration/render for specific components on client
body:
We have hamburger menu drawer that has a ton of expensive HTML content in it. It's not visible to the user on app load so it doesn't need to be hydrated or rendered immediately (from user perspective).

The catch, hamburger menu drawer is main navigation content. For SEO purposes it's required HTML be available for Google bots. Ideally we would like to only render HTML on server but NOT hydrate hamburger menu drawer on the client. We don't want to have to run that JS operation to support the hydration work for a component that's not visible to user (it's only visible on user select). Ideally drawer is dynamic imported / lazy loaded.

Is it possible to have all HTML on initial request, but dynamic import / lazy load drawer when user selects hamburger menu button?

We have done this:
`const [setShowDrawer, showDrawer] = useState(!isClient);`

// On hamburger button select
`showDrawer(true)`

We get this desired behavior. But it feels dirty, since we get the following expected React warning, since drawer HTML comes from server and then it get removed immediately on the client causing an intentional HTML mismatch...
```
Warning: An error occurred during hydration. The server HTML was replaced with client content in <div>. 
See more info here: https://nextjs.org/docs/messages/react-hydration-error
```

What is the correct way to do what we are trying to do so we don't get that React warning? Is it possible?

We would like to employ this strategy (progressive hydration) for below the fold components as well. We only want the in-view portion to have hydration tasks applied to them. Same problem though, all HTML needs to be there for Google bots. And React will complain if there is a mismatch between server and client HTML.

I looked at this
https://github.com/valcol/react-hydration-on-demand
But I would like to know if there is a native Next.js solution to this problem before reaching for a 3rd party library.


Our versions...
Next 12.2.4
React 18.2.0

**2. How can I implement it in stable middleware?**
> pages/dasboard/_middleware.tsx

```jsx
import { NextRequest, NextResponse } from 'next/server'
import { SESSION_ID } from '../constants'

export function authMiddleware(req: NextRequest) {
  const isLoggedIn = req.cookies[SESSION_ID] || false
  if (isLoggedIn) {
    return NextResponse.next()
  }
  // Update url pathname
  req.nextUrl.pathname = `/${isLoggedIn ? 'dashboard' : 'login'}`

  // Rewrite to url
  return NextResponse.redirect(req.nextUrl)
}

```

title: ESLint rule to enforce `key` property on tags inside `next/head`
body:
### Describe the feature you'd like to request

The [Next.js docs](https://nextjs.org/docs/api-reference/next/head) for the `next/head` component states the following:

> To avoid duplicate tags in your head you can use the key property, which will make sure the tag is only rendered once.

The idea is to enforce this recommendation with an eslint rule to prevent potential bugs (duplicate tags) by spotting cases where a `key` property is missing from tags that live inside `next/head`.

### Describe the solution you'd like

We would need to create new rule for `eslint-plugin-next`. I suggest the name `next-head-key`. Not sure if this should be an error or a warning.

The rule would check for the presence of a `key` prop on:
- Direct children of the `Head` element, 
- Children that are wrapped into maximum one level of `<React.Fragment>`
- Children that are wrapped into a top-level JSX array expression
- Ignore the `<title>` element as it already gets deduped automatically

Here's how I would implement something like this:
https://github.com/vercel/next.js/compare/canary...etienne-martin:next.js:eslint-head-key

### Describe alternatives you've considered

The temporary solution I came up with in a different project was to write a global mock for `next/head` that would throw if a `key` property is missing. Not ideal.

title: How to handle unathorized errors thrown by the server
body:
Hi,

This issue is kinda hard to explain, so I apologize in advance. 😄 

We're building a fairly large Next.js application and are looking a smooth way to handle unauthorized errors. Our amibition is to throw a custom `UnauthorizedError` if the server response is `401`, check the `err` property from the `getInitialProps` context in `_error.tsx`, return a `statusCode` prop of `401` if `err instanceof UnauthorizedError` evaluates to `true`, and have some conditional logic in `_error.tsx` to render an `Unauthorized` component if `props.statusCode === 401`. If `props.statusCode` is anything else, we want to render some "Internal Server Error" markup instead.

In the [example I've made here](https://github.com/alinjie/next-unauthorized-error-repro) the flow works fine, but in our production application a client side error is thrown at some point after the `Unauthorized` component is rendered, causing the `_error.tsx` page's `getInitialProps` function to re-run on the client side (with a different unknown error), making the `statusCode` prop to equal `500` and causing the `_error.tsx` page to never render the `Unauthorized` component, even though the intitial error was `UnauthorizedError`.

**My question is:**
What is the recommended way, if any, to deal with unauthorized errors thrown by the server? We could of have a `try/catch` in every `getServerSideProps` and redirect if the thrown exception is an instance of `UnauthorizedError`, but this quickly becomes tedious as the application grows. The Auth0 SDK, for example, provides a `withPageAuthRequired` helper where they handle auth stuff, is this the best way to handle this type of logic?

Any input is appreciated.

title: What is the best place to generate a request-id and log it?
body:
Hi community,

Since Next.js plays as a server, our dev team want to be able to log it and generate/attach a **consistent** `request-id` for all the http requests initiated by Next.js(unless refresh or request another page). After some days of research I couldn't find an optimal solution still. I only see examples using a `custom server`  but that is not recommended by Next.js team.

The documentation mentioned `pino` but there is no guidance on how to use it without a custom server. I tried to use `middleware` but I don't know where to pass on the `x-request-id` in response header to the next request in `middleware` so the `requestId` will be different on each asset request. 
An exmaple would be like

```
 browser visit a page 
  -> nextjs server generate `{requestId: uuid-1}`
  -> nextjs server send 3 request for different resources(image, api, etc) 
  -> all the 3 request has the same `requestId` and are being logged
````

Any suggestion will be much appreciated.

title: No Content-Length header passed when getServerSideProps is used
body:
I have noticed that when we use `getServerSideProps` the `Content-Length` is not calculated and because of this we are not able to use the automatic compression from AWS CloudFront (we have also set `compress: false` in `next.config.js`)

The decision not to include the header is done here: https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/20486c159d8538a337da6b07b0b4490a3a0d6b91/packages/next/server/send-payload/index.ts#L58 and it has to do with the response being "dynamic". But as for now, I don't understand why is Next deciding not to include this header all the time. 

Is someone else experiencing the same problem?

title: Common chunks in serverless page bundles
body:
As I understand it there is quite a bit of overlap / duplicated code between each serverless page bundle, where each base file at least includes react and react-dom. Adding external libraries also leads to an increase in each page bundle size.

This does not pose a problem when you deploy one Lambda Fn per serverless page bundle (like Now and [serverless-nextjs-plugin](https://github.com/danielcondemarin/serverless-next.js/tree/master/packages/serverless-nextjs-plugin) do.

However for [this project](https://github.com/danielcondemarin/serverless-next.js) I've been maintaining for a while now is not possible to deploy one Lambda Fn. per page as it uses Lambda@Edge behind the scenes where doing this isn't possible.
An example of the issue can be found in https://github.com/serverless-nextjs/serverless-next.js/issues/236 where a user hit the limit of how big the Lambda artifact can be.

Is it possible to output common chunks between sls page bundles? This would considerably reduce duplication. Considering the next framework is pushing so much for serverless this change would give library maintainers like myself the flexibility to take even further what's possible in this area.

I'm more than happy to look into it myself, all I'd need would be a bit of initial guidance 🙌

title: No way to disable prefetching completely in next/link
body:
### Verify canary release

- [X] I verified that the issue exists in the latest Next.js canary release

### Provide environment information

    Binaries:
      Node: 18.5.0
      npm: 8.12.1
      Yarn: 1.22.19
      pnpm: N/A
    Relevant packages:
      next: 12.2.3
      eslint-config-next: 11.0.1
      react: 18.2.0
      react-dom: 18.2.0

### What browser are you using? (if relevant)

_No response_

### How are you deploying your application? (if relevant)

_No response_

### Describe the Bug

While you can disable prefetching when a `<Link>` comes in the viewport, there is no way to disable prefetching on hover with `<Link>`.

This is a bit frustrating for us as we show many links on the page, and it is reasonable that a user could hover over 10-50 before clicking on one. Also, the prefetching use of `<Link>` is irrelevant to us as we prefer to do client-side fetching.

On the flip side though, `<Link>` is great at doing client-side navigation for us, and I prefer using that over manually messing with `router.push/replace` myself.

This behaviour was added in #18859 but sadly there was no discussion about this case.

### Expected Behavior

Would be great if `prefetch={false}` disabled prefetching on hover.

### Link to reproduction

https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/canary/packages/next/client/link.tsx#L477-L496

### To Reproduce

Create a `<Link prefetch={false} />` and see that this code is evaluated when the element is hovered on: https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/canary/packages/next/client/link.tsx#L477-L496

author: Nefcanto

title: Specify different layouts for routes within the same folder in the new layout system
body:
### Describe the feature you'd like to request

After reading the [Layout RFC blog post](https://nextjs.org/blog/layouts-rfc), I realized that the routes within the same folder will inevitably share the same layout.js file.

In most cases, a web app is hosted on a subdomain and has protected routes. Usually, the app homepage is on the root segment and almost all other pages are on the first non-root segment, protected or not. The problem is that those pages have different layouts which are wrapped by different route guards.

For instance, pages on '/' and '/analytics' are protected against non-authenticated users and share the same layout and pages on '/signup' and '/login' are protected against authenticated users and share the same layout. Ideally, we would like '/' and '/analytics' to share the root layout.js file, but at the same time we would like '/signup' and '/login' to share a totally different layout.js file which is currently not possible.

Creating a folder for each segment and adding a layout.js file will not work for two reasons:

1. The root layout.js file will still apply
2. There is no mechanism to tell Next.js that navigating between pages from different folders that have the same layout.js file should not re-render the layout

Currently, the only way to achieve this result is either by keep using getLayout on a per page basis or adding a single layout.js file at the root level of the app folder that displays a conditional layout using useRouter, which is not great.


### Describe the solution you'd like

1. Add the option to prevent layout.js from propagating further down the tree
2. Add the option to specify that the layout.js files from different folders are the same and make sure the layout will not re-render when navigating

### Describe alternatives you've considered

None.

title: Appending conditional CSS classes?
body:
Came across this when creating an animated dropdown for a navbar.

In a strict React implementation, an inline if/else statement can be used with an onClick toggle to set and remove CSS animation styles. In order to provide a default styling (with no animation) for when state is null, a class can be added before the inline if/else operation:

```
<div className={`navbar ${reactState ? "expanded" : "collapsed"}`}>
```

How do I replicate this in NextJS?

I can't find anything in the documentation and have blindly attempted the following (unsuccessfully):

```
<div className={styles.navbar (reactState ? `${styles.expanded}` : `${styles.collapsed}`)}>
```
```
<div className={styles.navbar [reactState ? `${styles.expanded}` : `${styles.collapsed}`]}>
```
```
<div className={styles.navbar `${reactState ? `${styles.expanded}` : `${styles.collapsed}`}`}>
```

The only success I've had is with the following, which seems like overkill:

```
<div className={
  reactState != null ?
    (reactState ? `${styles.navbar} ${styles.expanded}` 
    : `${styles.navbar} ${styles.collapsed}`)
  : styles.navbar
}>
```

I'm clearly not fully understanding how NextJS handles React styling. Any help would be appreciated.

title: Nextjs converts "typeof window" -> "object" in web workers?
body:
Nextjs is converting "typeof window" to "object" in web workers, is this intentional?

From my understanding, Nextjs converts "typeof window" to "object" for the client, and to "undefined" for the server, to help with code splitting / purging. But it also converts it to "object" in web workers, where window should be "undefined".

This causes errors when you try to run some libraries, such as [three.js](https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/blob/9f4de99828c05e71c47e6de0beb4c6e7652e486a/src/Three.js#L168), in a web worker. It uses a "typeof window" check to see if its in a worker or not, but Nextjs is breaking this check.

Whether this is a bug or just a quirk of Nextjs, does anyone know of a way to get around this? I'm trying to run threejs in a worker.

title: Docs: Supporting Next.js App deployment to China
body:
### What is the improvement or update you wish to see?

I created a PR ([vercel/next.js: Pull Request 39228](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/pull/39228)) a few days ago about supporting Next.js app deployment for China, and @leerob mentioned pushing it back on the issue stage, so I'm creating this issue here for other devs to review.

### Is there any context that might help us understand?

A quick background brief: Mainland China is unlike any other country. Vercel or Netlify-like deployment solutions don't exist. [Vercel](https://www.21cloudbox.com/solutions/how-to-speed-up-vercel-in-china.html#:~:text=Does%20Vercel%20work%20in%20China%3F) and [Netlify](https://www.21cloudbox.com/solutions/alternatives-to-netlify-in-china.html) don't work out-of-the-box in China due to their Firewall and other legal issues.

21YunBox created [guides](https://www.21cloudbox.com/solutions/how-to-speed-up-vercel-in-china.html) to make deployment of Next.js apps easy like Vercel for China. If you think this is good conjunction for the Nextjs app in China, I think adding some links on the [next.js docs](https://nextjs.org/docs/deployment) would definitely help developers get less headache for deployment in China.

Do leave comments here, so I know what you guys think.

### Does the docs page already exist? Please link to it.

https://nextjs.org/docs/deployment

title: Image: fade transition for placeholder
body:
### Describe the feature you'd like to request

When using `Next/Image` with `placeholder` prop, full image visibly pops up in place of blurred placeholder. To make it more smooth I suggest implementing fade transition (prop, lets say, `placeholderTransition`, enabled by default).

### Describe the solution you'd like

In my opinion, the best way to do it is to:

1. Load placeholder
2. Load full image under placeholder
3. Animate opacity of placeholder to 0
4. Remove placeholder completely

### Describe alternatives you've considered

Alternative solution could be crossfade:

1. Load placeholder
2. Load full image above placeholder, opacity 0
3. Animate simultaneously
   - Opacity of full image from 0 to 1
   - Opacity of placeholder from 1 to 0
4. Remove placeholder completely

title: Caching Pages with CDN and NextJS SSG
body:
Hi,
we are using NextJS with an Azure Front Door CDN in front. 
Because we have world wide user base we already cache complete pages (not only static files) in the CDN which are generated by NextJS with SSG and invalidated with ISR.
We already faced problems with displaying an old page after a deployment (because the page is cached in CDN) and requesting old js resources which are not cached/invalidated in the CDN and after the deployment not available in the server anymore. This issue was relatively easy to solve by pushing all static resource versions to central storage.
Now we are facing the related issue that the old page cached by the CDN cannot retrieve the data via /_next/data/${buildid} because it is not available any more after deployment.
Do you have good recommendations on how to solve this?
Considerations we already took:
- also keep a history of the deployments for a certain amount of time -> seems like too much overhead (costs, complex routing) 
- cache only the static content and never the pages (with our worldwide user base we are afraid of the latencies and performance impact)
