author: theHarisKhan

title: Inconsistency with environment variables in middleware
body:
This seems like an inconsistency in how Vercel handles environment variables for middleware.

1. In local development, ANY environment variable in your local .env file is made available to middleware.ts files
2. In dev/branch/production environment on Vercel, only environment variables in your .env file prefaced with NEXT_PUBLIC_ are made available to middleware.ts files
3. In dev/branch/production environment on Vercel, any Vercel created (from their interface rather than injected by Dotenv) are made available to middleware.ts files

This is was an unpleasant surprise.

Is there a design decision for this or is it just something overlooked? I would expect middleware to have access to all envs in my packaged .env file.

Welcome any and all thoughts.

title: Performance implications of rewrite
body:
Hey folks 👋, I've been using Next.js for quite some time now, and it's an honour to see it grow and evolve.

I have a small doubt about the performance implications of the `rewrites`.

Usually, whenever we build our application, we have a `server` and a `frontend`.

Say the server is hosted at https://example.com, and the frontend is hosted at https://sigmafit.vercel.app. We cannot use cookies in this architecture as third-party cookies are disabled by default in modern browsers.

However, we can rewrite all outgoing requests from the `frontend -> server` using the `rewrite` feature. 

My question is, what're the performance implications of it? How a rewrite is implemented in Next.js. Does a `rewrite` cause a serverless function to proxy my request?

### Additional context
* Live deployment of a website using Next.js rewrite: https://sigmafit.tech/
* Corresponding `next.config.js`: [next.config.js](https://github.com/SigmaFitness/sigmafit/blob/prod/apps/frontend/next.config.js#L6-L9)

author: thanhtutzaw

title: Is it possible to hide the locale in urls for localized routing?
body:
## The Question
I was wondering if there is any way of having the same visible URL for the client while being able to handle internationalization. 

## My use case
* I want my users to have universal access to pages and hide the locales from the URL path. If a user shares a link with another user, the content should be loaded in the preferred language of the current user that opens the page.
* I'm fetching localized data on the server-side with `getServerSideProps` and caching the result with `Cache-Control` for 5-10 minutes. So, passing locale as a query parameter (`?lang=en`, `?lang=ca`, etc) probably won't work as the cached results should be different for different locales.
* It would be great to have a language-specific `Cache-Control` if there is a way of sending the language somehow in the request to `getServerSideProps`.


## What I have done before
* My app was written entirely in React and I'm now moving to Next.js. I used to pass the locale in a URL query parameter like `?lang=en`. I would then store that locale in the `localStorage`, send API requests with the given locale, and change the app's appearance accordingly.
* That used to work fine, but now I want to make use of the server-side rendering and request caching, which makes things more complicated.

-----------

This seems to be a common use case. Is there any way to handle the internationalization without explicitly changing the URL? Or maybe having one URL at first and then hiding some parts after? Any kind of idea/workaround would be really appreciated!

title: Deployment "Automatic Updates" broken?
body:
Hi,

We've got a NextJS site where [Automatic Updates](https://nextjs.org/docs/deployment#automatic-updates) aren't working. Are there caveats to when this works and when it doesn't?

[Our site](https://dev.blues.io) is deployed by [exporting](https://nextjs.org/docs/advanced-features/static-html-export) to AWS S3 for HTTP and Cloudfront as a CDN. We are using `<Link>`. Unfortunately and mysteriously, navigating around the site doesn't ever force a full-page reload (like a vanilla `<a>` would) when a deploy definitely happened in the background. Opening a new browser tab will show the latest site but an old tab seems to be stuck in the past.

What's the mechanism for detecting a page reload needs to happen? Does it work off the [buildId](https://nextjs.org/docs/api-reference/next.config.js/configuring-the-build-id)? Wondering what network traffic I might spy on to debug this.

Thanks

title: How loading from Git LFS (Large File Storage) might work in Next.js on Vercel?
body:
I am considering building an app which uses Git LFS (which costs about $5 per 50GB on GitHub) to store let's say a terabyte of images/audios/videos/etc. (which would be 20 50GB packs so $100/mo, or at $0.023/GB on S3, about $23 there). In addition to the terabyte of "large files", the repo itself might grow up to several Gigabytes (let's say 100GB, the max GitHub allows).

If the 100GB of "JSON data" is in the form of JSON, YAML, CSV, and JavaScript files, and gets compiled to memory at runtime into a big Map or something of JSON objects, that would take a long time to "load" all that structured data into JavaScript objects, probably several minutes if not longer.

So I am wondering how a deploy would work/look in this scenario on Vercel with Next.js. Would a push to the repo download the diff of all the terabyte of binary files, plus the diff of the 100GB of structured data, and then load it up somehow, and only after it is loaded flip a switch to the new live app? Or how does it work (or is it even possible).

The 100GB of structured data in theory would be then rendered into the Next.js app, such as through the /api folder, but just reading in-memory JSON structures.

I guess so far, first, 100GB won't fit into the 1GB memory max allowed on Vercel.

title: Is it possible to use public CDN cache for not-authenticated users and use private cache for authenticated users for the same route

## My use case
* I use firebase authentication: https://github.com/gladly-team/next-firebase-auth
* When accessing the homepage, I'd like to:
    * Show the landing page for users that are not authenticated
    * Show the dashboard to users that have been authenticated.
* The landing page basically doesn't change - so it would be very helpful to cache it to reduce redundant requests to the server.
* The dashboard is specific to each user - so it can be both rendered on the server-side and on the client-side.

Any advice, workaround, tip, or suggestion would be really appreciated!

title: How do you run TypeScript files used in a Next.js project, outside of Next.js?
body:
I have right now a project with this directory structure:

```
components
configurations
contents
contexts
displays
hooks
pages
processors
public
types
utils
```

In the `utils` folder, I have a bunch of basic TypeScript utils, which get used (amongst other places) in the `components` React components. I also use `utils` and to generate content in the `contents` folder, which can (should) be run in a basic Node.js process. Similarly, I have a `types` folder which has some type definitions used in both Next.js frontend and Node.js processes. And I have `processors` which are data processing scripts, also in TypeScript which should run as Node.js processes outside of Next.js. In another project I had `models` and `tests` folders, where the models connected through Knex.js to the database, and I ran those `tests` using Mocha, against the model (all outside Next.js functionality).

Currently (and for the past few years, on personal projects), I have written the utils/processors/types/etc. in plain JS, not using `import x from 'x'` but `const x = require('x')`, and doing otherwise basic Node-land conventions. I have tinkered with `babel-node` and some other typescript projects like `ts-node` to try and compile the TypeScript and run it in the plain Node.js process, but I always run into complexities/problems when starting a new project, I can't remember what the last problems I faced were, it's been a while since I've tried with TypeScript using it in a Next.js overall project, but running in a plain Node.js project. I have been out of the loop for a little while on what the latest techniques or best practices are in TypeScript land, and how to get my desired workflow working. The desired workflow is to be able to run the **TypeScript** modules (with `import`, etc.), as a Node.js process, and otherwise reuse any of the common/shared code that is also used by the Next.js app.

How can I accomplish that in a straightforward way? What tools do I need to add to my `package.json`? What about for any of the "config" files? What is the idiomatic "Next.js way" of doing this? I can piece together a new "TypeScript compiling Node.js-module workflow", but I would read several blog posts and try out a few projects before landing on something, and it might not be the recommended approach for dealing with this in the Next.js TypeScript project. So wondering if one could point me in the right direction to skip a few steps.

I am getting this when running a script in ts-node.

```
import fs from 'fs';
^^^^^^

SyntaxError: Cannot use import statement outside a module
```

Changing `esnext` to be `"module": "commonjs"` allows the script to run locally, but I'm sure this is not the right approach.

title: How to return 404 page from api handler?
body:
By default, all non-existing paths (including frontend and API) return the default 404 Next.js page.
It can be customized in `pages/404.js` file.

It's possible to return that default/customized error page from `getServerSideProps` by returning
```
return {
  notFound: true,
}
```
https://nextjs.org/docs/api-reference/data-fetching/get-server-side-props#notfound

But what about API handlers?

Response helper has no `error` method. The body has to be sent manually and JSX obviously doesn't work there.
https://nextjs.org/docs/api-routes/response-helpers

I thought about
```
res.status(404).send("copied and pasted HTML code from JSX 404 error page")
```
but it's not an ideal solution...

How to send the default Nextjs error page as a response in API handler?

title: How to automatically create shortlinks for content coming from CMS?
body:
I want to generate shortlinks for my blog posts coming from a CMS (headless WordPress).

Currently, my blog URLs are like `site.com/blog/[slug]`. What I want is some short url like `site.com/b/[id]`, `site.com/?b=[id]`, `site.com/blog/?id=[id]` to redirect (or rewrite) to `site.com/blog/[slug]`.

## Example:
Assume every post is like this:
```
post: {
    id: "125",
    slug: "how-to-do-this",
    title: "How to do this?"
    ...
}
```
One of these (or a similar URL format):
```
site.com/b/125
site.com/?b=125
site.com/blog/?id=125
```
should redirect/rewrite to:
```
site.com/blog/how-to-do-this
```

## Problem:
1 - I don't know where to define these rules. `.htaccess` is an option, but I want it to be done automatically.

2 - I don't know what is better for SEO. Should it be a rewrite, a redirect, or maybe a permanent redirect?

## Ideal Solution
Something like this:
```js
module.exports = {
  async rewrites() {
    return [
      {
        source: '/b/:id',
        destination: `/blog/${getSlugById(:id)}`,
      },
    ]
  },
}
```
author: ShahriarKh

title: Docker best practice + Nextjs + environment variables
body:
Hello,

A while back (https://github.com/vercel/next.js/discussions/16995#discussioncomment-2514009) I joined a discussion on how to set up Nextjs with Docker best practice and the discussion never really found a good solution. We manage to run docker best practice with environment variables in run time, for build time we use fallback variables. I just want to share what we ended up with and hear what you think of this approach.

Environment:
* Docker
* Nextjs
* Kubenetes
* Azure devops
* Azure library (holds our environment variables)

Requirements:
* Docker "Best practice" - During the deploy process the image is built **only once** and then re-deployed through the steps so we ensure that the same code is passed from environments dev -> test -> acc -> prod.
* Environment variables - For each different environment we have diffrent setups for our environment variables. Like passwords and connection strings etc

**How**
1. We do not use the standard nextjs way for variables like .env files or next.config.js. The reason why we did this was that we could not get our variables from azure library to overwrite the variables that were in the .env files in the environments. Instead we created a custom config.ts located in our /lib folder. 
config.ts:
```
const serverEnvironmentVariables: ServerConfigVariables = {
  baseUrl: process.env.BaseUrl || "https://local/",
  ...
};


const clientEnvironmentVariables: ClientConfigVariables = {
  baseUrl: process.env.BaseUrl || "https://local/",
  ...
};
```

In local enviroment or during the build step for deploy the fallback is used. We split the variables that should be only available on the server or client. The reason why is that we do not want our secrets to be bundeld into the client app.js code. We also have tests to ensure that no secrets have been included. 

2.
During deploy and image is built once in azure pipeline, but during the deploy step to kubenetes the environment variables stored in azure library is included. So when the app starts it will now use the `process.env.BaseUrl ` for that specific environment.
config.map.yaml

```
apiVersion: v1
kind: ConfigMap
metadata:
  name: "{{ .Values.environment }}-{{ .Chart.Name }}-{{ .Chart.Version }}"

data:
    Baseurl: "{{ .Values.config.Baseurl }}"

```
values.yaml
```
...
config:
  Baseurl: "#{Baseurl}#"
```
 3. When deplyoing this to the next stage in the deploy process, for example testing environment. The same image is deployed but this time with azure library variables for the testing environment. And so on.
 
 
 Now, this works well for us. We don't have anything specific at the moment that needs to be built a special way depending on the environment. My main concern is if someone by mistake includes our "serverEnvironmentVariables" in client facing code so that the secrets is included in the app.js bundle and the test and code reviews do not pick it up.
 
 Let me hear what you think of this approach? Maybe something has changed since we created this solution to do this in a more secure way.

Seeing this:

info  - Disabled SWC as replacement for Babel because of custom Babel configuration ".babelrc" https://nextjs.org/docs/messages/swc-disabled
event - compiled client and server successfully in 964 ms (507 modules)
wait  - compiling...
info  - Using experimental wasm build of next-swc

event - compiled client and server successfully in 43.1s (507 modules)
```
...when starting up with `npm run dev`. (we run Next within an Express app)

In particular, why are there 2 lines of...
```
event - compiled client and server successfully in 964 ms (507 modules)
```
and
```
event - compiled client and server successfully in 43.1s (507 modules)

It's a bit of a concern of ours that it's taking 43.1s seconds for the second one which might be a mixture of upgrading to 12.2.5 from 12.2.4 but I also very much suspect one of your `pages/**/*.tsx` files does something crazy when it drags in some Node import which is needed by the `getServerSideProps`.

When there is a link within the NextJS app that refers to a page in Sitecore, the NextJS app router tries to find it within NextJS, then throws a 404. To circumvent that I've added the attributes (rel="noreferrer" and target="_blank") to the offending links but it feels awfully hacky. Is there a more elegant solution for that where by if the page is not found in NextJS it forwards the request to the Sitecore app?

Thanks!

title: Seemingly random 404s on SSG
body:
I have a weird issue I'm debugging. Looking to see if anyone else has this problem.

We run 2 instances of NextJS on AWS behind a loadbalancer. After an unknown amount of time, one of the instances starts giving 404s for the url: `/_next/data/{build-id}/en.json` (en being the locale). There's 1 codepath on this page that can return a 404 and there we set revalidate to 1.

The weird thing is that the cache-control header is set to: `s-maxage=31536000, stale-while-revalidate`. When returning the 404. The other instance happily returns a 200 with the data.

This breaks our homepage periodically. We're using Next `12.2.1`. I've been unable to reliably reproduce because it happens seemingly randomly. The logs don't indicate any problems.

Questions:

- Is there a way to log more information about static generation? I'd like to see whether a page is being regenerated or not, and what the status was.
- Could this be conflicting: We have an index.ts as well as a [slug].ts. In the chrome network logs I sometimes see this call being made: `/_next/data/{build-id}/en.json?slug=en` which seems to suggest it's also being recognized as a dynamic route. But when you manually call that endpoint you get the normal data

title: `next dev` rewrites `tsconfig.json` when it shouldn't
body:
### Verify canary release

- [X] I verified that the issue exists in the latest Next.js canary release

### Provide environment information

```
    Operating System:
      Platform: win32
      Arch: x64
      Version: Windows 10 Enterprise
    Binaries:
      Node: 16.15.1
      npm: N/A
      Yarn: N/A
      pnpm: N/A
    Relevant packages:
      next: 12.2.6-canary.4
      eslint-config-next: N/A
      react: 18.2.0
      react-dom: 18.2.0
```


### What browser are you using? (if relevant)

_No response_

### How are you deploying your application? (if relevant)

_No response_

### Describe the Bug

Next.js rewrites `tsconfig.json` even when it doesn't need to. In the reproduction repo it's adding `"exclude": ["node_modules"]`, even though `node_modules` directory is covered by the default of `exclude` (see https://www.typescriptlang.org/tsconfig#exclude). There are other similar situations when Next.js thinks it's smarter than me and changes `tsconfig.json`, but they are not included in the reproduction repo because it is not relevant to the proposed solution.

Also, when making those edits, Next.js doesn't respect Prettier config.

This is `tsconfig.json` from the reproduction repo:
```json
{
  "compilerOptions": {
    "target": "es5",
    "lib": ["dom", "dom.iterable", "esnext"],
    "allowJs": true,
    "skipLibCheck": true,
    "strict": true,
    "forceConsistentCasingInFileNames": true,
    "noEmit": true,
    "incremental": true,
    "esModuleInterop": true,
    "module": "esnext",
    "moduleResolution": "node",
    "resolveJsonModule": true,
    "isolatedModules": true,
    "jsx": "preserve"
  },
  "include": ["next-env.d.ts", "**/*.ts", "**/*.tsx"]
}
```

This is `tsconfig.json` after following the steps to reproduce the bug:
```json
{
  "compilerOptions": {
    "target": "es5",
    "lib": [
      "dom",
      "dom.iterable",
      "esnext"
    ],
    "allowJs": true,
    "skipLibCheck": true,
    "strict": true,
    "forceConsistentCasingInFileNames": true,
    "noEmit": true,
    "incremental": true,
    "esModuleInterop": true,
    "module": "esnext",
    "moduleResolution": "node",
    "resolveJsonModule": true,
    "isolatedModules": true,
    "jsx": "preserve"
  },
  "include": [
    "next-env.d.ts",
    "**/*.ts",
    "**/*.tsx"
  ],
  "exclude": [
    "node_modules"
  ]
}
```

### Expected Behavior

Next.js **never** touches `tsconfig.json`. It can print warnings, but I should be able to turn them off.

### Link to reproduction

https://github.com/jakubmazanec/nextjs-tsconfig-bug

### To Reproduce

1) Clone https://github.com/jakubmazanec/nextjs-tsconfig-bug
2) Run `npm install`
3) Run `npm run dev`
4) Observe changed `tsconfig.json`

title: Link getServerSideProps prefetch [onmousedown]
body:
### Describe the feature you'd like to request

Requesting pages that require any API requests (`getServerSideProps`) before render may last for about ~170ms, so user have to wait for request to finish before transition happens. This long transition requests is visible and makes your app look slow

### Average click durations (from `onmousedown` to `onclick` events):
#### Fast (almost instant) ~50ms
#### Normal ~100ms
#### Slow ~150ms
****
By average we can make page transitions **>2X faster** by prefetching `getServerSideProps` when `onmousedown` event was fired
### You can test your click times by using this sample html:
```html
<!DOCTYPE html>
<html>
  <body>
    <button id="test" onmousedown="onDown()" onclick="onClick()">Test</button>

    <script>
      let time;

      function onDown() {
        time = Date.now();
      }

      function onClick() {
        console.log(`clicked in: ${Date.now() - time}ms`);
      }
    </script>
  </body>
</html>
```

### Describe the solution you'd like

Prefetching page when `onmousedown` event was fired

### Describe alternatives you've considered

-

title: Why don't advanced static generation features work with custom servers?
body:
I'm working at a company that uses NextJS for several application builds. Our architecture uses a custom server for several reasons. First, we migrated to NextJS from an application that was built with a node server package, and keeping our old server logic around made the migration easier. Second, the interface to the NextJS server has changed frequently over the past few years, and the existing mix of documentation can be very difficult to parse. the last and largest reason we've avoided the NextJS server is that it is new. There is an existing Node server interface paradigm that anyone who has built one will recognize, centering on the context object and modifications to req and res. NextJS is built on top of that paradigm, but inserts its own set of interfaces on top, which can be extremely limiting in some cases. The redirect configuration is a good example. There is an existing, well documented way to handle redirects in most Node servers that NextJS replaces entirely, and the replacement a significant step down, feature-wise, from the thing it replaces. It seems to me that NextJS has decided to re-invent the wheel here, which is totally fine as it optimizes the interface for a class of user, except that some of the core features of NextJS are gated behind it.

Basically what I'm saying here is that if you are building a fresh infrastructure, and starting out from scratch with NextJS this all probably works fine for you. If you're dealing with an existing infrastructure and trying to make everything work together, you're almost certainly going to run into some challenging problems that have documented solutions for common Node servers, but if you're using NextJS's built in server you are out of luck.

So having said all of that, I would add that I've built and supported an application that implemented SSR without NextJS, so I'm familiar in with how one renders a React application server side, and how one might write the rendered string to disk as an HTML page. I'm not sure what about the NextJS server specifically permits this feature set, and why it can't be achieved with a custom server. From my perspective, trying to integrate NextJS into existing architectures and support complex migrations, the more modular a software package is, the more powerful and useful it is. The React ecosystem is generally constructed with modularity in mind, and NextJS seems like a significant departure from this. I'm curious what the genesis of this approach is. Are there technical limitations I'm not aware of? What is driving the push towards the adoption of the built-in server, and why are the Node-standard features hidden and inaccessible behind a novel interface? Can future versions of NextJS support standard interfaces and offer more modularity, or should users like me be looking elsewhere?

title: New router utility functions for picking the first or all query parameter values
body:
I see a lot of times Next.js users mistakingly type-cast the value of `router.query.searchQueryName` to satisfy TypeScript:

```ts
const id = useRouter().query.id as string;
```

`ParsedUrlQuery` is correctly typed to have values that are either `string` or `string[]`. Because a URL like `https://example.com?searchQueryName=123&searchQueryName=456` is valid and if you using the standard URL APIs you can get both values:


```ts
const url = new URL("https://example.com?searchQueryName=123&searchQueryName=456");
url.searchParams.get("searchQueryName"); //  "123"
url.searchParams.getAll("searchQueryName"); // ["123", "456"]
```

The `URLSearchParams` API returns the first query param when calling the `get()` method. 


### `pickFirst`
In our codebase we have a function called `pickFirst` to go around this type-cast situation:


```ts
function pickFirst(queryParam: string | string[] | undefined) {
  if (Array.isArray(queryParam)) {
    return queryParam[0];
  }
  return queryParam;
}
```


```ts
// const id = useRouter().query.id as string;
const id = pickFirst(useRouter().query.id); // type string | undefined
```


## Suggestion
put the `pickFirst` logic into the router:

```ts
const id = useRouter().getQuery("id"); // type string | undefined
const toppings = useRouter().getQueryAll("topping"); // type string[] | undefined
```

### Goals
* use the same terminology as `URLSearchParams`
* do not break existing API 
* recommend using this new API in the docs instead of `router.query`

title: Which redirect approach to use when?
body:
There are multiple ways to redirect from one page to another. Say we have a `/home` page where we want our `/` route to redirect to. There are a few options:

1. `getServerSideProps` in the index.js file in pages folder.
2. Using redirect in middleware using NextResponse
3. Using next.config.js redirects


Using the third approach complex logic can't be used. But in simple cases as above, which approach should be used when?

title: useEffect order of execution in _app.tsx
body:
Hi,

I have a question about the order of execution in `_app.tsx`.

According to this [post](https://blog.logrocket.com/post-hooks-guide-react-call-order/), react executes the `useEffect`s from the child component up to the parent component.

However, when I run `useEffect` from `_app.tsx`, `PageComponent`, `SomeComponentInPageComponent`, the order of execution is `_app.tsx` -> `SomeComponentInPageComponent` -> `PageComponent`.

(What I expected was `SomeComponentInPageComponent` -> `PageComponent` -> `_app.tsx`)

It seems like `_app.tsx` is an exception to the order of execution rule, and I would like to know the reason (if there is one) or the cause of such behavior. (It's just out of curiosity)

Thanks,
Son

title: What does "disable Automatic Static Optimization in pages without Static Generation" mean?
body:
In the caveats of implementing `getInitialProps` on custom `App`, we get:

https://nextjs.org/docs/advanced-features/custom-app#caveats

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/43407798/187372393-d11a386e-2555-4276-a54c-55ea88e9f8cf.png)

DIsabling _Automatic Static Optimization_, means that my page will be server side rendered, instead of statically rendered during build, right?

But it says that it'll only happen for pages _without Static Generation_.

That's where it loses me a little.

This is what it says about _Static Generation_

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/43407798/187372924-65eadaa6-b146-4223-ba72-c6b8bff90b6e.png)

Am I right assuming the following:

- I implement `getInitialProps` on my custom `App`
- Assume `PageA` doesn't implement `getServerSideProps` and `getInitialProps`
- That would disable static generation on my `PageA` (because of the caveat).
- But if `PageA` explicitly implements `getStaticProps`
- It will still be statically built (pre-rendered during build). So it basically reverts the caveat?

Is that correct?

title: Is server-side caching possible?
body:
Apologies, I couldn't find a thread similar to what I'm asking, although I'm sure it's been asked.  I have to questions:

1. I have a collection of pages, some using `getStaticProps` and some using `getServerSideProps`. All of these pages share the same `Layout` component and therefore the same `Header` component. 

If I have to call a series of 3 requests on the server, where all 3 requests are used to populate the `Header`, is there a recommended way to call this only one time? 

For example, if I visit 5 different pages that are all using `getServerSideProps`, currently it seems like those 3 requests need to happen each time, even though they are the same data (I understand it doesn't matter much with `getStaticProps` though).

An idea I've seen is to take those initial 3 queries, call them once, and write them to the file system (nice if the data isn't dynamic). 

2. Is it possible to cache a request response on the server for different users when using `getServerSideProps`? 

I've looked at `react-query`, but it seems like the calls need to happen regardless each time `getServerSideProps` runs, although applying a key can help provide frontend requests with already-fetched server-side data. However, I don't think it can cache the same data on the server.

Another example might be a shop with thousands of products. On each product page when you fetch the product data, it would be nice to cache the data across multiple users (Just the query, as opposed to caching the whole page via the `Cache-Control` header).

1)  Next.config.js
```

  3)  In my React component that is used on one of the pages, I have
 ```

  import homeIntroImg from '../../../public/assets/images/homeIntro.png';

            src={homeIntroImg}

  ```
"scripts": {
    "dev": "next dev",
    "build": "next build",
    "start": "NEXT_SHARP_PATH=/usr/src/app/node_modules/sharp next start ",
  
```

5. Dockerfile.  Due to the internal build pipeline system, we cannot perform 'npm install' in Docker image. "npm install" & "next build' are performed outside and all the files including dist, node_modules directories are copied to this image.
```
FROM base-image-node-16.0:latest AS runner
WORKDIR /usr/src/app

ENV NODE_ENV production

USER 2020

On local when i try to build docker container,

Warning: For production Image Optimization with Next.js, the optional 'sharp' package is strongly recommended. Run 'yarn add sharp', and Next.js will use it automatically for Image Optimization.
but images load fine.

When the same docker image is deployed to cluster,

```
Warning: For production Image Optimization with Next.js, the optional 'sharp' package is strongly recommended. Run 'yarn add sharp', and Next.js will use it automatically for Image Optimization.

Am I missing anything? Any help is greatly appreciated.

title: next/image: expose the loaders
body:
### Describe the feature you'd like to request

My project mostly uses imgix, but in some cases I want to import static images. I want to be able to use one loader for some image instances, and another for others.

I can do this if I write a custom loader for some of the images, but the built-in loaders would serve my needs so I'd rather use those.

I'm not able to use more than one built-in loader at a time, however, because they're not exported. As far as I can tell, I can only choose them via the `next.config.js` configuration file.

### Describe the solution you'd like

Export each of the loaders as named exports. This way, I can override the configured loader via the `loader` prop on a case-by-case basis. Perhaps this would also allow a custom loader to fall back to a built-in one under particular circumstances.

Alternatively, allow a string to be passed to the `loader` prop matching an entry in the `loaders` array of the image.js source, to override the configured loader with that particular built-in.

### Describe alternatives you've considered

- Reimplementing (or copy-pasting) one or the other of the imgix or default loader.
- Not doing any static imports so I can just use a single loader.
- Opting out of optimization for the images I want to statically import.

Any help is appreciated :)

title: Docker + Custom Server + HTTPS
body:
Hello,

I am looking for feedback on what I am trying to currently do for a prod deployment.

A few of the things I am trying to do are as follows.
- Create a Docker image
- Run the next server using https, my assumption is this can only be done with a custom server.
- The application will be running behind an Apache reverse proxy with a `basePath`

I have created a docker image using the `with-docker` example, which works flawlessly, and I am able to serve it successfully behind the Apache reverse proxy with the correct base path.  I am able to accomplish this using `output: 'standalone'` in the `next.config.js`. title: Why is a JSON file generated when calling getStaticProps in Next.js?
body:
I've been reading through the [Next.js docs][1] and came across this:

> Next.js generates a JSON file holding the result of running `getStaticProps`. The JSON file will be used in client-side routing through `next/link` or `next/router`. When you navigate to a page that's pre-rendered using `getStaticProps`, Next.js fetches this JSON file (pre-computed at build time) and uses it as the props for the page component.

My question is, why is this JSON file needed? If the page is rendered into static HTML at build time using the props provided by `getStaticProps`, why does the page component exist at runtime and needs access to those props?

[1]: https://nextjs.org/docs/basic-features/data-fetching/get-static-props#statically-generates-both-html-and-json

title: Override the Default API Routes folder (/pages/api) so that actual pages can be hosted at that route
body:
### Describe the feature you'd like to request

Currently the /pages/api route is reserved for Next API Routes. If I want to host pages on my app at domain/api/page-path, I need a workaround.

Rewrites is a possible workaround, but I am using next export, so it won't work.

exportPathMap is another potential workaround, but I have hundreds of pages hosted at domain/api/page-path because I have a file /pages/api/[[...slug]].tsx that leverages getStaticPaths and creates hundreds of pages.

I'd like a way to override the Next API Routes default path so that my /pages/api/[[...slug]].tsx file gets treated as any other file at a non-reserved route.

### Describe the solution you'd like

A config value in next.config.js that allows me to either turn off API Routes or change the default folder for them so that pages/api/page-file gets treated as other page files.

### Describe alternatives you've considered

rewrites, but that doesn't work because I'm using next export.

exportPathMap, but that's unsustainable because I have hundreds of pages in the app.

title: Who's hiring (Sep 2022)
body:
# What is this?
New month, new post! This discussion is for companies looking for people with Next.js experience. Both permanent positions and contract work are welcome.

# Companies
Please use the following format followed by a brief description of the role:

> COMPANY_NAME | ROLE | LOCATION | Full-time | $100-150k base | LINK

Please only post if you personally are part of the hiring company—no recruiting firms or job boards. Only one post per company.

Good luck with recruiting!

## Sample Job Post

Here's a good example job post.

> Vercel | Senior Full-Stack Engineer | Remote (US, Canada, Europe, or Asia) | Full-time | $100-225k base | [Link](https://vercel.com/careers/senior-full-stack-engineer-amer)
>
> Vercel’s hiring across the board on various engineering teams and positions, but the above link is for our full-stack engineering position. Vercel is innovating on and for the web and at the edge. We build tools and workflows developers love and deliver outstanding performance for end users.
>
> I've been at the company for 2 years and still find plenty of opportunity for impact, and love the teammates I'm working with. Feel free to apply using the link above or reach out to me directly.

---

If you hire someone through this job board, please reach out to me (hassan@vercel.com) and let me know so I can send you some swag :)

If you're a developer looking to post about themselves, use #40172.

*If you're looking for last month's discussion, see #39273. At least one company has [hired someone](https://twitter.com/Nutlope/status/1475929984174895105) as a result of it!*
